> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When a spur length exceeds $0.30 B$, it begins to act as a **groin across the entire width** ŌĆö its function changes from training to partial closure. Avoid this in design.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö When to Use Which Training Work

| Site Condition | Recommended Training Work | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Wide floodplain, frequent flooding | **Marginal Embankments (Levees)** | Confine high discharge economically |
| Erosion at convex bank of bend | **Repelling Spurs / Revetment** | Push flow away from eroding bank |
| Severe meandering, active cut-bank | **Cut-off + Guide Bunds** | Shorten path, fix alignment |
| Bridge approach in wide floodplain | **Guide Bunds** | Funnel flow through bridge waterway |
| Bridge pier on sand bed | **Pitched Island + Flexible Apron** | Prevent scour-induced pier failure |
| Hilly river, boulder bed, permeable strata | **Hollow Permeable Spurs** | Induce siltation, cost-effective |
| Navigable river, low-flow depth inadequate | **Bed Bars + Dredging** | Maintain navigable depth |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification of River Training Works (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[RTW: River Training Works]:::root
    A --> B[By Purpose]:::grp
    A --> C[By Function]:::grp
    A --> D[By Component Type]:::grp

    B --> B1[High Flood Training]
    B --> B2[Mean Flow Training]
    B --> B3[Low Water Training]

    C --> C1[Longitudinal Works]
    C --> C2[Transverse Works]
    C --> C3[Bank Protection Works]
    C --> C4[Channel Improvement Works]

    C1 --> C11[Marginal Embankments]
    C1 --> C12[Guide Bunds]

    C2 --> C21[Impermeable Spurs]
    C2 --> C22[Permeable Spurs]
    C2 --> C23[Bed Bars]

    C3 --> C31[Pitching Revetment]
    C3 --> C32[Flexible Apron]
    C3 --> C33[Vegetative Cover]

    C4 --> C41[Cut Offs]
    C4 --> C42[Dredging]

    C21 --> S1[Attracting Spur]
    C21 --> S2[Deflecting Spur]
    C21 --> S3[Repelling Spur]
    C21 --> S4[T-Head Spur]
    C21 --> S5[Kantal Spur]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef grp fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram above is the **functional architecture** of all training works taught in KTU Module 2. It is the standard map used for 14-marker classification questions.

## 4.2 Plan of a Typical Spur / Groyne (Mermaid Block View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Bank - Tail of Spur]:::bank
    B[Shank Body]:::body
    C[Head Nose Under Scour]:::head
    D[Flexible Apron Around Head]:::apron
    E[Flow Direction Arrow]:::flow

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    E -.-> C

    classDef bank fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000
    classDef body fill:#d6d6d6,stroke:#000
    classDef head fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef apron fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000
    classDef flow fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Engineering Reading of the Block Architecture:**

| Block | Real-World Element | Design Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Bank** | Natural river bank | Must be tied properly to prevent flanking |
| **Shank** | Main straight trunk | Sized for slope protection and stone weight |
| **Head** | Riverward tip | Subject to **maximum scour** ŌåÆ deepest foundation |
| **Apron** | Stone cover around head | Length $\geq 1.5 \times D$ to prevent progressive scour failure |
| **Flow arrow** | Mean flow direction | Spur angle is measured from this direction |

## 4.3 Plan of a Guide Bund System at a Bridge (Mermaid Block View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    U[Upstream Reach]:::up
    G1[Curved Head - Elliptical]:::curve
    G2[Straight Shank Parallel to Flow]:::shank
    G3[Tail - Anchored to Floodplain]:::tail
    BR[Bridge Waterway]:::br
    GR1[Guide Bund Right Side]:::gb
    GR2[Guide Bund Left Side]:::gb
    AF[Afflux Rise in Water Level]:::afflux

    U --> G1 --> G2 --> G3
    G2 --> BR
    GR1 -.-> G2
    GR2 -.-> G2
    AF -.-> G1

    classDef up fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000
    classDef curve fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000
    classDef shank fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000
    classDef tail fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000
    classDef br fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef gb fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef afflux fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000
```

**Notes on Each Block:**

- **Curved Head (Elliptical):** Smoothly guides flow into the bridge waterway; the major axis is typically **1.5 to 2.0 times the minor axis**.
- **Straight Shank:** Length depends on the **anticipated meander width** ŌĆö long enough to prevent flanking.
- **Tail:** Anchored well into the high-flood-line with a deep key trench.
- **Afflux (Golden Block):** Upstream rise in water level due to constriction ŌĆö **must be estimated and accounted for in design** of the embankment freeboard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Marker Question:** *"Sketch a plan of a guide bund and label its parts."* Use the block structure above as your reference.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]**
*Define river training. List any four objectives of river training works.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** River training is the process of guiding and confining a river to a planned alignment and cross-section by means of engineering structures, so as to make the river flow safe, predictable, and useful.

**Four Objectives (any four):**

1. To provide safe passage for floods without damage to banks and property.
2. To protect river banks from erosion.
3. To improve navigability of the river.
4. To protect important hydraulic structures (bridges, barrages) from scour and damage.
5. To reclaim and protect marginal lands.
6. To stabilize the river regime.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "controlling floods" as a vague answer. Always specify **"confining the river to a designed cross-section"** ŌĆö the word *confining* is the KTU keyword.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]**
*Differentiate between permeable and impermeable spurs. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Permeable Spur | Impermeable Spur |
|---|---|---|
| Material | Piles, brushwood, trees, gabions | Stone, masonry, concrete |
| Flow passage | Allows slow seepage | Total blockage near bank |
| Function | Induces silt deposition, dissipates energy | Deflects flow away from bank |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Example | Hollow spur (brushwood) | Rubble-mound masonry spur |

> [!NOTE]
> Permeable spurs are preferred in **Himalayan boulder-bed rivers** (Kosi, Gandak). Impermeable spurs are preferred in **Indo-Gangetic alluvial plains**.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Q.A. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** *Classify river training works in detail, with neat sketches of different types of spurs.* **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** *A river has a dominant discharge of 4500 cumecs and a silt factor of 1.2. Determine (i) Lacey's regime width, (ii) regime hydraulic mean depth, (iii) normal scour depth, and (iv) design scour depth assuming a factor of safety of 1.5.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Classification of RTW:**

*Functional classification* (as in the diagram of Section 4.1) with sketches:

1. **Marginal Embankments:** Longitudinal embankments parallel to flow.
2. **Guide Bunds:** Curved embankments at bridge approaches.
3. **Spurs / Groynes:** Transverse structures. Sub-types:
   - *Repelling* (perpendicular, shank at 90┬░)
   - *Attracting* (angled upstream 10┬░ŌĆō30┬░)
   - *Deflecting* (angled downstream 15┬░ŌĆō30┬░)
   - *T-Head* (perpendicular shank with T-head parallel to flow)
   - *Kantal* (hockey-shaped ŌĆö combination of attracting and deflecting)
4. **Revetments / Pitching:** Surface protection on banks.
5. **Cut-offs:** Artificial channels shortening river length.
6. **Dredging:** Excavation to increase depth.
7. **Bed Bars:** Low structures to control bed level.

*(Neat sketches showing plan of each spur type with the bank on the left and flow arrow pointing rightward.)*

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical:**

Given: $Q = 4500$ cumecs, $f = 1.2$, $F_s = 1.5$.

**(i) Regime width:**

$$
P = 4.75 \sqrt{4500} = 4.75 \times 67.08 = 318.6 \ \text{m}
$$

**(ii) Regime hydraulic mean depth:**

$$
R = 0.473 \left(\frac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3} = 0.473 \left(\frac{4500}{1.2}\right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
\frac{4500}{1.2} = 3750
$$

$$
(3750)^{1/3} = 15.54
$$

$$
R = 0.473 \times 15.54 = 7.35 \ \text{m}
$$

**(iii) Discharge per unit width:**

$$
q = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{4500}{318.6} = 14.12 \ \text{cumecs/m}
$$

**Normal scour depth:**

$$
D = 1.35 \cdot \frac{q^{2/3}}{f^{1/3}} = 1.35 \cdot \frac{(14.12)^{2/3}}{(1.2)^{1/3}}
$$

Compute $(14.12)^{2/3}$:
- $\ln(14.12) = 2.647$
- $\frac{2}{3} \times 2.647 = 1.765$
- $e^{1.765} = 5.84$

Compute $(1.2)^{1/3}$:
- $e^{(1/3)\ln(1.2)} = e^{0.0607} = 1.063$

Therefore:

$$
D = 1.35 \times \frac{5.84}{1.063} = 1.35 \times 5.495 = 7.42 \ \text{m}
$$

**(iv) Design scour depth:**

$$
D_s = 1.5 \times D = 1.5 \times 7.42 = 11.13 \ \text{m}
$$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Regime width $P$ | 318.6 m |
| Regime hydraulic mean depth $R$ | 7.35 m |
| Normal scour depth $D$ | 7.42 m |
| Design scour depth $D_s$ | 11.13 m |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Part (b):**
> 1. Students often forget to compute $q = Q/P$ before applying the scour formula. **Always find $q$ first.**
> 2. Some students use $R$ in place of $D$ in the scour formula. They are **different quantities**: $R$ is the hydraulic mean depth, $D$ is the maximum scour depth below HFL.
> 3. Forgetting the factor of safety $F_s$ is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.

---

### Q.B. **[Alternative Choice, 14 Marks]**
**(a)** *Explain with neat sketches the various types of spurs used in river training. Discuss the criteria for selection of spur type based on site conditions.* **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** *Describe the design of a guide bund system at a bridge crossing. What is afflux? How is it estimated using Bradley's method?* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**Part (a) ŌĆö Spurs:**

- Define spur, classify by permeability (permeable vs impermeable), and by alignment (attracting, deflecting, repelling, T-head, kantal).
- **Sketch each type** in plan, indicating bank, shank, head, flow direction, and angle of inclination.
- **Selection criteria:**
  - Sharp bend with severe erosion ŌåÆ **T-head spur**
  - Gentle meander, bank protection ŌåÆ **Repelling spur**
  - Need to attract flow into a channel ŌåÆ **Attracting spur**
  - Combination of erosion + deposition zones ŌåÆ **Kantal spur**
  - Hilly river with permeable strata ŌåÆ **Hollow permeable spur**

**Part (b) ŌĆö Guide Bunds:**

- Definition: massive embankments built on either side of a river at a bridge.
- **Components** (as in Section 4.3 block diagram): curved head, straight shank, tail, pitched slope.
- **Function:** funnel the entire design flood through the bridge waterway; prevent avulsion.
- **Afflux** = rise in upstream water level due to flow constriction.
- **Bradley's method:**
  - Compute $M = Q_b / Q_{total}$.
  - Estimate approach velocity $V$.
  - Use $h_a \approx 0.05 \cdot V^2 \cdot (1/M^2 - 1)$.
- The freeboard of the guide bund must exceed HFL + $h_a$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Area in Q.B:**
> 1. Not explaining the **elliptical shape** of the upstream head ŌĆö examiners expect a 1-mark mention of "elliptical plan, major axis $\approx 2 \times$ minor axis."
> 2. Confusing **afflux with backwater** ŌĆö afflux is a *local* rise at the bridge, backwater is a *profile* effect.
> 3. Skipping the freeboard check: a guide bund whose top is below HFL + $h_a$ will overtop and fail.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE ŌĆö River Training Works (Module 2)**

### Key Definitions
- **River Training Works:** Engineering structures to confine a river to a planned cross-section and alignment.
- **Afflux:** Local rise in upstream water level at a bridge or weir.
- **Regime Channel:** A channel in dynamic equilibrium ŌĆö neither silting nor scouring over a long period.
- **Silt Factor $f$:** $f = 1.76 \sqrt{d_m}$ where $d_m$ is median grain diameter in mm.
- **Design Scour Depth:** $D_s = 1.5 D$ to $2.0 D$, used for foundation design.

### Must-Remember Formulas
$$
P = 4.75 \sqrt{Q} \qquad
R = 0.473 (Q/f)^{1/3} \qquad
D = 1.35 \, q^{2/3} / f^{1/3}
$$

$$
h_a \approx 0.05 \, V^2 \left(\frac{1}{M^2} - 1\right) \qquad
L_{spur} = (0.2 \text{ to } 0.3) B
$$

### Spur Classification (Most Asked)
- **Repelling (perpendicular)** ŌĆö most common, simplest
- **Attracting (upstream-tilted)** ŌĆö pulls flow towards itself
- **Deflecting (downstream-tilted)** ŌĆö pushes flow away
- **T-Head** ŌĆö used at sharp bends
- **Kantal (Hockey-shaped)** ŌĆö combined function
- **Hollow / Permeable** ŌĆö induces siltation in Hilly rivers

### Site-Based Recommendations
- Convex bank erosion ŌåÆ **Repelling spur + Revetment**
- Bridge approach ŌåÆ **Guide Bunds**
- Meander cutoff ŌåÆ **Artificial Cut-off**
- Navigable river with depth issues ŌåÆ **Dredging + Bed Bars**
- Wide floodplain flooding ŌåÆ **Marginal Embankments**

### Design Cautions
- Spur head scour is **deepest** ŌĆö design foundation using $D_s = 1.5 D$ to $2.0 D$.
- Apron length around spur head $\geq 1.5 \times D_s$.
- Guide bund freeboard = HFL + Afflux + Freeboard (1.0ŌĆō1.5 m).
- For straight reach, **Lacey's regime formulas are valid**; for bends, use empirical bend corrections (typically 1.2ŌĆō1.5 multiplier on scour at outer bend).

### Typical 3-Mark Question Keywords (Memorize)
- *Classification of spurs* ŌĆö expect table with **5 types**.
- *Difference between permeable and impermeable spurs* ŌĆö at least **3 differences**.
- *Objectives of river training* ŌĆö at least **4** for full marks.

### Typical 14-Mark Question Patterns
- Numerical: Lacey regime width + scour depth (always with $F_s$).
- Descriptive: classification + sketches of spur types.
- Design: guide bund layout + afflux estimation.
- Case study: Himalayan river vs. alluvial plain river ŌĆö choice of spurs.

> **End of KTU 2024 Module 2 Notes ŌĆö River Training Works.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of River Training Works - Objectives - Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET747 - RIVER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification of River Training Works - Objectives - Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **River Training Works (RTW)** are a comprehensive system of engineering structures and measures constructed within, along, or across a river channel to guide, confine, and stabilize the river flow along a predetermined, hydraulically efficient, and economically viable course. The principal aim is to control the destructive and erratic behaviour of the river, particularly during high floods, to ensure safety of life, property, and infrastructure in the floodplains.

According to **IS 8408:1994 (Planning and Design of Open Drains ŌĆō Guidance)** and standard river engineering literature (e.g., Garg, 1987; Punmia & Lal, 1992), River Training Works include all hydraulic structures whose interaction with the river produces a desirable change in the flow pattern, sediment transport regime, and channel geometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** River training is essentially the *geometric control* of an alluvial river. Unlike flood control reservoirs, training works manipulate the river *in situ* without storing water.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Plain English)

Imagine a **wild, untrained horse** running across a green pasture. The horse is powerful but unpredictable ŌĆö it may trample crops, kick people, or wander into the village. As the owner, you have three options:
1. **Build a strong fence on both sides** so the horse runs only in a defined corridor (analogy: *marginal embankments / levees*).
2. **Tie a series of short ropes along the path** so the horse is gently pushed back to the centre if it tries to bend (analogy: *groynes / spurs*).
3. **Lead the horse with a long guiding rein** along a smoother, straighter path (analogy: *guide bunds and cut-offs*).

A river behaves exactly like this horse during monsoons ŌĆö it meanders, erodes banks, shifts its course, and causes flooding. **River training is the science of gently "leading" this wild horse (the river) through the use of fences, ropes, and reins (training structures) without breaking it.**

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** A river engineer never *fights* the river ŌĆö he *negotiates* with it using hydraulically and structurally intelligent training works.

---

## 1.3 Why Classification is Needed

All rivers are different. A braided river in the Brahmaputra plains behaves very differently from a meandering river in the Indo-Gangetic plains or a rocky mountain stream. Hence, **one single training structure cannot solve every problem**. The need for classification arises from:

- Different river **morphologies** (meandering, braided, straight, anastomosing).
- Different **objectives** (flood control, navigation, bank protection, sediment control).
- Different **flow stages** (low water, medium water, high water) that the structure must interact with.
- Different **structural rigidity** (permeable vs impermeable, temporary vs permanent).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A properly classified training scheme ensures that the structure works **synergistically with the natural flow**, rather than against it.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology to Remember

| Terminology | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Groyne / Spur** | A structure built transverse to the river flow from the bank into the river. |
| **Guide Bund** | A long embankment constructed along the approach to a bridge or barrage. |
| **Marginal Embankment / Levee** | A continuous embankment parallel to the river to confine the high floods. |
| **Bandal** | A permeable groyne made of bamboo, timber, or brushwood used for low-water training. |
| **Cut-off** | An artificial channel connecting two bends of a meander to reduce length. |
| **Revetment** | Surface protection of river bank against erosion (pitching, mattresses). |
| **Pitched Island** | A small artificially created island in the river bed to split the flow. |
| **Afflux** | The rise in upstream water level caused by a structure obstructing the flow. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of River Training Works

River training works can be classified in **four logical ways** based on KTU-approved curriculum (Garg; Punmia; K.Subramanya).

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Purpose (Functional Classification)

This is the most commonly adopted KTU classification:

**1. Training for High Water / Flood Control (Flood Protection Works)**
- Marginal Embankments (Levees)
- Dikes
- Flood walls
- High guide bunds

**2. Training for Low Water (Navigational Works)**
- Bandals
- Low spurs (permeable groynes)
- Dredging
- Closing of side channels

**3. Training for Medium Water (Channel Stability)**
- Impermeable spurs
- Bank revetment
- Pitched islands
- Cut-offs

**4. Training for River Diversion / Realignment**
- Cut-offs (artificial and natural)
- Pilot channels
- Diversion structures

**5. Training for Protection of Specific Structures (Bridges, Barrages)**
- Guide bunds
- Approach embankments
- Afflux bunds

> [!NOTE]
> A single training structure may serve multiple purposes. For example, a guide bund protects a bridge (Purpose 5) and also trains the river (Purpose 1 and 3).

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Type of Structure (Structural Classification)

This classification is widely used in IS 8408:1994 and KTU reference books.

**A. Transverse Structures (built at an angle to the flow, projecting from one bank):**
- Spurs (Impermeable, Permeable, Submerged)
- Groynes

**B. Longitudinal Structures (built parallel to the flow, along the bank):**
- Guide bunds
- Marginal levees
- Revetments
- Dikes

**C. Detached / Isolated Structures (not connected to the bank):**
- Pitched islands
- Dumped islands
- Articulated mattresses

**D. Cut-off Structures (works that re-align the channel):**
- Pilot channels
- Closure dams
- Cut-offs

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on Flow Stage at which the Structure is Effective

This is the **classical engineering classification** adopted by the Central Water Commission (CWC), India, and is a KTU high-weightage concept.

| Training Type | Effective Flow Stage | Main Objective | Typical Structures |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High Water Training** | Above Highest Flood Level (HFL) | Confine flood waters to the flood plain | Marginal embankments, guide bunds, flood walls |
| **Medium Water Training** | At Mean Water Level + bankful flow | Stabilize the channel, protect banks, prevent meandering | Impermeable spurs, pitched islands, revetments |
| **Low Water Training** | At Low Water Level (LWL) | Ensure adequate depth for navigation, concentrate flow | Bandals, low spurs, dredging, closure of side channels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Concept:** A given structure can function in *more than one stage* depending on its crest level. A spur with crest at HFL is high-water training; with crest at LWL+1 m, it is medium-water training; if it is submerged, it acts as low-water training.

### 2.1.4 Classification Based on Material / Permeability

- **Impermeable RTW:** Solid spurs, masonry/concrete guide bunds, pitched islands.
- **Permeable RTW:** Bandals (bamboo), tree spurs, rockfill spurs, sausage bundles.
- **Semi-permeable / Composite RTW:** Combinations used in modern river engineering.

---

## 2.2 Objectives of River Training Works

The objectives of river training are **multi-fold and inter-related**. For KTU 2024 examination, the following is the standard objective-based list (mapped to COs of PECET747).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary CO mapping (KTU 2024):** CO2 ŌĆō Apply river training concepts for flood control, navigation, and channel stability.

**The principal objectives are:**

1. **To provide a definite and stable river course** ŌĆö eliminate uncontrolled meander migration.
2. **To prevent flooding** of adjoining agricultural land, towns, and infrastructure.
3. **To protect the river banks** from erosion and retreat.
4. **To ensure navigability** of the river for ships/barges (especially in peninsular Indian rivers like Ganga, Brahmaputra).
5. **To control sediment transport** ŌĆö prevent excessive siltation in reservoirs, channels, and ports.
6. **To protect hydraulic structures** ŌĆö bridges, barrages, aqueducts, water intakes.
7. **To reclaim land** from the river floodplain for agriculture/development.
8. **To improve the discharge capacity** of the channel by reducing excessive width and providing a more efficient section.
9. **To maintain stable channel alignment** near towns, harbours, and estuaries.
10. **To reduce the cost of maintenance** of riverside infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> The **objectives are not independent** ŌĆö improving navigability often requires channel contraction, which in turn increases scour depth, which may require bank protection. The river engineer must balance these trade-offs.

---

## 2.3 Methods of River Training (KTU High-Yield Concept)

The **methods** are the actual engineering techniques used to achieve the objectives. There are **three classical methods** of river training corresponding to the three flow stages:

### Method 1: High Water Training (Flood Plain Method)
- **Goal:** Confine the flood water to a defined flood plain.
- **Used when:** Floods are large in volume and the river is wide.
- **Main technique:** Construction of **continuous marginal embankments** on both banks with a wide gap (Ōēź flood width).
- **Advantage:** Economical, simple, minimal river disturbance.
- **Disadvantage:** Encourages siltation in floodplain; needs periodic raising.

### Method 2: Medium Water Training (Channel Method)
- **Goal:** Confine the medium flood flow to a narrower, deeper, stable channel.
- **Used when:** The river has a tendency to meander and erode banks.
- **Main techniques:**
  - **Impermeable spurs / groynes** projecting from the bank.
  - **Bank revetment** (stone pitching, concrete mattress).
  - **Pitched islands** to split and train the flow.
- **Advantage:** Provides channel stability and useful depth.
- **Disadvantage:** Creates afflux; may cause scour at the spur nose.

### Method 3: Low Water Training (Peripheral Method)
- **Goal:** Maintain a navigable low-water channel.
- **Used when:** Riverbed is wide and shallow at low water (typical of braided rivers).
- **Main techniques:**
  - **Bamboo / brushwood bandals** to close off side channels and concentrate flow.
  - **Permeable low spurs** to deflect low flow.
  - **Dredging** of silt in critical shoals.
  - **Closing of side channels** with closure dams.
- **Advantage:** Encourages natural scour of the main channel during low-flow months.
- **Disadvantage:** Limited lifespan; needs renewal after every flood season.

### Method 4: Combined Training
Most modern KTU-classified schemes use a **combination** of all three methods. For example:
- **Farakka Barrage Project** uses high guide bunds + medium spurs + low bandals.
- **Ganga Bridge (Mokama) approach** uses guide bunds + cut-offs + revetments.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following formulas are most frequently used in KTU 2024 ESE questions on River Training Works. Units are given in **SI** unless stated.

| Equation / Parameter | Formula | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Afflux caused by a bridge/training work | $h_{afflux} = \left( \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + y_1 \right) - \left( \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + y_2 \right)$ | Sudden constriction, computed from energy balance (Bradley/USSR) |
| Bradley's Afflux Equation | $h^* = K \cdot \dfrac{V_{n2}^2}{2g}$ | $K$ is the backwater coefficient (function of $M = Q/Q_{n}$) |
| Lacey's regime scour depth | $R = 0.473 \cdot \left( \dfrac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3}$ | For design of spur/groyne foundation depth |
| Lacey's silt factor | $f = 1.76 \cdot \sqrt{m}$ | $m$ = weighted median grain size in mm |
| Design scour depth below HFL | $D_{design} = 1.5 \cdot R$ | Standard design coefficient adopted in India (CWC) |
| Manning's velocity in training reach | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Used for low-water training design |
| Length of groyne (Lane's formula) | $L = (0.25 \text{ to } 0.33) \cdot B$ | $B$ = river width at bankful stage |
| Curvature ratio for meander cut-off | $\dfrac{L_c}{L} = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5$ | $L_c$ = centre-line length, $L$ = straight distance |
| Groyne spacing (Inosemtsev / KTU) | $S_p = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5) \cdot L$ | $L$ = length of groyne projecting into the river |
| Angle of groyne with flow | $\theta = 90┬░ \text{ (repelling)} \text{ to } 60┬░ \text{ (attracting)}$ | Smaller angle = attracting spur |

> [!TIP]
> In the formulas above, $g$ denotes acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s┬▓**), $Q$ is discharge in **m┬│/s**, and $R$ is the hydraulic mean depth in **metres**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of River Training Works

1. **Bridges and Barrages:** Guide bunds are constructed upstream and downstream of every major bridge across an alluvial river in India to ensure the river passes safely under the bridge without meandering.
2. **Inland Waterways (NW-1, NW-2):** River training on the Ganga and Brahmaputra is essential for maintaining a minimum **3.0 m** navigable depth (as per Jal Marg Vikas Project).
3. **Urban Flood Management:** Cities like Patna, Guwahati, Varanasi, and Kolkata rely on marginal embankments and spurs to prevent annual monsoon flood damage.
4. **Reservoir Desiltation:** Spurs are used to deflect sediment-laden flow away offtakes and intakes (e.g., Ukai Dam on Tapi River).
5. **Port Engineering:** Pitched islands and breakwaters train the flow in the approach channels to major ports (Kolkata, Haldia).
6. **International Context:** The Mississippi River (USA), the Rhine (Europe), and the Yangtze (China) all use elaborate training works modelled on classical Indian engineering practice (Inglis, Lacey).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bradley's Afflux Equation (Most Asked KTU Derivation)

When a river training work (such as a bridge, barrage, or guide bund) creates a constriction in the river width, the water level upstream rises. This rise in water level is called **afflux**. Joseph R. Bradley (1970s, USA, adopted in CWC India) provided a comprehensive equation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical basis:** Afflux is governed by the principle of **conservation of energy** between the natural (unconstricted) river reach and the constricted reach.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

- Steady, gradually varied flow.
- The river is treated as a wide rectangular channel of width $B$.
- A constriction of width $b$ is created (e.g., a bridge with guide bunds).
- Total discharge $Q$ is constant (continuity).
- Energy loss between section 1 (upstream, natural) and section 2 (downstream of constriction) is small and neglected initially.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Continuity Equation at the natural (upstream) section and the constricted section:**

$$ Q = V_1 \cdot A_1 = V_2 \cdot A_2 $$

where $A_1 = B \cdot y_1$ and $A_2 = b \cdot y_2$, and $V_1, V_2$ are the mean velocities.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Express the constriction ratio:**

$$ M = \dfrac{Q}{Q_n} = \dfrac{b}{B} $$

where $Q_n$ is the natural channel discharge capacity at the constriction section, and $M$ is the **bridge constriction ratio** ($0 < M < 1$).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Energy Equation) between section 1 and section 2:**

$$ y_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = y_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + h_L $$

Since we are interested in maximum afflux, $h_L$ is taken as a function of the constriction geometry.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rearrange to express afflux $h^* = y_1 - y_2$:**

$$ h^* = y_1 - y_2 = \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} - \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + h_L $$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Use continuity to substitute $V_2 = V_1 \cdot (A_1/A_2) = V_1 \cdot (B \cdot y_1) / (b \cdot y_2)$.**

**Step 6 ŌĆö Bradley's empirical step (verified by USGS and CWC):**

Define the **normal (natural) velocity at the constriction** as $V_{n2}$. From continuity:

$$ V_{n2} = \dfrac{V_1}{M} $$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Introduce the backwater coefficient $K$** (empirical, depends on $M$):

$$ h^* = K \cdot \dfrac{V_{n2}^2}{2g} $$

**Final Bradley's Afflux Equation:**

$$ \boxed{h^* = K \cdot \dfrac{V_{n2}^2}{2g} = K \cdot \dfrac{1}{2g} \left(\dfrac{V_1}{M}\right)^2} $$

### 3.1.3 Tabulated Values of $K$ (CWC / Bradley)

| Constriction Ratio $M$ | Backwater Coefficient $K$ |
|---|---|
| 0.9 | 0.32 |
| 0.8 | 0.65 |
| 0.7 | 1.05 |
| 0.6 | 1.55 |
| 0.5 | 2.10 |
| 0.4 | 2.85 |
| 0.3 | 3.75 |
| 0.2 | 5.00 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As $M$ decreases (more constriction), $K$ increases rapidly, and afflux becomes disproportionately large. This is why a bridge should not constrict the river by more than **20 %** ($M \geq 0.8$) at bankful stage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Design Scour Depth around Spurs (Lacey's Theory)

> [!NOTE]
> **Lacey's Regime Theory (1930)** is the basis of river training design in India. It applies to alluvial, sediment-transporting channels in regime.

### 3.2.1 Lacey's Regime Flow Depth

Lacey (1930) proposed that for a channel flowing in regime:

$$ V = 10.8 \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/3} $$

where $R$ is the hydraulic mean depth, $V$ is mean velocity, and $S$ is the energy slope.

### 3.2.2 Combining with Manning's Equation to Get Regime Depth

Lacey's equation is equivalent to Manning's with a specific roughness:

$$ R = 0.473 \cdot \left( \dfrac{Q}{f} \right)^{1/3} $$

**Derivation Outline:**

**Step 1:** Lacey's $V$ formula: $V = 10.8 R^{2/3} S^{1/3}$.

**Step 2:** Continuity: $Q = A \cdot V = (B \cdot R) \cdot V$.

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$ Q = B \cdot R \cdot 10.8 R^{2/3} S^{1/3} = 10.8 B R^{5/3} S^{1/3} $$

**Step 4:** Introduce Lacey's silt factor $f = 1.76 \sqrt{m}$, where $m$ is the median grain size (mm). Lacey also proposed $S = f^{5/3} / (3340 Q^{1/6})$ for regime channels.

**Step 5:** Substituting and simplifying gives:

$$ R = 0.473 \left(\dfrac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3} $$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Design Scour Depth for Spurs (CWC Recommendation):**

To account for local scour at the spur nose, the design depth is taken as:

$$ \boxed{R_{design} = 1.5 \cdot R = 0.71 \left(\dfrac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3}} $$

The factor **1.5** is empirical and is universally adopted in Indian CWC manuals.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A bridge is proposed across an alluvial river with a designed discharge $Q = 5000$ m┬│/s. The width of the river at bankful stage is $B = 400$ m, and the bridge is designed for a clear waterway of $b = 320$ m. The natural velocity at the bridge site is $V_1 = 2.5$ m/s. Calculate:
> (a) The constriction ratio $M$.
> (b) The afflux $h^*$ using Bradley's equation.
> (c) The design scour depth if Lacey's silt factor $f = 0.95$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a): Constriction ratio**

$$ M = \dfrac{b}{B} = \dfrac{320}{400} = 0.80 $$

**Part (b): Afflux using Bradley's equation**

Normal velocity at constriction:
$$ V_{n2} = \dfrac{V_1}{M} = \dfrac{2.5}{0.80} = 3.125 \text{ m/s} $$

From the table, for $M = 0.80$, $K = 0.65$.

$$ h^* = K \cdot \dfrac{V_{n2}^2}{2g} = 0.65 \times \dfrac{(3.125)^2}{2 \times 9.81} $$

$$ h^* = 0.65 \times \dfrac{9.766}{19.62} = 0.65 \times 0.498 $$

$$ \boxed{h^* \approx 0.324 \text{ m} = 324 \text{ mm}} $$

**Part (c): Design scour depth using Lacey**

$$ R = 0.473 \cdot \left( \dfrac{Q}{f} \right)^{1/3} = 0.473 \cdot \left( \dfrac{5000}{0.95} \right)^{1/3} $$

$$ R = 0.473 \cdot (5263.16)^{1/3} = 0.473 \cdot 17.39 = 8.226 \text{ m} $$

Design scour depth:
$$ \boxed{R_{design} = 1.5 \times 8.226 = 12.34 \text{ m below HFL}} $$

> [!TIP]
> This is the depth at which the foundation of bridge piers and spur noses must be taken down to ensure safety against scour failure.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Method vs Objective vs Structure

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU tool** to remember the relationship between methods, objectives, and structures.

| Method | Primary Objective | Typical Structures | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **High Water Training** | Flood confinement | Marginal embankments, levees, guide bunds | Economical, simple | Encourages siltation; needs raising |
| **Medium Water Training** | Channel stability, bank protection | Impermeable spurs, pitched islands, revetments | Long-lasting, stabilizes channel | Causes afflux, scour at nose |
| **Low Water Training** | Navigation, low-flow channel maintenance | Bandals, low spurs, dredging | Cheaper, renewable | Short life, needs annual repair |
| **Cut-offs** | Realignment, length reduction | Pilot channels, closure dams | Increases slope, restores energy | Risk of head-cut erosion |
| **Bank Revetment** | Direct bank protection | Stone pitching, mattresses | Continuous, durable | High initial cost, vulnerable to undermining |
| **Guide Bunds** | Protect bridge/barrage | Long embankments with shanks | Defines flow path, prevents avulsion | Costly; needs careful design |

---

## 3.5 Selection Logic for a Training Method (Decision Matrix)

A river engineer selects a method using the following KTU-classified logic:

- **If the river is wide and the flood is large** ŌåÆ **High water training (levees)** is preferred.
- **If the river is meandering and eroding** ŌåÆ **Medium water training (spurs + revetment)** is preferred.
- **If the river is shallow and braided at low flow** ŌåÆ **Low water training (bandals + dredging)** is preferred.
- **If a bridge/barrage is involved** ŌåÆ **Guide bund + afflux bund** is mandatory.
- **If the river is excessively meandering** ŌåÆ **Cut-off** is used to reduce length and improve slope.
- **If banks are collapsing** ŌåÆ **Bank revetment (pitching/mattress)** is the immediate answer.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, all three training methods (high, medium, low) are usually adopted **simultaneously** in a comprehensive river training scheme.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
