## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*List the essential design parameters required for the design of river training works.*

**Model Answer:**
The essential design parameters are:
1. **Hydraulic parameters** ŌĆō design discharge $Q$, HFL, approach velocity $V_a$, bed slope $S$.
2. **Sediment parameters** ŌĆō median bed particle size $D_{50}$, silt factor $f$, sediment transport rate.
3. **Geotechnical parameters** ŌĆō cohesion $c$, angle of internal friction $\phi$, specific gravity $G_s$, permeability.
4. **Geomorphic parameters** ŌĆō river planform, sinuosity, radius of curvature, meander belt width.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*State Lacey's formulae for regime width, scour depth, and silt factor.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Regime width:** $P = 4.75\sqrt{Q}$
2. **Normal scour depth:** $d = 0.473(Q/f)^{1/3}$
3. **Silt factor:** $f = 1.76\sqrt{D_{50}}$

where $P$ is in m, $Q$ in cumecs, $D_{50}$ in mm, and $d$ is below HFL.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain with neat sketches the **components of a guide bank** and derive Khosla's empirical formula for the length of the upstream guide bank. State the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design the **upstream and downstream guide banks** for a bridge crossing on a straight reach of an alluvial river with the following data: **(7 Marks)**
- Design discharge $Q = 6,400$ cumecs
- Bed material $D_{50} = 0.25$ mm
- HFL = RL 102.00 m
- Bed level = RL 98.00 m
- Bridge waterway = 180 m

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
A guide bank has three components: **Head (curved nose)**, **Shank (straight body)**, and **Tail (downstream end)**. Khosla's formula:

$$L_u = K\sqrt{Q}$$

where $K = 1.5$ for straight, $2.5$ for curved reach. Derived from field data of major Indian bridges (CWC, 1950s study). The formula is empirical and assumes regime condition, alluvial bed, and uniform approach flow.

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: Length of upstream guide bank** (straight reach, $K = 1.5$):
$$L_u = 1.5 \times \sqrt{6400} = 1.5 \times 80 = 120\ m$$

**Step 2: Length of downstream guide bank:**
$$L_d = 0.25 \times 120 = 30\ m$$

**Step 3: Radius of curvature of head:**
$$R = 0.45 \times 120 = 54\ m$$

**Step 4: Silt factor:**
$$f = 1.76 \times \sqrt{0.25} = 0.88$$

**Step 5: Lacey's scour depth:**
$$d = 0.473 \times (6400/0.88)^{1/3} = 0.473 \times (7272.7)^{1/3} = 0.473 \times 19.36 = 9.16\ m$$

**Step 6: Maximum scour at nose:**
$$D_{max} = 1.5 \times 9.16 = 13.74\ m\ \text{below HFL} = 102.00 - 13.74 = 88.26\ m$$

**Step 7: Foundation level (1.5 m below scour):**
$$88.26 - 1.5 = 86.76\ m$$

**Step 8: Crest level (freeboard 1.5 m):**
$$102.00 + 1.5 = 103.50\ m$$

**Step 9: Shank length = Bridge waterway + Head projection =** $180 + 120 = 300\ m$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]`

**Q. (a)** Differentiate between **flood embankment** and **guide embankment**. State the design steps for a flood embankment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A meandering river has $L_{old} = 4,500$ m, $S_{old} = 0.00018$, and discharge $Q = 5,000$ cumecs. An artificial **cut-off** of length $L_{new} = 1,200$ m is proposed. Compute the **new slope, drop in HFL, and stable width** of the cut-off channel. Use Lacey's formula. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

| Feature | Flood Embankment | Guide Embankment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Purpose | Protects flood plain from inundation | Guides flow through bridge/barrage |
| Location | Parallel to river at safe distance | Immediately upstream/downstream of structure |
| Length | Long (tens of km) | Short (a few hundred metres) |
| Design head | HFL + freeboard | HFL + 1.5 m (higher standard) |
| Scour design | Toe protection, gentle slopes | Heavy stone pitching + launching apron |

**Design steps for flood embankment:**
1. Fix alignment and set-back (Ōēź 1.5 P).
2. Determine HFL from flood studies.
3. Fix crest level = HFL + freeboard.
4. Choose crest width (3ŌĆō9 m).
5. Fix side slopes based on soil type.
6. Design pitching/revetment for water face.
7. Provide toe protection (stone/boulder).
8. Stability check (sliding, overturning, piping).
9. Construct with compaction Ōēź 95% Proctor density.

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: Lacey regime width (original):**
$$P_{old} = 4.75\sqrt{5000} = 4.75 \times 70.71 = 335.9\ m$$

**Step 2: New slope after cut-off:**
$$S_{new} = S_{old} \times \left(\frac{L_{old}}{L_{new}}\right)^2 = 0.00018 \times (4500/1200)^2$$

$$= 0.00018 \times (3.75)^2 = 0.00018 \times 14.0625 = 0.00253$$

**Step 3: Drop in HFL:**

$$\Delta H = S_{old} \times (L_{old} - L_{new}) \left[1 - \frac{L_{new}}{L_{old}}\right]$$

$$= 0.00018 \times 3300 \times [1 - 0.2667] = 0.00018 \times 3300 \times 0.7333 = 0.4356\ m$$

**Step 4: Stable cut-off width (Lacey's recommendation):**
$$P_{cutoff} = 0.67 \times P_{old} = 0.67 \times 335.9 = 225.0\ m$$

**Step 5: Critical ratio check:**
$$L_{old}/L_{new} = 3.75 \leq 4\ \Rightarrow \text{NOT self-sustaining without engineering intervention.}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the multiplier in scour depth:** Many students compute Lacey's $d$ but forget the **1.5├Ś factor** at the nose of the guide bank. The maximum scour is **NOT** the same as Lacey's normal scour.
> 2. **Confusing $L_u$ and $L_d$:** The downstream guide bank is **0.25 times** the upstream, not equal to it.
> 3. **Cut-off ratio sign error:** Always check $L_{old}/L_{new}$, not the inverse, against Lacey's critical value of **4ŌĆō5**.
> 4. **Skipping units in Lacey formula:** $Q$ must be in **cumecs** and $D_{50}$ in **mm** ŌĆö wrong units = wrong answer.
> 5. **No freeboard in crest level:** 1.0 to 1.5 m freeboard is mandatory; missing it loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö KTU Module 2**
> - [x] **Design Discharge ($Q$):** 50ŌĆō100 yr return period for bridges; SPF for barrages.
> - [x] **Lacey's Regime Width:** $P = 4.75\sqrt{Q}$ ŌĆö the **backbone formula** of river engineering.
> - [x] **Lacey's Silt Factor:** $f = 1.76\sqrt{D_{50}}$ ($D_{50}$ in mm).
> - [x] **Lacey's Scour Depth:** $d = 0.473(Q/f)^{1/3}$ ŌĆö measured **below HFL**.
> - [x] **Embankment freeboard:** 0.5ŌĆō2.0 m depending on importance.
> - [x] **Guide bank length (Khosla):** $L_u = K\sqrt{Q}$, with $K = 1.5$ (straight) to $2.5$ (curved).
> - [x] **Downstream guide bank:** $L_d = 0.25 L_u$.
> - [x] **Head radius of curvature:** $R = 0.45 L_u$.
> - [x] **Maximum scour at nose:** $1.5 \times$ Lacey's normal scour.
> - [x] **Stone pitching (Isbash):** $V_a = K_s\sqrt{2g\Delta\rho d/\rho_w}$.
> - [x] **Artificial cut-off critical ratio:** $L_{old}/L_{new} \geq 4$ŌĆō5 (Lacey).
> - [x] **New slope after cut-off:** $S_{new} = S_{old} \times (L_{old}/L_{new})^2$.
> - [x] **Stable cut-off width:** $0.67 \times P_{old}$ (about 2/3 of original).
> - [x] **Components of guide bank:** Head ŌåÆ Shank ŌåÆ Tail (in flow direction).
> - [x] **Types of embankments:** Homogeneous, Zoned, Diaphragm.
> - [x] **Stability criteria:** FoS Ōēź 1.5 (overturning), Ōēź 1.25 (sliding).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pitched Island
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET747 - RIVER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pitched Island

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pitched Island** is a detached, mound-type river training structure constructed within the river bed (running either transverse or longitudinal to the flow) consisting of an earthen core protected on its slopes by a layer of stone/boulder pitching. It is a non-permeable, semi-submerged training work whose crest is kept above the **Low Water Level (LWL)** but below the **High Flood Level (HFL)**, designed to concentrate the low-water flow into a defined, narrower channel while simultaneously inducing siltation in the sheltered zone between the island and the river bank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardised):**
> A Pitched Island is a classical *island-type* river training work ŌĆö a *detached* mound in the river cross-section, armoured with stone pitching, used to *narrow the low-water channel*, *guide the flow*, and *protect banks* from scour during floods. It falls under the broader category of **Floor/Island Protection Works** in River Engineering.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö "The Traffic Island in a River"**
>
> Imagine a wide, multi-lane road where cars are scattered and slow. The traffic police build a small concrete median (island) in the middle of the road. Suddenly, vehicles are forced to use only the two narrow lanes on either side ŌĆö they move faster and in an orderly fashion. Over time, dirt and leaves accumulate (silt) on the road just behind the median, narrowing the effective road permanently.
>
> A **Pitched Island does the exact same thing in a river**:
> - The **river bed** = the wide road
> - The **pitched island** = the concrete median
> - The **narrower channel** between island and bank = the orderly traffic lane (deeper, faster flow)
> - The **siltation** between island and bank = the leaves/dirt that accumulate

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard design values are used in KTU-recognised practice:

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Specific weight of water ($\gamma_w$) | **9.81 kN/m┬│** |
| Specific weight of stone ($\gamma_s$) | **24 ŌĆō 26 kN/m┬│** |
| Manning's $n$ (stone pitching) | **0.025 ŌĆō 0.035** |
| Crest width of pitched island | **2.0 m ŌĆō 4.0 m** |
| Side slope of earth core | **1.5 H : 1 V to 2.0 H : 1 V** |
| Pitching thickness | **0.30 m ŌĆō 0.60 m** |
| Freeboard above LWL | **0.3 m ŌĆō 0.6 m** |
| Top of island below HFL by | **0.6 m ŌĆō 1.0 m** |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams ŌĆö "PITCHED":**
> **P**rotect banks, **I**nduce siltation, **T**rain the channel, **C**oncentrate flow, **H**eight between LWL & HFL, **E**arth core + stone **D**ressing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step

A pitched island operates on three concurrent hydraulic principles:

1. **Flow Concentration Principle**
   - By occupying a portion of the river cross-section, the island forces the low-water discharge to pass through the **reduced width** $(B - b_i)$, where $B$ is the original river width and $b_i$ is the projected width of the island.
   - This increases the **hydraulic radius** and the **mean velocity**, producing the desired **scour in the main channel** for navigation or flood carrying capacity.

2. **Siltation-Inducement Principle**
   - The sheltered pocket *behind* the island (between island and bank) becomes a **dead-water zone** with near-zero velocity.
   - Suspended sediment settles out, gradually converting the area into *char* (raised land) ŌĆö effectively reducing the active river width permanently.

3. **Energy Dissipation & Bank Protection**
   - The pitched slopes offer a **rough boundary**, dissipating kinetic energy of the flow.
   - The pitching protects the **earth core** from being eroded by the shear stress of the passing flood.

### 2.2 Structural Components of a Pitched Island

The cross-section of a pitched island consists of the following layered components (from inside out, bottom to top):

- **A. Earth Core (Hearting):** Locally available sand, silt, or clayey material, forming the bulk of the mound.
- **B. Filter Layer:** A graded layer of sand and gravel (or geotextile) preventing piping of fines from the core into the pitching.
- **C. Stone Pitching (Armour):** The outermost protective layer of hand-packed stones/boulders, usually 0.3ŌĆō0.6 m thick.
- **D. Toe Protection:** A launching apron or weighted stone trench at the foot of the slope to prevent undermining by scour.
- **E. Crest Dressing:** A small, well-compacted top layer, sometimes with a kerb stone to mark the alignment.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently asked in KTU University Examinations** for River Engineering. Memorise the variables and the form of each equation.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Continuity Equation | $Q = A \cdot V$ | $Q$ = discharge (m┬│/s), $A$ = area (m┬▓), $V$ = velocity (m/s) | Discharge computation after flow concentration |
| 2 | Continuity (Reduced Width) | $Q = (B - b_i) \cdot d \cdot V'$ | $b_i$ = island width, $d$ = flow depth, $V'$ = new velocity | Velocity increase due to island |
| 3 | Tractive Shear Stress | $\tau_0 = \gamma_w \cdot y \cdot S$ | $y$ = depth (m), $S$ = energy slope | Force on pitching |
| 4 | Critical Shear (Stone) | $\tau_c = 0.06 \cdot (\gamma_s - \gamma_w) \cdot d_{50}$ | $d_{50}$ = median stone size (m) | Stability check |
| 5 | Lane's Permissible Velocity | $V_p = C \cdot \sqrt{d_{50}}$ | $C$ = constant (1.4ŌĆō1.7), $d_{50}$ in m | Stone size for a given velocity |
| 6 | Isbash's Equation (Partially Exposed) | $V = 1.7 \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot d \cdot \left(\dfrac{\gamma_s - \gamma_w}{\gamma_w}\right)}$ | $d$ = stone dia, $g$ = 9.81 m/s┬▓ | Size of stone in launching apron |
| 7 | Shields' Parameter (Critical) | $\tau_c^* = \dfrac{\tau_c}{(\gamma_s - \gamma_w) \cdot d}$ | Dimensionless | Incipient motion check (Ōēł 0.06 for uniform stone) |
| 8 | Scour Depth (Lacey's Regime) | $D = 1.34 \cdot \left(\dfrac{q^2}{f}\right)^{1/3}$ | $q$ = unit discharge, $f$ = silt factor | Maximum scour near island toe |
| 9 | Specific Gravity Relation | $G_s = \dfrac{\gamma_s}{\gamma_w}$ | ŌĆö | Used in Isbash/Shields |
| 10 | Permissible Velocity (Khosla's) | $V_p = C \cdot d_{50}^{0.5}$ | $C$ from table | Indian practice (CWC) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Note that `|` symbols are replaced with `\vert` in the table above to preserve markdown formatting. In your answer sheets, always write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pitched islands are extensively used in:
- **Brahmaputra & Kosi river training** (India) ŌĆö to tame the braided, shifting channels.
- **Dam spillway approach channels** ŌĆö to prevent meandering near the intake.
- **Bridge protection works** ŌĆö preventing scour around guide bunds and abutments.
- **Navigation channels** ŌĆö concentrating flow to maintain minimum draught.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Increased Velocity Due to Flow Concentration

> **Problem Statement:**
> A river of width $B = 500$ m carries a low-water discharge $Q = 250$ m┬│/s at a depth $d = 1.5$ m. A pitched island of width $b_i = 80$ m is constructed in the centre. Determine the new mean velocity in the concentrated low-water channel. Take the original mean velocity as $V_0 = 0.33$ m/s.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Original Velocity Verification (Sanity Check)**

The original cross-sectional area is:

$$A_0 = B \cdot d = 500 \cdot 1.5 = 750 \text{ m}^2$$

The original mean velocity is:

$$V_0 = \dfrac{Q}{A_0} = \dfrac{250}{750} = 0.333 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Effective Width After Construction of the Island**

The island occupies $b_i$ metres, so the effective flow width becomes:

$$B_{eff} = B - b_i = 500 - 80 = 420 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö New Effective Area**

Assuming the water depth remains approximately the same at low stage (island is submerged only at high stage, but we consider low stage first):

$$A_{eff} = B_{eff} \cdot d = 420 \cdot 1.5 = 630 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö New Mean Velocity**

$$V_{new} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{eff}} = \dfrac{250}{630} = 0.397 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Percentage Increase in Velocity**

$$\Delta V \,(\%) = \dfrac{V_{new} - V_0}{V_0} \cdot 100 = \dfrac{0.397 - 0.333}{0.333} \cdot 100 = 19.2\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A nearly 20% velocity increase in the main channel is *hydraulically significant*. It causes natural scour, deepening the channel and improving navigation or flood-conveyance without dredging.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Design of Stone Size Using Lane's Formula

> **KTU Standard Problem:**
> Design the stone size required for the pitching of a pitched island where the local velocity during a moderate flood is $V = 2.5$ m/s. The pitching is in clean, angular, well-packed stone on a slope of 1 in 2 (V : H). Use Lane's formula with $C = 1.4$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recall Lane's Formula**

$$V_p = C \cdot \sqrt{d_{50}}$$

where $V_p$ is the permissible velocity (m/s) and $d_{50}$ is the median stone diameter in metres.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute Known Values**

$$2.5 = 1.4 \cdot \sqrt{d_{50}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Isolate $d_{50}$**

$$\sqrt{d_{50}} = \dfrac{2.5}{1.4} = 1.786$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Square Both Sides**

$$d_{50} = (1.786)^2 = 3.189 \text{ m}$$

This is *unrealistically large* ŌĆö indicating that $C = 1.4$ is too conservative or the velocity is excessive for $C = 1.4$. Per Indian practice for clean angular stones, **$C$ should be taken higher (Ōēł 1.7)**.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Recompute with $C = 1.7$**

$$\sqrt{d_{50}} = \dfrac{2.5}{1.7} = 1.471$$

$$d_{50} = (1.471)^2 = 2.16 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Still very large. In real practice, for a velocity of 2.5 m/s, the design is shifted to **Isbash's equation** (which is more physical) or the slope is reduced (made flatter) to lower the velocity component along the pitching.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply Isbash's Equation (Partially Exposed Stone)**

$$V = 1.7 \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot d \cdot \left(\dfrac{\gamma_s - \gamma_w}{\gamma_w}\right)}$$

Assume $\gamma_s = 25$ kN/m┬│, $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m┬│:

$$2.5 = 1.7 \cdot \sqrt{9.81 \cdot d \cdot \left(\dfrac{25 - 9.81}{9.81}\right)}$$

$$2.5 = 1.7 \cdot \sqrt{9.81 \cdot d \cdot 1.548}$$

$$2.5 = 1.7 \cdot \sqrt{15.19 \cdot d}$$

$$\dfrac{2.5}{1.7} = \sqrt{15.19 \cdot d}$$

$$1.471 = \sqrt{15.19 \cdot d}$$

$$2.164 = 15.19 \cdot d$$

$$d = \dfrac{2.164}{15.19} = 0.1425 \text{ m} \approx 14.3 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Select Practical Stone Size**

A practical boulder size of **150 mm to 200 mm** is recommended with a pitching thickness of **0.30 m**, using two layers of stone.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Tractive Force Check on Pitching

> **KTU Question Type:** "Check the stability of pitching against shear failure."

**Given:** Depth of flow over island slope during flood = $y = 3.5$ m, Energy slope $S = 1/4000$, $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m┬│, stone $\gamma_s = 25$ kN/m┬│, $d_{50} = 0.20$ m.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Average Tractive Stress on the Bed**

$$\tau_0 = \gamma_w \cdot y \cdot S = 9.81 \cdot 3.5 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{4000}\right)$$

$$\tau_0 = 9.81 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 0.00025 = 0.00858 \text{ kN/m}^2 = 8.58 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Critical (Permissible) Tractive Stress on Stones**

$$\tau_c = 0.06 \cdot (\gamma_s - \gamma_w) \cdot d_{50}$$

$$\tau_c = 0.06 \cdot (25 - 9.81) \cdot 0.20$$

$$\tau_c = 0.06 \cdot 15.19 \cdot 0.20 = 0.1823 \text{ kN/m}^2 = 182.3 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Stability Check**

$$\tau_0 \leq \tau_c$$

$$8.58 \text{ N/m}^2 \ll 182.3 \text{ N/m}^2 \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

**Factor of Safety (FoS):**

$$FoS = \dfrac{\tau_c}{\tau_0} = \dfrac{182.3}{8.58} = 21.2$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners love asking *"Check the stability of the pitching and compute the factor of safety."* Always present both the average and permissible shear stress with units, and end with a clear SAFE / UNSAFE verdict.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix ŌĆö When to Use a Pitched Island

| Site Condition | Suitability of Pitched Island | Alternative / Comment |
|---|---|---|
| Wide, shallow braided river | Ō£ģ Highly suitable | Narrows & trains flow |
| Deep, narrow gorge section | ŌØī Not suitable | Use spurs/groynes instead |
| Aggressive meandering reach | Ō£ģ Suitable | Acts as flow director |
| Confined, urban reach | ŌÜĀ Conditional | Ensure no flood surcharge risk |
| Loose sandy bed, high scour | Ō£ģ Suitable | Provides floor protection too |
| Hard rock bed | ŌØī Not required | No need for protection |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cross-Sectional Architecture of a Pitched Island (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FLOW DIRECTION --> ] --> B{ZONE 1: UPSTREAM APRON}
    B --> C{EARTH CORE: Sand/Silt/Clay Mound}
    C --> D[FILTER LAYER: Graded Sand + Geotextile]
    D --> E[ARMOUR LAYER: Stone Pitching 0.3-0.6 m]
    E --> F[CREST: Above LWL by 0.3-0.6 m]
    E --> G[UPSTREAM SLOPE 1.5H:1V to 2H:1V]
    E --> H[DOWNSTREAM SLOPE 1.5H:1V to 2H:1V]
    G --> I{TOE PROTECTION: Launching Apron 1-2 m Wide}
    H --> I
    I --> J[ZONE 4: SILTATION POCKET]
    J --> K[RIVER BANK: Protected by Pitching Extension]
    K --> L[LOW WATER CHANNEL: Narrowed, Deepened, Self-Scouring]
    B --> M[ZONE 2: PITCHED ISLAND BODY]
    M --> E
    L --> N[ZONE 3: SCOUR HOLE near Island Nose]
    N --> O[ORIGINAL RIVER BED]
```

### 4.2 Plan View of Pitched Island in a River Reach

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[UPSTREAM RIVER REACH] --> P2{FLOW SPLITS at ISLAND NOSE}
    P2 --> P3[LEFT LOW WATER CHANNEL]
    P2 --> P4[RIGHT LOW WATER CHANNEL]
    P3 --> P5[CONVERGENT FLOW DOWNSTREAM]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[DOWNSTREAM RIVER REACH]
    subgraph ISLAND_BLOCK
    P7[PITCHED ISLAND: Detached Mound]
    P8[SILTATION ZONE: Sheltered Pocket]
    P9[STONE PITCHING ON SLOPES]
    end
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P7
```

### 4.3 Component-Wise Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[PITCHED ISLAND SYSTEM] --> S1[A. STRUCTURAL COMPONENT]
    R --> S2[B. HYDRAULIC FUNCTION]
    R --> S3[C. PROTECTION MECHANISM]
    S1 --> S1a[Earth Core]
    S1 --> S1b[Filter Layer]
    S1 --> S1c[Stone Pitching]
    S1 --> S1d[Toe Apron]
    S2 --> S2a[Flow Concentration]
    S2 --> S2b[Siltation Inducement]
    S2 --> S2c[Channel Training]
    S3 --> S3a[Resists Scour]
    S3 --> S3b[Dissipates Energy]
    S3 --> S3c[Protects Bank]
    S1c --> S3a
    S1d --> S3a
    S2a --> S2c
```

### 4.4 Hydraulic Force Balance on a Pitched Island (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    F1[HYDRODYNAMIC FORCES ACTING ON ISLAND] --> F1a[Drag Force Fd]
    F1 --> F1b[Lift Force Fl]
    F1 --> F1c[Weight of Stone Ws]
    F1 --> F1d[Friction Force Ff]
    F1a --> F1e{NET FORCE BALANCE}
    F1b --> F1e
    F1c --> F1e
    F1d --> F1e
    F1e --> F1f[STABLE: Ws sin theta + Ff > Fd + Fl]
    F1e --> F1g[UNSTABLE: Fd + Fl > Ws sin theta + Ff]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The above block diagrams are *functional architecture* representations (per the safeguard protocol), illustrating the layered structure, hydraulic zones, and force interactions of a pitched island. For KTU exams, you are expected to draw a *neatly-labelled cross-sectional sketch* with the earth core, filter, pitching, and toe apron clearly demarcated.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023]**
*Define a pitched island. State any two situations where it is preferred over conventional groynes/spurs.*

**Model Answer:**

A **pitched island** is a detached, mound-type river training work constructed in the river bed, consisting of an earthen core protected by stone pitching on its slopes, with its crest above the LWL but below HFL.

**Two situations where it is preferred over groynes:**

1. **Wide, shallow braided rivers** (e.g., Brahmaputra, Kosi): Groynes cause excessive local scour at their noses, whereas pitched islands provide a smoother, distributed flow-concentration with self-scour in the channel and silting in the pocket.
2. **Where the river needs long-term width reduction without aggressive bank attack:** Pitched islands encourage permanent siltation, gradually reclaiming land, whereas spurs remain as permanent local disturbances.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for the two situations)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]**
*List the main components of a pitched island and state the function of the toe protection.*

**Model Answer:**

**Components:**
1. Earth core (hearting)
2. Filter layer (sand-gravel / geotextile)
3. Stone pitching (armour)
4. Toe protection / launching apron
5. Crest dressing

**Function of Toe Protection:**
The toe protection (launching apron) is provided at the foot of the pitching slope to **prevent undermining of the island due to scour** at its base. When scour occurs, the apron *launches* (slides down) into the scour hole, providing a continuous protective cover and arresting further erosion. *(3 marks: 2 for components + 1 for toe function)*

---

### ­¤ōĢ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Ō£Å’ĖÅ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **constructional features and functions of a pitched island** in river training. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A river of width **600 m** carries a discharge of **400 m┬│/s** at a depth of **1.8 m**. A pitched island of width **100 m** is proposed at the centre. Calculate:
   1. The new mean velocity in the concentrated channel.
   2. The percentage increase in velocity.
   3. The tractive force per unit area on the pitching, assuming energy slope $S = 1/5000$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Constructional Features & Functions (7 marks)**

*Sketch (3 marks):* Neat cross-section showing ŌĆö

```
   _____________
  |  CREST      |  <- Above LWL by 0.3-0.6 m
  |_____________|
 /|             |\
/ | EARTH CORE  | \  <- Pitching 0.3-0.6 m thick
 | (with filter)|  \
 |             |   \
/|_____________|\___\___TOE APRON______
                                 \________SILT POCKET________
   LOW WATER CHANNEL (concentrated)
```

*Description (4 marks):*

A pitched island is a **detached river training structure** placed in the river bed. It consists of:
- An **earth core** of locally available material.
- A **graded filter** (sand + gravel) preventing piping.
- **Stone pitching (armour)** 0.3ŌĆō0.6 m thick, hand-packed on all slopes.
- A **launching apron** at the toe for scour protection.
- **Crest** kept above LWL but below HFL by ~0.6ŌĆō1.0 m.

**Functions:**
1. Concentrates low-water flow into a defined, deeper channel.
2. Induces siltation in the sheltered pocket ŌåÆ reclaims char land.
3. Protects banks from direct flood attack.
4. Reduces excessive river width in braided reaches.
5. Acts as an energy dissipator due to rough pitching.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $B = 600$ m, $Q = 400$ m┬│/s, $d = 1.8$ m, $b_i = 100$ m, $S = 1/5000$

**(i) New mean velocity (3 marks):**

Original area:
$$A_0 = B \cdot d = 600 \cdot 1.8 = 1080 \text{ m}^2$$

Original velocity (verification):
$$V_0 = \dfrac{400}{1080} = 0.370 \text{ m/s}$$

Effective width:
$$B_{eff} = 600 - 100 = 500 \text{ m}$$

Effective area:
$$A_{eff} = 500 \cdot 1.8 = 900 \text{ m}^2$$

New mean velocity:
$$V_{new} = \dfrac{400}{900} = 0.444 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Percentage increase (2 marks):**

$$\% \text{ Increase} = \dfrac{0.444 - 0.370}{0.370} \cdot 100 = 20.0\%$$

**(iii) Tractive force (2 marks):**

$$\tau_0 = \gamma_w \cdot y \cdot S = 9.81 \cdot 1.8 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{5000}\right)$$

$$\tau_0 = 9.81 \cdot 1.8 \cdot 0.0002 = 0.00353 \text{ kN/m}^2 = 3.53 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Final Answers:** $V_{new} = 0.444$ m/s, $\Delta V = 20\%$, $\tau_0 = 3.53$ N/m┬▓

---

#### Ō£Å’ĖÅ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö July 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Differentiate between a **pitched island and a groyne** as river training works. List the advantages and limitations of pitched islands. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design the **stone size for the pitching** of a pitched island where the local velocity during a moderate flood is **3.0 m/s**. Take $\gamma_s = 25$ kN/m┬│, $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m┬│, and assume partially exposed stone in Isbash's equation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pitched Island vs. Groyne (7 marks)**

| Aspect | Pitched Island | Groyne (Spur) |
|---|---|---|
| **Connection to bank** | Detached, independent | Attached to the bank |
| **Flow obstruction** | Partial (only at low water) | Full across section |
| **Siltation effect** | Behind the island | Behind the groyne head |
| **Local scour** | Distributed, milder | Aggressive at groyne nose |
| **Construction cost** | Lower (less material) | Higher (longer structure) |
| **Suitability** | Wide braided rivers | Confined meandering rivers |

**Advantages of Pitched Islands (3 marks):**
1. Causes gentler, more uniform flow concentration.
2. Lower construction cost than full-length groynes.
3. Induces large-scale siltation ŌåÆ land reclamation.
4. Can be built incrementally (phase-wise construction).
5. Less interference with floodplain drainage.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. Ineffective in deep, narrow channels.
2. Difficult to maintain alignment in highly mobile braided rivers.
3. Requires continuous maintenance of crest and toe.
4. May cause *excessive* silting in the wrong zone if not oriented properly.

---

**(b) Stone Size Design using Isbash's Equation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $V = 3.0$ m/s, $\gamma_s = 25$ kN/m┬│, $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m┬│

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Write the governing equation:

$$V = 1.7 \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot d \cdot \left(\dfrac{\gamma_s - \gamma_w}{\gamma_w}\right)}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute values:

$$3.0 = 1.7 \cdot \sqrt{9.81 \cdot d \cdot \left(\dfrac{25 - 9.81}{9.81}\right)}$$

$$3.0 = 1.7 \cdot \sqrt{9.81 \cdot d \cdot 1.548}$$

$$3.0 = 1.7 \cdot \sqrt{15.19 \cdot d}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Solve:

$$\dfrac{3.0}{1.7} = \sqrt{15.19 \cdot d}$$

$$1.765 = \sqrt{15.19 \cdot d}$$

$$3.115 = 15.19 \cdot d$$

$$d = \dfrac{3.115}{15.19} = 0.205 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Recommended practical size:

Use boulder of median size **$d_{50} \approx 0.25$ m (250 mm)** with pitching thickness of **0.30ŌĆō0.40 m** in two layers, over a graded filter of 100ŌĆō150 mm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract island width** ŌĆö Many students compute $V_{new} = Q/(B \cdot d)$ *without* subtracting $b_i$. This gives the *original* velocity, not the new one. Marks deducted: 1ŌĆō2.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in Lane's formula** ŌĆö $d_{50}$ MUST be in *metres*, not mm. Writing $d_{50} = 200$ (in mm) will give a wildly wrong answer.
> 3. **Confusing Lane's $C$ value** ŌĆö For *clean angular* stone $C \approx 1.7$; for *smooth water-worn* stone $C \approx 1.2$. Examiners will check if you used the correct value with proper justification.
> 4. **Sketch missing or unlabelled** ŌĆö A pitched island sketch without labels (Earth Core, Pitching, Toe, LWL, HFL) will lose 2ŌĆō3 marks easily.
> 5. **Forgetting Factor of Safety** ŌĆö In tractive force problems, always state FoS, not just "safe/unsafe".
> 6. **Mixing up Isbash's coefficient** ŌĆö Coefficient is **1.7** for *partially exposed* and **0.7** for *fully exposed* stone. Mixing them is a frequent error.

---

### ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ A **pitched island is a detached, semi-submerged training work** with its crest between LWL and HFL.
- Ō£ģ Its **three primary functions** are: (1) Flow concentration, (2) Siltation inducement, (3) Bank/floor protection.
- Ō£ģ **Components (in order):** Earth Core ŌåÆ Filter Layer ŌåÆ Stone Pitching ŌåÆ Toe Apron.
- Ō£ģ **Pitching thickness:** 0.30ŌĆō0.60 m; **Side slopes:** 1.5H:1V to 2H:1V; **Crest width:** 2ŌĆō4 m.
- Ō£ģ **Design equations to remember (in order of priority):**
   - Continuity: $Q = B_{eff} \cdot d \cdot V$
   - Lane: $V_p = C \cdot \sqrt{d_{50}}$ (C = 1.4ŌĆō1.7)
   - Isbash: $V = 1.7 \cdot \sqrt{g \cdot d \cdot (G_s - 1)}$
   - Tractive: $\tau_0 = \gamma_w \cdot y \cdot S$
   - Stability: $\tau_0 \le 0.06 \cdot (\gamma_s - \gamma_w) \cdot d_{50}$
- Ō£ģ **Pitched island vs. Groyne:** Detached vs. Attached; gentle vs. aggressive scour; cheaper vs. costlier.
- Ō£ģ **Suitable for:** Wide, shallow, braided rivers (Brahmaputra, Kosi-type).
- Ō£ģ **Not suitable for:** Deep gorges, narrow rock-bound channels.
- Ō£ģ **Always label** the sketch with: LWL, HFL, crest, earth core, filter, pitching, toe apron, flow direction.
- Ō£ģ **Mention Factor of Safety** in every stability calculation.
- Ō£ģ **Standard C value for clean angular stone** in Lane's formula = **1.7** (use this unless otherwise specified).
- Ō£ģ **Typical practical stone size** for river training pitching: 150ŌĆō300 mm boulders.
- Ō£ģ **Launching apron** is *non-negotiable* ŌĆö it prevents catastrophic undermining of the island.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes ŌĆö Pitched Island | PECET747 ŌĆö River Engineering | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: River Diversions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET747 - RIVER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** River Diversions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RIVER DIVERSION** is a hydrological and hydraulic engineering operation in which the natural flow path of a river is temporarily or permanently redirected through engineered conduits (tunnels, channels, or pipelines) to create a dry working area ŌĆö known as the *dewatered construction pit* ŌĆö for the safe execution of in-stream structures such as dams, barrages, weirs, spillways, bridges, and powerhouse foundations.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, river diversion falls under the broader umbrella of **River Training and Control Works**, and is classified as a *temporary construction-phase hydraulic structure*. The diverted flow is typically carried through:

- **Diversion Tunnels** (preferred in narrow gorges, hard rock reaches)
- **Diversion Channels** (open-cut, used in wide alluvial valleys)
- **Flumes / Pipes** (used for small discharges, generally < 50 m┬│/s)

The Hydraulic design window for diversion is based on the **Mean Annual Flood (MAF)** or a flood of chosen **Return Period (Tr)** ŌĆö typically 25 years for diversion structures (as per IS 4247 and CWC guidelines).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight ŌĆö Module 2:** *River Diversions ŌĆö methods, layout, hydraulic design, cofferdams, and case studies.* This topic integrates open channel hydraulics, hydrology, and geotechnical engineering, making it one of the most application-heavy topics in River Engineering.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are renovating a large aquarium that has a continuous stream of water flowing through it. To clean or rebuild the bottom gravel, you cannot simply turn off the flow (the fish would die). Instead, you install a **temporary bypass pipe** that carries the water around the work zone, allowing you to dewater the area safely while life upstream continues uninterrupted. The bypass pipe is your **diversion tunnel**, the temporary walls you build around the work zone are your **cofferdams**, and the dry pit you create is your **construction pit**.

In river engineering, we apply the same principle ŌĆö but the "fish" is the river's discharge, the "bypass pipe" is an engineered tunnel or channel, and the "gravel" is the riverbed foundation of a major hydraulic structure worth hundreds of crores.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of water:** $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Unit weight of water:** $\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- **Standard design return period for diversion:** $T_r = 25 \text{ years}$ (CWC / IS 4247)
- **Diversion flood standard:** typically **1.5├Ś to 2.0├Ś the Mean Annual Flood (MAF)**
- **Allowable velocity in diversion tunnel (concrete-lined):** $V_{allow} = 2.5 \text{ to } 4.5 \text{ m/s}$
- **Allowable velocity in unlined diversion channel (rock):** $V_{allow} = 1.5 \text{ to } 3.0 \text{ m/s}$

---

## 1.4 Objectives / Purposes of River Diversion

The following table summarizes the engineering objectives served by river diversion works:

| S.No. | Purpose | Typical Application |
|:-----:|:--------|:--------------------|
| 1 | Foundation dewatering for in-stream construction | Dams, barrages, weirs |
| 2 | Protection of works during floods | Bridge piers, well foundations |
| 3 | Routing of design flood away from work area | Spillway construction |
| 4 | Powerhouse and intake construction | Hydroelectric projects |
| 5 | Inter-basin water transfer | Link canal headworks |
| 6 | Environmental / fish passage bypasses | Eco-friendly diversions |

---

## 1.5 Classification of Diversion Methods

Diversion works are classified based on the **mode of flow conveyance** and the **stage of project execution**:

- **Stage I Diversion (Pre-monsoon / Initial):** River is diverted through one half of the river width while the other half is closed by an upstream cofferdam for foundation work.
- **Stage II Diversion (Post-monsoon / Completion):** After the dam body is raised above the river's HFL, the river is routed through the reservoir outlets (spillway gates, low-level outlets) and the diversion tunnel/channel is plugged permanently.
- **Single-Stage Diversion:** Used for smaller projects or where topography permits a single conduit (tunnel or channel) of adequate capacity.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU examinations, students must remember:** The selection of diversion method depends on the **discharge to be diverted, geological conditions, project duration, and cost economics.** Tunnels are preferred in narrow gorges with hard rock; open channels are economical in wide valleys with soft overburden.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of River Diversion ŌĆö Step by Step

The complete engineering process of river diversion can be decomposed into **seven logical steps**. Each step is a separate scoring opportunity in KTU examinations.

### Step 1: Hydrological Assessment
- Determine the **design flood discharge (Q_design)** for the chosen return period (typically 25 years for diversion).
- Methods: **Rational method, Unit Hydrograph method, Flood Frequency Analysis (Gumbel / Log-Pearson Type III).**
- Input data: Catchment area, rainfall intensity, runoff coefficient, time of concentration.

### Step 2: Topographical & Geological Survey
- Identify suitable locations for **upstream and downstream cofferdams.**
- Determine the **alignment, length, and gradient** of the diversion conduit.
- Geological investigations (RQD, joint spacing, rock mass rating) influence the choice of **lined vs. unlined tunnel.**

### Step 3: Selection of Diversion Conduit Type
- **Tunnel** ŌĆö for narrow gorges, high discharges, hard rock.
- **Channel** ŌĆö for wide valleys, alluvial deposits, low-lying areas.
- **Combination (channel + tunnel)** ŌĆö used when the upstream reach is open and the downstream reach requires tunnelling through a ridge.

### Step 4: Hydraulic Sizing of Conduit
- Apply **Manning's equation** (open channel) or **Darcy-Weisbach / Hazen-Williams** (closed conduit).
- Size such that the water surface elevation at the upstream cofferdam does not exceed the **cofferdam crest elevation.**

### Step 5: Cofferdam Design
- Cofferdams retain water and soil on the upstream and downstream sides of the work pit.
- Types: **Earthen cofferdams, Rockfill cofferdams, Sheet pile cofferdams, Cellular cofferdams (cofferdams made of interlocking steel sheet piles).**
- Designed for hydrostatic pressure, seepage, and piping (a phenomenon in which soil particles are eroded by seepage water, leading to failure).

### Step 6: Construction and Monitoring
- Sequence: Excavate diversion conduit ŌåÆ Build cofferdams ŌåÆ Divert flow ŌåÆ Dewater pit ŌåÆ Construct main structure ŌåÆ Raise dam above HFL ŌåÆ Plug diversion conduit.

### Step 7: Post-Construction Plugging
- The diversion tunnel is permanently sealed with a **concrete plug** (typically keyed into the surrounding rock for 1.5ŌĆō3.0 tunnel diameters in length).
- Grouting is performed around the plug to prevent seepage.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are board-exam ready. Memorize the formula name, the variables, and the application context ŌĆö not just the equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Table 2.1 ŌĆö Master Formula Sheet for River Diversion} \\
\hline
\text{Formula} \quad & \text{Application} \quad & \text{Equation} \quad & \text{Units} \\
\hline
\text{Continuity} \quad & \text{Discharge computation} \quad & Q = A \cdot V \quad & \text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
\text{Manning's eqn.} \quad & \text{Open channel flow} \quad & V = \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2} \quad & \text{m/s} \\
\text{Conveyance} \quad & \text{Channel capacity} \quad & Q = A \cdot \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2} \quad & \text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
\text{Specific energy} \quad & \text{Open channel transitions} \quad & E = y + \frac{V^2}{2g} \quad & \text{m} \\
\text{Froude number} \quad & \text{Flow regime check} \quad & Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot D_h}} \quad & \text{dimensionless} \\
\text{Head loss (friction)} \quad & \text{Tunnel / pipe flow} \quad & h_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot V^2}{2g \cdot D} \quad & \text{m} \\
\text{Head loss (entry)} \quad & \text{Entrance loss} \quad & h_e = K_e \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} \quad & \text{m} \\
\text{Lacey's scour depth} \quad & \text{Scour in alluvium} \quad & R = 0.47 \left(\frac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3} \quad & \text{m} \\
\text{Silt factor (Lacey)} \quad & \text{Soil grain parameter} \quad & f = 1.76 \sqrt{d_m} \quad & \text{dimensionless} \\
\text{Stability of cofferdam} \quad & \text{Sliding check} \quad & SF = \frac{(\mu \cdot W)}{\Sigma H} \quad & \text{dimensionless} \\
\text{Seepage (Bligh's)} \quad & \text{Weir foundation creep} \quad & L_w = C \cdot H \quad & \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Variable Definitions used in the table above:**
> - $A$ = cross-sectional area of flow (m┬▓)
> - $V$ = mean velocity (m/s)
> - $n$ = Manning's roughness coefficient (dimensionless, typical values: 0.013 for concrete, 0.030 for natural channel)
> - $R$ = hydraulic radius $= A/P$ (m)
> - $S$ = bed slope (dimensionless, m/m)
> - $D_h$ = hydraulic depth (m)
> - $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless)
> - $d_m$ = median grain size of bed material (mm)
> - $H$ = head of water (m)
> - $C$ = Bligh's creep coefficient (typically 5ŌĆō18 depending on soil)
> - $\mu$ = coefficient of friction at base

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles covered above are not academic exercises ŌĆö they are the **backbone of every major dam constructed in India and worldwide.** Notable Indian examples include:

- **Bhakra Dam (Punjab-Himachal):** Two diversion tunnels of 9.14 m diameter each, designed for a flood of ~7000 m┬│/s.
- **Tehri Dam (Uttarakhand):** Two diversion tunnels (1.5 km and 1.4 km long) handling the Bhagirathi and Bhilangana rivers.
- **Sardar Sarovar Dam (Gujarat):** Diversion channel of 200 m width designed for the Narmada floods.
- **Hirakud Dam (Odisha):** Earthen cofferdams up to 28 m height were used for foundation dewatering.

In all these cases, the **Manning equation** governed the sizing of the diversion conduit, and **Lacey's scour depth formula** was used to design the cut-off depth of the cofferdams. The KTU examiner expects you to know **at least one such case study in detail** for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydraulic Sizing of a Diversion Channel Using Manning's Equation

> **Problem Setup (KTU Exam Standard):** Design the cross-section of a rectangular diversion channel to carry a discharge of **Q = 250 m┬│/s** on a bed slope of **S = 1 in 1500**, with Manning's n = 0.025 (earth channel, well-maintained). Assume the channel is **most economical (best hydraulic section)** and check whether the flow is subcritical or supercritical.

### Step 1: Recall the Best Hydraulic Section for a Rectangular Channel

For a rectangular channel, the **best hydraulic section** (maximum conveyance for a given area) occurs when:

$$
b = 2y
$$

where $b$ is the bed width and $y$ is the depth of flow. This is a fundamental result derived by differentiating Manning's equation with respect to $y$ while keeping $A = b \cdot y$ constant.

### Step 2: Express Area and Wetted Perimeter in Terms of y

For $b = 2y$:

$$
A = b \cdot y = (2y) \cdot y = 2y^2
$$

The wetted perimeter $P$ for a rectangular channel is:

$$
P = b + 2y = 2y + 2y = 4y
$$

The hydraulic radius $R$ is therefore:

$$
R = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{2y^2}{4y} = \frac{y}{2}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Manning's Equation

$$
Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}
$$

Substituting the expressions from Step 2:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot (2y^2) \cdot \left(\frac{y}{2}\right)^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{2 \cdot S^{1/2}}{n \cdot 2^{2/3}} \cdot y^{2} \cdot y^{2/3}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{2^{1/3} \cdot S^{1/2}}{n} \cdot y^{8/3}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for the Flow Depth y

Rearranging for $y$:

$$
y^{8/3} = \frac{Q \cdot n}{2^{1/3} \cdot S^{1/2}}
$$

$$
y = \left[\frac{Q \cdot n}{2^{1/3} \cdot S^{1/2}}\right]^{3/8}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute Numerical Values

Given: $Q = 250$ m┬│/s, $n = 0.025$, $S = 1/1500 = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}$.

First, compute $S^{1/2}$:

$$
S^{1/2} = \sqrt{6.667 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02582
$$

Then:

$$
2^{1/3} = 1.2599
$$

Now substitute into the equation for $y^{8/3}$:

$$
y^{8/3} = \frac{250 \times 0.025}{1.2599 \times 0.02582}
$$

$$
y^{8/3} = \frac{6.25}{0.03254} = 192.07
$$

Therefore:

$$
y = (192.07)^{3/8}
$$

Computing the exponent:

$$
192.07^{0.375} = 9.55 \text{ m (approximately)}
$$

### Step 6: Calculate the Bed Width

$$
b = 2y = 2 \times 9.55 = 19.10 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 7: Calculate the Mean Velocity

$$
A = 2y^2 = 2 \times 91.20 = 182.40 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{250}{182.40} = 1.371 \text{ m/s}
$$

### Step 8: Check Flow Regime Using Froude Number

Hydraulic depth for rectangular section:

$$
D_h = \frac{A}{b} = \frac{182.40}{19.10} = 9.55 \text{ m}
$$

Froude number:

$$
Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot D_h}} = \frac{1.371}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 9.55}}
$$

$$
Fr = \frac{1.371}{9.681} = 0.142
$$

Since $Fr < 1$, the flow is **subcritical (tranquil flow)** ŌĆö the design is hydraulically stable. Freeboard of 0.5 to 1.0 m should be added to the depth for practical design.

### Final Design Summary

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Depth of flow}, \; y &= 9.55 \text{ m} \\
\text{Bed width}, \; b &= 19.10 \text{ m} \\
\text{Mean velocity}, \; V &= 1.371 \text{ m/s} \\
\text{Froude number}, \; Fr &= 0.142 \text{ (subcritical)} \\
\text{Recommended freeboard} &= 1.0 \text{ m} \\
\text{Total channel depth (with freeboard)} &= 10.55 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Scour Depth at the Cofferdam Using Lacey's Formula

> **Problem Setup:** A cofferdam is proposed on an alluvial river bed. The design discharge is **Q = 1500 m┬│/s**, and the median grain size of the bed material is **d_m = 0.30 mm**. Estimate the **maximum scour depth** at the cofferdam location.

### Step 1: Compute the Silt Factor (Lacey's f)

Lacey's silt factor is given by:

$$
f = 1.76 \sqrt{d_m}
$$

where $d_m$ is in mm. Substituting:

$$
f = 1.76 \times \sqrt{0.30} = 1.76 \times 0.5477 = 0.964
$$

### Step 2: Apply Lacey's Normal Scour Depth Formula

$$
R = 0.47 \left(\frac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3}
$$

Substituting $Q = 1500$ m┬│/s and $f = 0.964$:

$$
\frac{Q}{f} = \frac{1500}{0.964} = 1556.0
$$

$$
R = 0.47 \times (1556.0)^{1/3}
$$

Computing the cube root:

$$
(1556.0)^{1/3} = 11.58
$$

$$
R = 0.47 \times 11.58 = 5.44 \text{ m}
$$

This is the **normal scour depth** under steady uniform flow conditions.

### Step 3: Apply Multiplier for Local Scour at Cofferdam

For local scour near a cofferdam (or any obstruction), a **multiplier of 1.5 to 2.0** is applied to account for the increased turbulence and contraction of flow. Using a multiplier of **1.75** (typical for a well-aligned cofferdam):

$$
R_{local} = 1.75 \times 5.44 = 9.52 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 4: Recommended Sheet Pile / Cut-off Depth

For safe design, the cut-off depth of the cofferdam is taken as:

$$
\text{Depth of cut-off} = R_{local} + \text{extra depth for safety} = 9.52 + 1.0 = 10.52 \text{ m}
$$

A factor of safety in the range of **1.0 to 1.5 m** below the calculated scour depth is standard KTU/CWC practice.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Diversion Methods

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Table 3.1 ŌĆö Decision Matrix for Diversion Method Selection} \\
\hline
\text{Criterion} \quad & \text{Tunnel} \quad & \text{Open Channel} \quad & \text{Pipe / Flume} \\
\hline
\text{Discharge capacity} \quad & \text{High (up to } 5000 \text{ m}^3/\text{s)} \quad & \text{Moderate-High} \quad & \text{Low (} < 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{s)} \\
\text{Topographic suitability} \quad & \text{Narrow gorges} \quad & \text{Wide valleys} \quad & \text{Any} \\
\text{Geological requirement} \quad & \text{Competent rock} \quad & \text{Stable soil} \quad & \text{Minimal} \\
\text{Initial cost} \quad & \text{Very High} \quad & \text{Moderate} \quad & \text{Low} \\
\text{Time to construct} \quad & \text{Long (12-24 months)} \quad & \text{Moderate} \quad & \text{Short} \\
\text{Space occupied} \quad & \text{None (subsurface)} \quad & \text{Large footprint} \quad & \text{Minimal} \\
\text{Maintenance during use} \quad & \text{Very Low} \quad & \text{Moderate} \quad & \text{Low} \\
\text{Risk of blockage by silt} \quad & \text{Low} \quad & \text{Moderate-High} \quad & \text{High} \\
\text{Plugging complexity} \quad & \text{Complex (concrete plug)} \quad & \text{Simple (earthen bund)} \quad & \text{Simple (valve closure)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ŌĆö Typical River Diversion Project

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture of a typical diversion project**, showing the flow of water, the construction sequence, and the role of each component.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Upstream River Inflow] --> B[Upstream Cofferdam]
    B --> C{Diversion Conduit Type}
    C -->|Tunnel| D1[Diversion Tunnel]
    C -->|Open Channel| D2[Diversion Channel]
    C -->|Pipe| D3[Diversion Pipe/Flume]
    D1 --> E[Outlet Portal]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Downstream Cofferdam]
    F --> G[Downstream River Continues]
    B -. Blocks flow .-> H[Construction Pit Dewatered]
    F -. Blocks flow .-> H
    H --> I[Main Dam/Barrage Foundation]
    I --> J[Raise Dam Above HFL]
    J --> K[Plug Diversion Conduit]
    K --> L[Project Complete - Full Reservoir]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **upstream cofferdam** (Node B) is the first hydraulic control that diverts water away from the work area.
- The **diversion conduit** (Nodes D1/D2/D3) is the heart of the system ŌĆö it carries the design flood.
- The **construction pit** (Node H) is the dry area created by the cofferdams, where the main structure is built.
- The **plugging** (Node K) is the final closure step that permanently seals the diversion.

---

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic ŌĆö Diversion Through Tunnel with Upstream and Downstream Cofferdams

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UPSTREAM[Upstream Reach]
        U1[River Water Level U/S] --> U2[U/S Cofferdam Earth + Sheet Pile]
        U2 --> U3[Concrete Cut-off Wall]
    end
    UPSTREAM --> T1[Tunnel Inlet Trash Rack]
    T1 --> T2[Tunnel Lining Concrete]
    T2 --> T3[Bend/Transition Section]
    T3 --> T4[Tunnel Outlet]
    T4 --> DOWNSTREAM
    subgraph DOWNSTREAM[Downstream Reach]
        D1[D/S Cofferdam] --> D2[Stilling Basin]
        D2 --> D3[Downstream River]
    end
    WORKPIT[Construction Pit Dry Area Foundation Excavation] -.- U2
    WORKPIT -.- D1
```

**Engineering interpretation:**

- The **trash rack** at the tunnel inlet prevents floating debris (logs, boulders) from entering and blocking the tunnel.
- The **concrete lining** reduces friction and prevents erosion of the surrounding rock.
- The **stilling basin** at the outlet dissipates the high kinetic energy of the water before it rejoins the natural river ŌĆö preventing downstream scour.
- The **concrete cut-off wall** beneath the upstream cofferdam extends below the maximum scour depth to prevent piping failure.

---

## 4.3 Construction Sequence Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 0: Site Investigation and Hydrological Study] --> S2[Stage 1A: Excavate Diversion Tunnel and/or Channel]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 1B: Construct U/S and D/S Cofferdams]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 1C: Divert River Flow Through Conduit]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 2: Dewater Construction Pit]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 3: Excavate Foundation of Main Dam/Barrage]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 4: Construct Main Dam Up to River Bed Level]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 5: Raise Dam Above HFL During Dry Season]
    S8 --> S9[Stage 6: Close Diversion Conduit with Concrete Plug]
    S9 --> S10[Stage 7: Impound Water and Commission Project]
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Logic for Cofferdam Type Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the depth of water to be retained?}
    Q1 -->|< 3 m| A1[Use Earthen Cofferdam]
    Q1 -->|3 to 8 m| A2[Use Rockfill Cofferdam with Sheet Pile Core]
    Q1 -->|8 to 15 m| A3[Use Cellular Sheet Pile Cofferdam]
    Q1 -->|> 15 m| A4[Use Double-Wall Sheet Pile Cofferdam with Earthfill]
    A1 --> Z[Final Cofferdam Selected]
    A2 --> Z
    A3 --> Z
    A4 --> Z
```

---
