# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model Question 1]**
> **Q: Define river diversion. List the various methods adopted for diverting a river during construction of hydraulic structures.**
> 
> **Model Answer:**
> River diversion is the temporary or permanent hydraulic engineering operation of redirecting the natural flow of a river through an engineered conduit to create a dry construction pit for the safe execution of in-stream structures like dams, barrages, and bridges. The main methods adopted are:
> 1. **Diversion tunnel** ŌĆö used in narrow gorges with hard rock; carries high discharges with low maintenance.
> 2. **Diversion channel (open cut)** ŌĆö used in wide valleys with alluvial deposits; economical for moderate discharges.
> 3. **Diversion through pipes or flumes** ŌĆö used for small discharges (typically < 50 m┬│/s) in minor works.
> 4. **Stage diversion** ŌĆö the river is blocked in halves; one half is worked upon while the other half carries flow.
> 5. **Diversion through completed portions of the main structure** ŌĆö adopted in large dams where low-level outlets or spillway bays are used to pass flow during the later stages of construction.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model Question 2]**
> **Q: What is a cofferdam? Mention its types and the design considerations involved.**
> 
> **Model Answer:**
> A cofferdam is a temporary enclosing structure built in a river to create a dry working area for the construction of permanent hydraulic structures. It retains water and soil on the upstream and downstream sides of the work pit. **Types of cofferdams:**
> 1. **Earthen cofferdam** ŌĆö built from local soil; suitable for low heads (< 3 m) and short durations.
> 2. **Rockfill cofferdam** ŌĆö made of compacted rock with an impervious core; suitable for moderate heads.
> 3. **Sheet pile cofferdam** ŌĆö interlocking steel sheet piles driven into the bed; used for deeper water and limited space.
> 4. **Cellular cofferdam** ŌĆö interconnected circular or diaphragm-type sheet pile cells filled with earth or rock.
> 
> **Design considerations:** Stability against sliding and overturning, seepage and piping control, scour depth at the toe, freeboard above HFL, and constructability within the available construction season.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students commonly lose marks in the following ways:
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** explicitly in design problems (e.g., "best hydraulic section", "Mannings n = 0.025").
> 2. **Mixing up units** ŌĆö Lacey's formula takes $d_m$ in **mm**, not metres. Writing 0.30 m instead of 0.30 mm gives wrong results.
> 3. **Skipping the freeboard calculation** in channel design ŌĆö full marks require total channel depth with freeboard.
> 4. **Not specifying the return period** of the design flood ŌĆö examiners explicitly test whether you know $T_r = 25$ years is standard for diversion.
> 5. **Confusing "scour depth" with "cut-off depth"** ŌĆö scour depth is the natural depression; cut-off is the engineered penetration below scour, including a safety margin.
> 6. **In case-study questions**, students describe the project but do not **link the diversion design to the local topography/geology** ŌĆö examiners specifically test this integration.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Diversion Channel Hydraulic Design

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question Paper, Module 2, Set A]**

> **(a)** Derive the condition for the **best hydraulic rectangular section** for an open channel, starting from Manning's equation. State the key assumption made. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A diversion channel is to be designed to carry **Q = 180 m┬│/s** on a bed slope of **1 in 1200**. The channel is rectangular, lined with concrete (Manning's **n = 0.015**), and must be designed as a best hydraulic section. Determine the **depth of flow, bed width, and mean velocity**. Also check the **Froude number** and recommend whether this is a stable design. Assume the channel is in a wide alluvial valley. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Best Hydraulic Section for Rectangular Channel:**

We begin with Manning's equation:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}
$$

For a given discharge $Q$, slope $S$, and roughness $n$, the conveyance is maximum when $A \cdot R^{2/3}$ is maximum. Equivalently, for a given area $A$, we minimize the wetted perimeter $P$.

For a rectangular channel of bed width $b$ and depth $y$:

$$
A = b \cdot y \implies b = \frac{A}{y}
$$

$$
P = b + 2y = \frac{A}{y} + 2y
$$

Differentiating $P$ with respect to $y$ and setting $\frac{dP}{dy} = 0$:

$$
\frac{dP}{dy} = -\frac{A}{y^2} + 2 = 0
$$

$$
A = 2y^2
$$

Substituting back:

$$
b \cdot y = 2y^2 \implies b = 2y
$$

**Key Assumption:** The bed slope $S$ and roughness $n$ are constant, and the flow is uniform and steady.

---

**(b) Numerical Design Solution:**

Given: $Q = 180$ m┬│/s, $S = 1/1200 = 8.333 \times 10^{-4}$, $n = 0.015$, rectangular best hydraulic section.

For a best hydraulic rectangular section, $b = 2y$ and $R = y/2$.

Applying Manning's equation:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot (2y^2) \cdot \left(\frac{y}{2}\right)^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}
$$

$$
180 = \frac{1}{0.015} \cdot 2y^2 \cdot \left(\frac{y}{2}\right)^{2/3} \cdot (8.333 \times 10^{-4})^{1/2}
$$

Computing constants:

$$
S^{1/2} = 0.02887, \quad \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2/3} = 0.6300
$$

Therefore:

$$
180 = \frac{2 \times 0.6300}{0.015} \cdot y^2 \cdot y^{2/3} \cdot 0.02887
$$

$$
180 = \frac{1.2600 \times 0.02887}{0.015} \cdot y^{8/3}
$$

$$
180 = 2.425 \cdot y^{8/3}
$$

$$
y^{8/3} = \frac{180}{2.425} = 74.23
$$

$$
y = (74.23)^{3/8} = 6.04 \text{ m}
$$

Therefore:

$$
b = 2y = 12.08 \text{ m}
$$

Mean velocity:

$$
A = 2y^2 = 2 \times 36.48 = 72.96 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{180}{72.96} = 2.467 \text{ m/s}
$$

Froude number check:

$$
D_h = y = 6.04 \text{ m} \quad \text{(for rectangular section)}
$$

$$
Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot D_h}} = \frac{2.467}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 6.04}} = \frac{2.467}{7.700} = 0.320
$$

Since $Fr = 0.320 < 1$, the flow is **subcritical (tranquil)** ŌĆö the design is **stable**.

**Recommended freeboard:** 0.5 to 1.0 m (use 1.0 m for safety).

**Total channel depth with freeboard:** $6.04 + 1.0 = 7.04$ m.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Cofferdam Scour Depth and Diversion Tunnel Case Study

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question Paper, Module 2, Set B]**

> **(a)** A cofferdam is proposed on an alluvial river with a design discharge of **Q = 2400 m┬│/s**. The median grain size of the bed material is **d_m = 0.45 mm**. Estimate the **maximum scour depth** at the cofferdam location using Lacey's formula. Recommend the **depth of cut-off** with a suitable safety margin. State the limitations of Lacey's formula. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **layout of a typical river diversion arrangement using a diversion tunnel**. Discuss the function of each component. Mention the design flood return period adopted for diversion works as per Indian Standard guidelines. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Lacey's Scour Depth Calculation:**

Given: $Q = 2400$ m┬│/s, $d_m = 0.45$ mm.

Step 1: Silt factor:

$$
f = 1.76 \sqrt{d_m} = 1.76 \sqrt{0.45} = 1.76 \times 0.6708 = 1.181
$$

Step 2: Normal scour depth:

$$
R = 0.47 \left(\frac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3} = 0.47 \left(\frac{2400}{1.181}\right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
\frac{Q}{f} = 2032.2
$$

$$
R = 0.47 \times (2032.2)^{1/3} = 0.47 \times 12.66 = 5.95 \text{ m}
$$

Step 3: Local scour at cofferdam (multiplier 1.75):

$$
R_{local} = 1.75 \times 5.95 = 10.41 \text{ m}
$$

Step 4: Recommended cut-off depth (adding 1.0 m safety margin):

$$
D_{cut-off} = 10.41 + 1.0 = 11.41 \text{ m} \approx 11.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Limitations of Lacey's formula:**
1. Applicable only to **alluvial, non-cohesive bed materials** in regime conditions (channels that have achieved equilibrium between flow and sediment transport).
2. Does not account for **local scour due to obstructions** like piers, cofferdams ŌĆö empirical multipliers are needed.
3. Assumes **uniform flow** and ignores bends, transitions, and backwater effects.
4. Not applicable in **cohesive soils, rock, or stratified beds**.
5. The silt factor is based on **median grain size only**; does not account for grain size distribution.

---

**(b) Diversion Tunnel Layout ŌĆö Descriptive Answer:**

A typical river diversion layout using a tunnel consists of the following components (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2):

1. **Upstream Cofferdam:** A temporary barrier across the river upstream of the work area, designed to retain water and force it into the diversion tunnel. Usually constructed of earth/rockfill with a sheet pile or concrete cut-off wall extending below the scour depth.

2. **Diversion Tunnel Inlet:** A specially shaped entrance (often bell-mouthed or with a transition) provided with a **trash rack** to prevent debris from entering. The inlet is typically aligned tangentially to the river flow to minimize entry losses.

3. **Tunnel Conduit:** A horseshoe or circular cross-section excavated through rock, lined with concrete to reduce friction losses and prevent erosion. The tunnel is designed to flow partly full under the design discharge to maintain open-channel flow conditions.

4. **Tunnel Outlet and Stilling Basin:** At the downstream end, the high-velocity water is dissipated in a **stilling basin** before it rejoins the river, preventing downstream scour and erosion.

5. **Downstream Cofferdam:** A smaller barrier downstream of the work pit to prevent backflow into the construction area.

6. **Construction Pit:** The dry, dewatered area enclosed by the cofferdams, where the main dam/barrage foundation is excavated and concreted.

**Design Flood Return Period:** As per **IS 4247 and CWC guidelines**, the diversion flood for major hydraulic structures is typically based on a **25-year return period**, though for very large dams, a higher return period (50 or 100 years) may be adopted based on risk analysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision points for KTU exam ŌĆö high-density, exam-oriented summary:**

- **Definition:** River diversion = temporary/permanent redirection of river flow through engineered conduits for safe in-stream construction.
- **Standard design return period for diversion works:** $T_r = 25$ years (per IS 4247 / CWC).
- **Three main methods:** Diversion **tunnel** (hard rock, narrow gorge), diversion **channel** (wide valley, alluvium), diversion **pipe/flume** (small discharges).
- **Best hydraulic rectangular section** (maximum conveyance for given area): $b = 2y$, $R = y/2$, $A = 2y^2$.
- **Manning's equation:** $V = (1/n) R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ ŌĆö used universally for diversion channel design.
- **Lacey's scour depth formula:** $R = 0.47 (Q/f)^{1/3}$, with $f = 1.76 \sqrt{d_m}$ (note: $d_m$ in **mm**).
- **Local scour multiplier** at cofferdams/obstructions: typically **1.5 to 2.0** of the normal scour depth.
- **Cofferdam types:** Earthen, Rockfill, Sheet pile, Cellular ŌĆö selection depends on water depth and soil conditions.
- **Design of cofferdam** must check: (i) Sliding, (ii) Overturning, (iii) Piping, (iv) Scour at toe, (v) Seepage.
- **Diversion tunnel features:** Trash rack at inlet, concrete lining, bell-mouth entrance, stilling basin at outlet.
- **Permanent closure of tunnel:** Done with a **concrete plug** keyed 1.5 to 3.0 tunnel diameters into surrounding rock, with **pressure grouting** to seal seepage paths.
- **Froude number** must be checked: $Fr < 1$ (subcritical) for stable channel flow; $Fr > 1$ (supercritical) requires energy dissipation measures.
- **Freeboard** in diversion channels: typically **0.5 to 1.0 m** above design water surface.
- **Case studies to remember:** Bhakra Dam (2 tunnels of 9.14 m diameter), Tehri Dam (2 tunnels of ~1.5 km length), Sardar Sarovar (200 m wide diversion channel), Hirakud (28 m high earth cofferdams).
- **Continuous equation is the foundation** of all hydraulic design: $Q = A \cdot V$ ŌĆö never forget to state it first in any design problem.
- **Units check:** $Q$ in m┬│/s, $A$ in m┬▓, $V$ in m/s, $n$ dimensionless, $R$ in m, $S$ in m/m.

---

**End of Module 2 ŌĆö River Diversions | KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of River Training Works
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET747 - RIVER ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Examples of River Training Works # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What Are River Training Works? **River Training Works** are the engineered structures and measures constructed along a river or in its channel to guide, confine, and regulate the flow of water, stabilize the river course, prevent flood damages, and ensure the safety of adjacent lands, banks, and hydraulic structures (bridges, barrages, aqueducts, intakes). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** "River training works are the structural and non-structural measures implemented to provide a stable river channel with adequate waterway for the safe passage of design floods, to protect banks from erosion, to control sedimentation, and to ensure the desired alignment and regime of flow within the flood plains." ## 1.2 Why Are They Needed? ŌĆö The Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a long garden hose lying on a muddy slope with water gushing through it. Without support, the hose (river) wobbles, erodes the mud (banks), forms kinks (meanders), and may even burst its banks (flooding). If we pin the hose with stakes (groynes), place side walls (levees), and smooth out sharp bends (cut-offs), the water flows in a controlled, straight, and safe path. This is exactly what river training does to a natural river! **Primary Objectives of River Training Works:** 1. **Flood Protection** ŌĆö Confining the river to a defined section to prevent outflanking. 2. **Bank Protection** ŌĆö Preventing erosion of valuable agricultural/urban land. 3. **Stable Channel** ŌĆö Maintaining a defined low-flow channel for navigation and intakes. 4. **Protection of Structures** ŌĆö Safeguarding bridges, barrages, aqueducts, and irrigation works. 5. **Sediment Control** ŌĆö Regulating silt deposition and bed scour. 6. **Approach Flow** ŌĆö Ensuring smooth, axial flow at the entry to bridges/barrages. ## 1.3 Classification of River Training Works (The Master Map) River training works are broadly classified into two main categories, followed by a vast family of specific structural examples: ### A. Classical Classification (Indo-Gangetic Plain Perspective) | Category | Function | Key Examples | |----------|----------|--------------| | **High-Flood Training** | Confines river within banks during floods | Levees, Guide Bunds, Flood Embankments | | **Low-Flood Training** | Confines the low-flow (dry weather) channel | Groynes, Spurs, Dikes, Pitched Islands | ### B. Modern Functional Classification | Function | Structural Examples | |----------|---------------------| | **Bank Protection (Indirect)** | Groynes, Spurs, Jack fields, Porcupines | | **Bank Protection (Direct)** | Pitching, Revetments, Gabions, Bamboo cribs | | **Channel Improvement** | Artificial Cut-offs, Dredging, Channelization | | **Bed Stabilization** | Submerged Vanes, Bottom Panels, Sheet Piles | | **Approach Training** | Guide Bunds, Bell-mouth bunds, Mole heads | | **Sediment Management** | Silt excluders, Settling basins, Off-take structures | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Focus:** Questions are frequently asked on the *difference between High-Flood Training and Low-Flood Training* and the *selection of an appropriate training work for a given field situation*. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Examples with Engineering Logic The following are the **canonical examples** that a KTU 2024 examiner expects a student to describe. Each example is broken into *What*, *Why*, and *How* logic. --- ### Example 1: LEVEES (FLOOD EMBANKMENTS) **What:** Long, continuous earthen embankments constructed parallel to the river on either (or both) banks, raised above the expected high-flood level (HFL). **Why (Purpose):** - Confine the flood discharge to a defined cross-section. - Prevent spilling of water onto flood-plain croplands and habitations. - Reduce the wetted perimeter of flow ŌåÆ increase velocity ŌåÆ reduce silt deposition on the flood plain. **How (Design Logic):** The top of the levee is fixed using the **Standard Project Flood (SPF)** or **Probable Maximum Flood (PMF)** with a **freeboard (FB)**: $$\text{Levee Crest Level} = \text{HFL} + \text{Freeboard} + \text{Afflux due to levee construction}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Afflux** is the rise in water level upstream caused by the constriction of the flood plain by the levees themselves. Ignoring afflux is a classic valuation mistake! **Side Slopes & Construction:** - River-side slope: 2:1 to 3:1 (H:V) ŌĆö flatter because of wave attack. - Land-side slope: 1.5:1 to 2:1. - Core wall of puddle clay or impervious soil to prevent piping. - Stone pitching on the river face up to HFL. - Filter layers and drainage trenches on the land side to prevent seepage failure. --- ### Example 2: GROYNES (SPURS) **What:** Transverse structures (perpendicular or slightly inclined to the bank) projecting from the river bank into the flow, designed to deflect the current away from the bank and to induce controlled siltation between adjacent groynes. **Why (Purpose):** - Provide indirect bank protection. - Create a "still water zone" between two adjacent groynes where silt is deposited, eventually building a protective berm. - Train the river to a narrower, deeper, more hydraulically efficient section. **How (Design Logic):** A groyne has three functional zones: 1. **Head (nose)** ŌĆö rounded, subjected to maximum scour, needs deep stone pitching. 2. **Body (waist)** ŌĆö straight main length. 3. **Root** ŌĆö firmly embedded into the bank. **Types of Groynes:** | Type | Description | Application | |------|-------------|-------------| | **Impermeable (solid)** | Made of stone, masonry, or earth; blocks 100% of flow | Deep rivers, narrow channels, near bridges | | **Permeable** | Built of piles/bushes; allow partial flow | Wide, braided rivers, cost-effective | | **Retard (Dike)** | Low and partly submerged; only deflects at high stages | Shallow rivers, supplementary to main groynes | | **Repelling** | Pointing **upstream** (angle > 90┬░) | Used in special cases; aggressive deflection | | **Attracting** | Pointing **downstream** (angle < 90┬░ or 90┬░ itself) | Most common; silts up between groynes naturally | **Spacing Rule (KTU High-Yield):** $$S = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5) \times L$$ where $L$ is the length of the groyne projecting into the river. Closer spacing (1.5 L) is used for strong rivers; wider (2.5 L) for mild rivers. --- ### Example 3: GUIDE BUNDS (BELL-MOUTH BUNDS) **What:** A pair of specially shaped, curved embankments (like a bell or trumpet) constructed at the **upstream** approach to a hydraulic structure (bridge, barrage, weir, aqueduct) to smoothly guide the flood flow into the waterway without causing cross-currents, eddies, or heavy afflux. **Why (Purpose):** - Prevents outflanking of the bridge/barrage. - Provides a smooth, axially aligned entry of water ŌåÆ eliminates cross-flows. - Confines the river to a narrow "bell-mouth" entrance, ensuring the river cannot bypass the structure. **How (Design Logic):** - The **upstream end (mole head)** is curved, projecting upstream as a strong head. - The **downstream end (tail)** flares out and merges into the natural bank. - Length is typically **1.5 to 2 times the linear waterway (L)** of the bridge. - Radius of curvature at the nose: $R \approx 0.5 L$ to $L$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Note:** Guide bunds are sometimes confused with levees. The key distinction is that **guide bunds are short, paired, and align flow to a structure**, whereas **levees are long, continuous, and run along the river's length**. --- ### Example 4: DIKES (DYKES) / RETARDS **What:** Low, partly submerged, impermeable or permeable spurs constructed inside the river section, parallel to the bank, generally downstream of the main groynes or as **bandalling** aids. **Why (Purpose):** - Train the low-flow (dry-weather) channel. - Prevent shifting of the river from the desired alignment. - Often used in deltaic and braided rivers (e.g., Kosi, Ganga). --- ### Example 5: PITCHED ISLANDS (ARTIFICIAL ISLANDS / SILT PROMOTERS) **What:** Mid-channel artificial islands, either stone-pitched or built with boulder aprons, placed strategically in the middle of a wide river to: - Split the flow into two desired channels. - Cause silt deposition around them. - Force the river into a narrower, deeper central channel. **Why:** Used in **wide, shallow, braided rivers** (e.g., the Brahmaputra in Assam) where no single structure can effectively train the flow. --- ### Example 6: ARTIFICIAL CUT-OFFS **What:** A pilot channel (or fully excavated channel) cut across the neck of a meander loop, allowing the river to take a shorter, straighter course. The old loop is eventually blocked at both ends and becomes an **oxbow lake**. **Why:** - Increases channel slope ŌåÆ higher velocity ŌåÆ better sediment transport. - Reduces flood stages in the meandering reach. - Shortens the river length ŌåÆ faster flood passage. **How (Design Logic):** The new cut-off must be sized to carry **design discharge** at **regime slope**. The approach is governed by the **criterion of cut-off vs. natural meander**: > A cut-off is justified when the meander loop becomes *unstable* and the natural neck is *narrow and erodible*. **Lacey's Regime Slope for the Cut-off Channel:** $$S = \frac{0.000503 \cdot f^{5/3}}{Q^{1/6}}$$ where $f$ is Lacey's silt factor, $Q$ is discharge in cumecs. --- ### Example 7: SUBMERGED VANES (BOTTOM VANES) **What:** Small, thin, inclined plates installed on the **river bed** at the upstream approach of an intake/bridge pier. They generate secondary circulation that moves sediment **away** from the intake and toward the main flow. **Why:** Protect intakes (e.g., power canals, water supply) from silt entry without building large groynes. **How (Design Logic ŌĆö Odgaard & Kennedy Method):** $$H_v = (0.4 \text{ to } 0.5) \cdot \text{Local water depth}$$ $$\theta = 15┬░ \text{ to } 25┬░ \text{ (angle of inclination to flow)}$$ $$S_v = (10 \text{ to } 15) \cdot H_v \text{ (spacing, longitudinal)}$$ --- ### Example 8: PORCUPINES & JACK FIELDS **What:** - **Porcupines:** Three-legged steel/wooden frames (tripods) with pointed ends, placed on the bed to break up high-velocity flow and induce siltation. - **Jack fields:** A grid of slender, vertical steel rods (jack-bolts) driven into the river bed in an array pattern, again to dissipate energy and trap silt. **Why:** Cheap, semi-permanent measures for **bed protection and silt promotion** in wide, sandy rivers. Used in lower Ganga, Kosi, and Gandak systems. --- ### Example 9: BANK REVETMENT (DIRECT BANK PROTECTION) **What:** A continuous protective layer of stone, concrete blocks, geo-textiles, or gabions laid on the river bank to resist the shear stress of flowing water directly. **Why:** Where groynes are insufficient, the bank itself must be armored. Revetment is the **last line of defense**. **Design Rule ŌĆö Shields / Lane's Permissible Shear Stress:** $$\tau_c = 0.06 \cdot (\gamma_s - \gamma_w) \cdot d_{50}$$ where $d_{50}$ is the median stone size, $\gamma_s$ and $\gamma_w$ are unit weights of stone and water. > [!IMPORTANT] > If the bed shear stress $\tau_b = \gamma_w \cdot R \cdot S$ exceeds $\tau_c$, the riprap fails. This is a frequent KTU numerical question. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Equation List for Module 2 (Examples of River Training Works)** | # | Equation | Application | Variables & Units | |---|----------|-------------|-------------------| | 1 | $D = 1.5 \, q^{0.5}$ (Blench) | Scour depth below HFL | $q$ = discharge intensity (m┬│/s/m) | | 2 | $D = 1.34 \, q^{2/3}$ (Inglis) | Scour depth (alluvial rivers) | $q$ in m┬│/s/m | | 3 | $D = 0.473 \, (Q/f)^{1/3}$ (Lacey) | Maximum scour depth | $Q$ in m┬│/s, $f$ = silt factor (dimensionless) | | 4 | $P = 4.75 \, \sqrt{Q}$ (Lacey) | Wetted perimeter of regime channel | $Q$ in m┬│/s | | 5 | $S = 0.000503 \, f^{5/3} / Q^{1/6}$ (Lacey) | Regime slope | $f$ = silt factor, $Q$ in m┬│/s | | 6 | $S_{groyne} = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5) \cdot L$ | Spacing of groynes | $L$ = length of groyne in m | | 7 | $\tau_b = \gamma_w R S$ | Bed shear stress | $R$ = hydraulic radius (m), $S$ = slope | | 8 | $\tau_c = 0.06 (\gamma_s - \gamma_w) d_{50}$ | Critical shear for riprap | $d_{50}$ = median size (m) | | 9 | $\text{Crest} = \text{HFL} + FB + \text{Afflux}$ | Levee design level | $FB$ = freeboard (m) | | 10 | $H_v = 0.45 \cdot h$ (Odgaard) | Submerged vane height | $h$ = flow depth (m) | > **Rule of Thumb:** **Silt factor** $f = 1.76 \sqrt{d_{mm}}$ where $d_{mm}$ is median grain size in mm. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Regime Width and Scour Depth (Lacey's Theory) **Starting Point:** Lacey's empirical observations from stable alluvial channels in the Indo-Gangetic plain. The regime width and scour depth are derived from the **silt factor $f$** and the **design discharge $Q$**. ### Step 1: Silt Factor The silt factor is a dimensionless measure of the coarseness of the bed material. $$f = 1.76 \, \sqrt{d_{mm}}$$ where $d_{mm}$ is the median grain size in millimetres. ### Step 2: Regime Wetted Perimeter (Lacey) For a regime (self-formed, stable) channel, Lacey proposed: $$P = 4.75 \, \sqrt{Q}$$ where $P$ is the wetted perimeter in metres and $Q$ is the discharge in cumecs (m┬│/s). This equation **was obtained empirically** by plotting $P$ vs $\sqrt{Q}$ from observed regime channels ŌĆö slope of the best-fit line is 4.75. ### Step 3: Regime Area Lacey's area equation: $$A = 1.26 \, \frac{Q^{5/6}}{f^{1/3}}$$ This arises from the continuity $A = Q/V$ combined with Lacey's velocity formula $V = (1.55/f^{1/3}) \cdot Q^{1/6} \cdot R^{1/3}$ and $R = A/P$. ### Step 4: Regime Hydraulic Radius Combining $A$ and $P$: $$R = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{1.26}{4.75} \cdot \frac{Q^{5/6}}{f^{1/3} \, Q^{1/2}} = 0.265 \cdot \frac{Q^{1/3}}{f^{1/3}} = 0.265 \cdot \left(\frac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3}$$ ### Step 5: Maximum Scour Depth (Lacey's Formula) For a wide channel where $P \approx B$ (surface width), the regime scour depth below HFL is: $$D = 1.34 \cdot \left(\frac{q^2}{f}\right)^{1/3} \quad \text{(Lacey's Scour, wide channel form)}$$ Or equivalently, expressed in terms of total discharge: $$D = 0.473 \cdot \left(\frac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3}$$ ### Step 6: Worked Example **Problem:** A river has $Q = 4000$ m┬│/s, $d_{mm} = 0.5$ mm, $f = 1.76\sqrt{0.5} = 1.245$. Compute $P$, $D$, and $S$ (slope). $$ \begin{aligned} P &= 4.75 \cdot \sqrt{4000} \\ &= 4.75 \cdot 63.25 \\ &= 300.5 \text{ m} \\[6pt] D &= 0.473 \cdot \left(\frac{4000}{1.245}\right)^{1/3} \\ &= 0.473 \cdot (3212.8)^{1/3} \\ &= 0.473 \cdot 14.79 \\ &= 6.99 \approx 7.0 \text{ m} \\[6pt] S &= \frac{0.000503 \cdot (1.245)^{5/3}}{(4000)^{1/6}} \\ &= \frac{0.000503 \cdot 1.385}{4.31 \cdot 10^{0}} \\ &= \frac{6.97 \times 10^{-4}}{4.31} \\ &= 1.62 \times 10^{-4} \end{aligned} $$ **Answers:** $P = 300.5$ m, $D = 7.0$ m, $S = 1.62 \times 10^{-4}$ (Ōēł 1 in 6,175). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Groyne Spacing from Bend Theory **Starting Question:** Why must the spacing $S$ of two successive groynes be related to the groyne length $L$? ### Step 1: Flow Deflection A repelling/attracting groyne deflects the flow by an angle $\alpha$ from its original direction. The deflected jet re-attaches to the bank at a point downstream. ### Step 2: Reattachment Length From experimental fluid mechanics (analogous to flow past a flat plate): $$L_r \approx 6 \cdot L_{groyne}$$ This is the distance at which the flow re-attaches the bank if the groyne were the only disturbing element. To allow for the **bank curvature** and the **river's natural meander tendency**, the spacing is reduced. ### Step 3: Practical Spacing For an attracting groyne in a **straight, mild river**: $$S = (2.0 \text{ to } 2.5) \cdot L$$ For a **strong or curved river** (where the groyne must intercept high velocity): $$S = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \cdot L$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "1.5 to 2.5"?** A spacing of 3L or more allows the flow to re-attach to the bank **between** groynes, causing erosion of the unprotected bank and defeating the purpose. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Which Training Work to Choose? > [!NOTE] > **Engineer's Decision Matrix** (High-Yield for KTU Viva & ESE) | Site Condition | Best Training Work | Justification | |----------------|--------------------|---------------| | **Approach to a major bridge** | Guide Bunds | Smooth axial entry, no outflanking | | **Long, straight, aggrading reach** | Levees + Groynes | Confine flood + silt promotion | | **Sharp meander in narrow valley** | Artificial Cut-off | Increases slope, reduces length | | **Wide, braided, sandy river** | Pitched Islands + Dikes | Split flow, train low-flow channel | | **Bank erosion near a town** | Groynes + Revetment | Indirect then direct protection | | **Intake of a power canal** | Submerged Vanes | Move bed-load away from intake | | **Severely eroding outer bend** | Continuous Revetment | Direct armor needed | | **Deltaic distributary head** | Porcupines / Jack fields | Cheap, semi-permanent bed-armor | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: River Training Works > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of river training works ŌĆö the interaction between each example, the river flow, and the protected assets. ```mermaid flowchart TB RiverFlow["RIVER FLOW
with design discharge Q"]:::input RiverFlow --> HighFlood["HIGH-FLOOD TRAINING
confines Q within banks"]:::branch RiverFlow --> LowFlood["LOW-FLOOD TRAINING
confines dry-weather channel"]:::branch HighFlood --> Levees["LEVEES / EMBANKMENTS
parallel to bank, above HFL"]:::example HighFlood --> GuideBunds["GUIDE BUNDS
curved entry to structures"]:::example LowFlood --> Groynes["GROYNES / SPURS
transverse, silt-promoting"]:::example LowFlood --> Dikes["DIKES / RETARDS
low submerged spurs"]:::example LowFlood --> Islands["PITCHED ISLANDS
mid-channel silt promoters"]:::example Groynes --> ScourCheck{"SCOUR AT NOSE?"}:::decision ScourCheck -- "Yes" --> Revetment["STONE REVETMENT
at groyne head"]:::example ScourCheck -- "No" --> Siltation["SILTATION
between groynes"]:::success Levees --> AffluxCheck{"AFFLUX OK?"}:::decision AffluxCheck -- "No" --> RaiseCrest["RAISE LEVEE CREST
+ freeboard"]:::action AffluxCheck -- "Yes" --> ProtectFarm["PROTECT FARMLAND"]:::success GuideBunds --> Structure["PROTECTED STRUCTURE
bridge or barrage"]:::success Islands --> SiltTrap["MID-CHANNEL SILT TRAP"]:::success classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef branch fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 classDef example fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef action fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 classDef success fill:#d2cdf5,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Selection Logic for Training Works ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1["Problem:
Eroding bank in
wide alluvial river?"]:::q --> Q2{"Meander
developing?"}:::dec Q2 -- "Yes, sharp loop" --> A1["ARTIFICIAL CUT-OFF"]:::ans Q2 -- "No, straight reach" --> Q3{"Near a
bridge/barrage?"}:::dec Q3 -- "Yes" --> A2["GUIDE BUNDS +
STONE REVETMENT"]:::ans Q3 -- "No" --> Q4{"Bank material
cohesive?"}:::dec Q4 -- "Yes" --> A3["GROYNES
(permeable type)"]:::ans Q4 -- "No, sandy" --> A4["PITCHED ISLANDS
+ JACK FIELD"]:::ans classDef q fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef dec fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 classDef ans fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic ŌĆö Plan View of Groynes and Levees (Block Layout) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Bank1["RIVER BANK - LEFT SIDE"] L["LEVEE / EMBANKMENT
continuous, parallel to bank"]:::example end subgraph Channel["RIVER CHANNEL"] F1["FLOW DIRECTION -->"]:::input G1["GROYNE 1"]:::example G2["GROYNE 2"]:::example G3["GROYNE 3"]:::example S1["Silt berm
(deposition zone)"]:::success end subgraph Bank2["RIVER BANK - RIGHT SIDE"] L2["LEVEE / EMBANKMENT"]:::example end G1 --- S1 G2 --- S1 G3 --- S1 L1["SPACING S = 2L"]:::note L2_["LENGTH L (groyne)"]:::note classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef example fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef success fill:#d2cdf5,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000 classDef note fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between High-Flood Training and Low-Flood Training works. Give two examples of each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | High-Flood Training | Low-Flood Training | |--------|---------------------|---------------------| | Purpose | Confine the river during floods to a defined section | Confine the dry-weather flow to a defined channel | | Active during | Flood season (high stages) | Non-monsoon / low stages | | Design level | Above HFL + freeboard | At or slightly above LWL | | Examples | Levees, Guide Bunds, Flood Embankments | Groynes, Dikes, Pitched Islands, Porcupines | | Effect | Prevents flood damage to flood plain | Prevents bank erosion, deepens low-flow channel | > **Exam Tip:** A 3-mark answer must contain **at least 3 points of difference** and **2 examples each** for full marks. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **What is a guide bund? Why is it provided at the approach of a major bridge?** **Model Answer:** A **guide bund** is a curved earthen embankment constructed in pairs at the **upstream approach** to a bridge/barrage, shaped like a bell-mouth or trumpet. The upstream end is called the **mole head** and the downstream end merges into the natural bank. **Why provided:** 1. To guide the flood flow **smoothly and axially** into the bridge waterway, avoiding cross-currents. 2. To prevent the river from **outflanking** the bridge by changing course. 3. To **confine the flood** to a narrow bell-mouth, reducing afflux. 4. Length is generally **1.5 to 2 times** the linear waterway of the bridge. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]* **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **different types of groynes** used in river training. State the spacing rule between successive groynes. **(b) [7 Marks]** A river has a design discharge of **2500 m┬│/s** and median bed grain size of **0.4 mm**. Using **Lacey's formulae**, determine: 1. The regime wetted perimeter 2. The regime scour depth below HFL 3. The regime slope of the channel --- ### Model Answer to Q-A (a) **Types of Groynes (Classification):** 1. **Impermeable Groynes:** Constructed of stone, masonry, or earth; do not allow any flow through them. Used in deep, narrow rivers with strong currents. 2. **Permeable Groynes:** Built using piles, bushes, or cribs; allow partial seepage of flow, gradually reducing velocity and inducing siltation. More economical in wide, shallow rivers. 3. **Retards (Dikes):** Low-height, partly submerged impermeable spurs used in addition to main groynes. They only become effective at higher stages. 4. **Repelling Groynes:** Oriented **upstream** at an angle (i.e., the nose points upstream). Aggressively deflect flow toward the **opposite** bank. Used in special cases where the opposite bank is rocky/non-erodible. 5. **Attracting Groynes:** Oriented **downstream** (nose points downstream) or at right angle. They pull the flow **toward** the bank on which they are built, narrowing the active channel and promoting siltation between adjacent groynes. **Spacing Rule:** The spacing $S$ between two successive groynes must be: $$ S = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.5) \cdot L $$ where $L$ is the length of the groyne projecting into the river. The lower limit (1.5 L) is for **strong, deep rivers**; the upper limit (2.5 L) is for **mild, wide rivers**. Spacing beyond 3L defeats the purpose as the flow re-attaches the unprotected bank. --- ### Model Answer to Q-A (b) **Given:** $Q = 2500$ m┬│/s, $d_{mm} = 0.4$ mm **Step 1: Compute Silt Factor** $$f = 1.76 \sqrt{d_{mm}} = 1.76 \sqrt{0.4} = 1.76 \times 0.6325 = 1.113$$ **Step 2: Regime Wetted Perimeter (Lacey)** $$P = 4.75 \sqrt{Q} = 4.75 \sqrt{2500} = 4.75 \times 50 = 237.5 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3: Regime Scour Depth (Lacey)** $$D = 0.473 \left(\frac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3} = 0.473 \left(\frac{2500}{1.113}\right)^{1/3}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} D &= 0.473 \times (2246.2)^{1/3} \\ &= 0.473 \times 13.11 \\ &= 6.20 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Regime Slope (Lacey)** $$S = \frac{0.000503 \cdot f^{5/3}}{Q^{1/6}} = \frac{0.000503 \cdot (1.113)^{5/3}}{(2500)^{1/6}}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} (1.113)^{5/3} &= 1.113^{1.667} = 1.193 \\ Q^{1/6} &= 2500^{0.1667} = 3.394 \\[4pt] S &= \frac{0.000503 \times 1.193}{3.394} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}{3.394} = 1.77 \times 10^{-4} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answers:** - $P = 237.5$ m - $D = 6.20$ m - $S = 1.77 \times 10^{-4}$ (Ōēł 1 in 5,650) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam - May 2023]* **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a sketch, describe the **function and components of a guide bund system** at the approach to a major bridge. State the empirical design rules for its length and the radius of the curved mole head. **(b) [7 Marks]** A levee is to be constructed on a river reach where the design HFL is **102.50 m**, the afflux caused by the levee is estimated to be **0.85 m**, and the freeboard is **1.50 m**. The mean bed grain size is **0.6 mm**, and the peak discharge is **3000 m┬│/s**. Compute: 1. The crest level of the levee. 2. The Lacey regime scour depth (for the design of the levee foundation depth). 3. The hydraulic radius and regime width of the river. --- ### Model Answer to Q-B (a) **Function of Guide Bunds:** A guide bund is a curved, paired embankment constructed at the upstream of a bridge/barrage to: - Direct flood flow axially into the waterway. - Prevent the river from changing course and outflanking the structure. - Smooth the entry so that the flow distribution across the bridge is uniform. **Components (Block Diagram):** ``` MOLE HEAD (upstream nose) / \ / \ R = radius of curvature CURVED / \ PORTION / \ STRAIGHT PORTION (linear waterway) / \ / \ __________________________ | | | BRIDGE / BARRAGE | |________________________| | TAIL (merges with bank) ``` **Empirical Design Rules:** - Total length of guide bund: $L_{gb} = (1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \times L_{bridge}$ - Radius of curvature at the mole head: $R = (0.5 \text{ to } 1.0) \times L_{bridge}$ - Mole head is heavily stone-pitching protected, often with a boulder apron. - Crest level of guide bund = HFL + freeboard (similar to levee). --- ### Model Answer to Q-B (b) **Step 1: Crest Level of Levee** $$ \text{Crest} = \text{HFL} + \text{Afflux} + \text{Freeboard} $$ $$\text{Crest} = 102.50 + 0.85 + 1.50 = 104.85 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2: Lacey Scour Depth** Silt factor: $f = 1.76 \sqrt{0.6} = 1.76 \times 0.7746 = 1.363$ $$D = 0.473 \left(\frac{3000}{1.363}\right)^{1/3} = 0.473 \times (2201)^{1/3}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} 2201^{1/3} &\approx 12.99 \\ D &= 0.473 \times 12.99 = 6.14 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ So the levee toe must be founded **at least 6.14 m below HFL**, and stone-pitching must extend below this scour level. **Step 3: Regime Hydraulic Radius and Width** $$ \begin{aligned} R &= 0.265 \left(\frac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3} = 0.265 \times 12.99 = 3.44 \text{ m} \\[4pt] P &= 4.75 \sqrt{Q} = 4.75 \sqrt{3000} = 4.75 \times 54.77 = 260.2 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ For a wide channel $B \approx P$: $$B \approx 260 \text{ m}$$ **Final Answers:** - Levee crest = **104.85 m** - Scour depth $D = $ **6.14 m** - Hydraulic radius $R = $ **3.44 m**, Regime width $B \approx $ **260 m** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the afflux** in levee crest calculation ŌĆö this loses 1ŌĆō2 marks easily. > 2. **Using $B$ (width) instead of $P$ (wetted perimeter)** interchangeably ŌĆö for **wide channels** $B \approx P$ is OK, but for narrow/trapezoidal channels it is not. > 3. **Confusing groyne angle**: Attracting groynes point **downstream** (toward the river), repelling point **upstream** (toward the bank). This is a viva favourite. > 4. **Wrong unit for $d_{mm}$**: The silt factor formula uses grain size in **mm**, not in m. Mixing up units is a 2-mark error. > 5. **Spacing of groynes**: Writing $S = 3L$ or more is **wrong** ŌĆö flow re-attachment will erode the unprotected bank. > 6. **Guide bund vs. levee**: Many students describe guide bunds as "levees at bridges" ŌĆö the *bell-mouth shape* and *function of smooth entry* must be explicitly stated. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Examples of River Training Works** - ŌśÉ **Levees** = long, parallel, continuous embankments above HFL; designed for **HFL + Afflux + Freeboard**. - ŌśÉ **Groynes (Spurs)** = transverse structures; **attracting** type is most common; spacing = **(1.5 to 2.5)L**. - ŌśÉ **Guide Bunds** = **paired, curved, bell-mouth** embankments at **upstream** of bridges/barrages; length = **(1.5 to 2)L_bridge**. - ŌśÉ **Dikes (Retards)** = low, partly submerged spurs for **low-flow training**. - ŌśÉ **Pitched Islands** = mid-channel silt-promoters used in **wide, braided** rivers. - ŌśÉ **Artificial Cut-offs** = new straight channel across a meander neck; justified when loop is **unstable**. - ŌśÉ **Submerged Vanes** = bed-mounted vanes at intakes; **Odgaard & Kennedy** design: $H_v \approx 0.45 h$, angle **15┬░ŌĆō25┬░**. - ŌśÉ **Porcupines & Jack Fields** = cheap bed-armor in **sandy, deltaic** rivers. - ŌśÉ **Revetment** = **direct** bank armor; **Lane's critical shear**: $\tau_c = 0.06(\gamma_s - \gamma_w) d_{50}$. - ŌśÉ **Lacey's equations**: $P = 4.75\sqrt{Q}$, $D = 0.473(Q/f)^{1/3}$, $S = 0.000503 f^{5/3}/Q^{1/6}$. - ŌśÉ **Silt factor** $f = 1.76\sqrt{d_{mm}}$ ŌĆö always in **mm**. - ŌśÉ **High-Flood Training** ŌēĀ **Low-Flood Training** ŌĆö know the difference and the correct examples. - ŌśÉ Selection of training work depends on **river width, regime, location, and budget** ŌĆö always justify with the *why*.

topics.title: Properties of the Sediment, Settling Velocity, Incipient Motion, Critical Tractive Force, Empirical Equations, Scour Criteria, Shield's Analysis, White's Analysis, Regimes of Flow and Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET747 - RIVER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties of the Sediment, Settling Velocity, Incipient Motion, Critical Tractive Force, Empirical Equations, Scour Criteria, Shield's Analysis, White's Analysis, Regimes of Flow and Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Properties of Sediment

The engineering properties of sediment particles govern the entire behavior of a river ŌĆö from how easily grains are picked up by the flow, to how they settle, to how they erode river banks and bridge piers. The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies sediment properties into **Geometric, Gravitational, Kinematic, and Dynamic** categories.

### A. Geometric Properties (Size & Shape)

| Property | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Particle Size (d)** | Sieve diameter or fall diameter | $0.001$ mm ŌĆō $1000$ mm |
| **Shape Factor (SF)** | Ratio of surface area of sphere of same volume to actual surface area | $0.5$ ŌĆō $1.0$ |
| **Roundness (R)** | Average radius of curvature of corners divided by radius of inscribed circle | $0$ (angular) ŌĆō $1$ (perfectly round) |
| **Sphericity (╬©)** | Ratio of surface area of sphere (same volume) to surface area of particle | $0$ ŌĆō $1$ |
| **Angularity Number (AN)** | Percentage of a circle covered by re-entrants of corners | $0$ ŌĆō $100$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wentworth Scale of Classification (KTU High-Yield):**
> Clay < $0.004$ mm < Silt < $0.0625$ mm < Sand < $2$ mm < Gravel < $64$ mm < Cobble < $256$ mm < Boulder

### B. Gravitational Properties

- **Specific Gravity (G or $S$)**: Ratio of density of sediment ($\rho_s$) to density of water ($\rho_w$).
$$S = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_w}$$
For natural quartz sand, **$S \approx 2.65$**.
- **Bulk / Dry Unit Weight ($\gamma_d$)**: Weight of dry sediment per unit volume (accounts for voids).
- **Mass Density ($\rho_s$)**: For quartz, **$\rho_s = 2650 \text{ kg/m}^3$**.

### C. Kinematic Properties

- **Fall Velocity / Settling Velocity ($V_s$ or $\omega$)**: Terminal velocity of a particle falling in **still, quiescent fluid**.
- **Drag Coefficient ($C_D$)**: Function of Reynolds number of particle.

### D. Dynamic Properties

- **Angle of Repose ($\phi$)**: Maximum angle at which sediment remains stable without sliding.
  - Natural sand: **$30^\circ$ ŌĆō $37^\circ$**.
- **Porosity ($n$)**: Ratio of void volume to total volume ($0.25$ ŌĆō $0.50$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)**
> Think of sediment as a crowd of people in a bus station. **Size** is whether they are kids or adults (kids slip through gaps easily). **Shape** is whether they are standing straight or curled up (curled people block more space). **Specific gravity** is like body weight ŌĆö heavier people push the floor harder. **Angle of repose** is the steepest slope at which they can stand on stairs without sliding down. A river is essentially a moving bus station: the flow tries to "push" these particles downstream, and only the hydraulic "push" (tractive force) determines who gets carried away.

---

## 1.2 Settling Velocity ($V_s$ / $\omega$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** The *settling velocity* of a sediment particle is the constant terminal velocity attained by a particle falling in an infinite, stagnant fluid when the gravitational force is balanced by the drag force and the buoyancy-adjusted submerged weight.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine dropping a marble and a grain of rice in honey versus water. The **marble in water** falls fast (high $V_s$) because weight is large and viscous resistance is small. A **rice grain in honey** falls slowly because viscous resistance dominates. The transition between these two behaviors is the dividing line between **Stokes' Law** and **Newton's Law**.

---

## 1.3 Incipient Motion & Critical Tractive Force

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incipient Motion (Threshold of Motion):** The condition at which sediment particles on the bed **just begin to move** under the action of flowing water. The flow intensity (shear stress) at this stage is called the **Critical Tractive Force ($\tau_c$)** or **Critical Shear Stress ($\tau_{cr}$)**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of incipient motion like a stubborn mule. As long as the pulling force is small, the mule stands still. The moment the pull exceeds the friction between the mule's hooves and the ground, it moves ŌĆö that threshold pull is the **critical tractive force**. For sediment, the "friction" is the **submerged weight + cohesion + packing** of the bed material, and the "pull" is the **bed shear stress exerted by the flow**.

---

## 1.4 Scour, Shields' Analysis, White's Analysis, and Flow Regimes

- **Scour**: Removal of bed material around an obstruction (bridge pier, abutment) or due to localized flow acceleration.
- **Shields' Analysis (1936)**: A dimensionless framework that identifies threshold of motion by plotting the **Shields Parameter ($\tau_*$)** against the **Boundary Reynolds Number ($Re_*$)**.
- **White's Analysis (1940)**: An alternative, more direct method giving critical tractive force in terms of **median grain size ($d_{50}$)** ŌĆö practical for engineering design.
- **Regimes of Flow**: Classification of flow around grains as **Hydraulically Smooth, Transitional, or Fully Rough**, governed by the roughness Reynolds number.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Settling Velocity ŌĆö The Two Regimes

When a particle falls through water, two forces dominate:
1. **Submerged weight (downward):** $F_g = (\rho_s - \rho_w) g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d^3$
2. **Drag force (upward):** $F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_w V_s^2}{2}$

At **terminal velocity**, $F_g = F_D$, which gives:

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{4gd(S-1)}{3C_D}}$$

The value of $C_D$ depends on the **Particle Reynolds Number** $Re_p = \frac{V_s d}{\nu}$:

| Regime | Condition | Drag Law | $C_D$ Expression |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stokes (Laminar)** | $Re_p < 0.1$ (clay, silt) | $C_D = \frac{24}{Re_p}$ | $V_s = \frac{g d^2 (S-1)}{18\nu}$ |
| **Transition** | $0.1 < Re_p < 1000$ | Empirical curve | Use Figure |
| **Newton (Turbulent)** | $Re_p > 1000$ (gravel) | $C_D \approx 0.4$ (constant) | $V_s = 1.82 \sqrt{gd(S-1)}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Subtlety:** The transition regime is solved by **iteration** ŌĆö guess $V_s$, compute $Re_p$, read $C_D$ from the standard $C_D$ vs. $Re_p$ curve, re-compute $V_s$, repeat until convergence.

### Rubey's Equation (Empirical, used in KTU problems)
A widely used empirical formula (valid across all regimes):

$$V_s = \sqrt{gd(S-1)} \cdot \left[ \sqrt{\frac{2}{3} + \frac{36\nu^2}{g d^3 (S-1)}} - \sqrt{\frac{36\nu^2}{g d^3 (S-1)}} \right]$$

This is commonly adopted when $C_D$ is unknown for intermediate sizes.

---

## 2.2 Incipient Motion ŌĆö Force Balance Approach

Consider a grain of diameter $d$ on a flat bed. Forces acting:
- **Drag force** $F_D = C_D \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \tau_{cr}$ (proportional to bed shear stress)
- **Lift force** $F_L = C_L \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \tau_{cr}$ (helps lift the particle)
- **Submerged weight** $W_s = (\rho_s - \rho_w) g \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$

At incipient motion, the moments about the pivot point balance:

$$F_D \cdot \frac{d}{2} + F_L \cdot \frac{d}{6} = W_s \cdot \frac{d}{6}$$

This reduces to a critical shear stress:

$$\tau_{cr} = \frac{W_s}{K_1 d^2 + K_2 d^2} = K \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) g d$$

where $K$ is a dimensionless constant (calibrated experimentally).

---

## 2.3 Shields' Analysis ŌĆö The Dimensionless Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shields' Critical Shear Stress (Dimensionless Form):**
> $$\tau_{cr}^* = \frac{\tau_{cr}}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g d}$$
> 
> Shields found experimentally that $\tau_{cr}^*$ is **NOT a constant** ŌĆö it depends on the **Boundary Reynolds Number** $Re_* = \frac{u_* d}{\nu}$, where $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_{cr}/\rho_w}$ is the shear velocity.

The Shields curve (1936) is the foundational graph of sediment transport. It has three regions:

1. **Laminar sublayer regime** ($Re_* < 2$): $\tau_{cr}^*$ rises steeply (cohesive effect dominates).
2. **Transition regime** ($2 < Re_* < 70$): $\tau_{cr}^* \approx 0.03$ (approximately constant minimum).
3. **Fully rough regime** ($Re_* > 70$): $\tau_{cr}^*$ rises logarithmically.

**For coarse, non-cohesive sediment (sand, gravel), the asymptotic value is:**

$$\tau_{cr}^* \approx 0.047 \quad \text{(Shields' design value, often rounded to 0.06 for safety)}$$

---

## 2.4 White's Equation (Practical Design)

White (1940) proposed a direct formula relating critical shear stress to grain size for **uniform, non-cohesive sediments**:

$$\tau_{cr} = 0.18 \, d \quad \text{(SI: Pa, with d in mm; in CGS: } \tau_{cr} = 0.18 d, \text{ d in cm)}$$

Or, more precisely, White's curves give:

| Sediment Size $d$ (mm) | $\tau_{cr}$ (N/m┬▓) |
|---|---|
| $0.1$ | $0.16$ |
| $0.5$ | $0.80$ |
| $1.0$ | $1.58$ |
| $5.0$ | $7.20$ |
| $10.0$ | $13.50$ |

White's approach is **direct and engineer-friendly**, while Shields' is **theoretically rigorous**.

---

## 2.5 Scour Criteria

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Local Scour** is the excavation of bed material around hydraulic structures. The KTU curriculum emphasizes:
> - **Clear-Water Scour:** No upstream sediment transport; scour hole develops until $\tau_b \le \tau_c$.
> - **Live-Bed Scour:** Sediment is continuously supplied; scour hole depth oscillates with passage of bedforms.

### Empirical Scour Equations

| Equation | Source | Application |
|---|---|---|
| $d_s = 1.5 q^{0.6}$ | Blench | Local scour at piers |
| $d_s = 1.11 B^{0.5} y_1^{0.5}$ | Larras | Pier scour in cohesionless bed |
| $d_s/y_1 = 1.7 (B/y_1)^{0.78}$ | CSU HEC-18 | Bridge pier design |
| $d_s = 0.473 \left( \frac{Q}{f}\right)^{1/3}$ | Lacey's | Regime scour depth |

Where $B$ = pier width, $y_1$ = approach depth, $Q$ = discharge, $f$ = Lacey's silt factor.

---

## 2.6 Regimes of Flow & Resistance

The flow regime around a grain is determined by the **Roughness Reynolds Number**:

$$k_s^+ = \frac{u_* k_s}{\nu}$$

| Regime | $k_s^+$ Range | Flow Character |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydraulically Smooth** | $k_s^+ < 4$ | Grain submerged in viscous sublayer; friction depends only on Re |
| **Transitional** | $4 < k_s^+ < 70$ | Grains partly protruding from sublayer |
| **Fully Rough** | $k_s^+ > 70$ | Grains fully exposed; friction depends only on relative roughness $k_s/R_h$ |

### Friction Factor Correlations

**Smooth flow (PrandtlŌĆōvon K├Īrm├Īn):**
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2 \log_{10} \left( Re \sqrt{f} \right) - 0.8$$

**Fully rough (ColebrookŌĆōWhite explicit form, KTU standard):**
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10} \left( \frac{k_s}{3.7 \cdot 4R_h} \right)$$

Or, simpler: $\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2 \log_{10} \left( \frac{12 R_h}{k_s} \right)$ (Keulegan).

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Cheat Sheet ŌĆö Save This for Exam Day!**

| # | Formula | Description | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \rho_s/\rho_w$ | Specific gravity | Sediment classification |
| 2 | $V_s = \frac{g d^2 (S-1)}{18\nu}$ | Stokes' settling | $d < 0.1$ mm |
| 3 | $V_s = 1.82\sqrt{gd(S-1)}$ | Newton's settling | $d > 2$ mm |
| 4 | Rubey's equation | Settling velocity (all ranges) | Exam-standard |
| 5 | $\tau_{cr}^* = \frac{\tau_{cr}}{(\rho_s - \rho_w)gd}$ | Shields' parameter | Threshold of motion |
| 6 | $\tau_{cr} \approx 0.06 (\rho_s - \rho_w) g d$ | Shields' design | Coarse sediment |
| 7 | $\tau_{cr} = 0.18 d$ (mm) | White's | Direct design |
| 8 | $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_b/\rho_w}$ | Shear velocity | Boundary layer |
| 9 | $Re_* = u_* d / \nu$ | Boundary Reynolds | Shields' chart |
| 10 | $k_s^+ = u_* k_s / \nu$ | Roughness Reynolds | Flow regime |
| 11 | $d_s/y_1 = 1.7(B/y_1)^{0.78}$ | CSU pier scour | Bridge design |
| 12 | $f_s = 1.76 d_{75}^{1/2}$ | Strickler | Manning's n for coarse bed |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Engineers**: Use Shields/White to design stable piers against scour.
- **Dam Engineers**: Settling velocity governs the design of **settling basins** for sediment removal.
- **River Trainers**: Flow regime determines whether bedform (ripple/dune) resistance or grain resistance dominates.
- **Irrigation Channels**: Kennedy's and Lacey's silt theories build directly on settling velocity.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Stokes' Settling Velocity (Laminar Regime)

### Step 1 ŌĆö Force balance at terminal velocity

A spherical particle falling at terminal velocity $V_s$ in a viscous fluid is acted upon by:
- Net submerged weight (downward): $F_g = (\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d^3$
- Viscous drag (upward, Stokes): $F_D = 3 \pi \mu d V_s$

At terminal (constant) velocity, $F_g = F_D$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Equate the two forces

$$(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 = 3 \pi \mu d V_s$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify

$$V_s = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g d^2}{18 \mu} = \frac{(S-1) g d^2}{18 \nu}$$

where $\nu = \mu/\rho_w$ is the **kinematic viscosity of water**.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Validity check

This is valid only when $Re_p = \frac{V_s d}{\nu} < 0.1$, i.e., for **fine silt and clay**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** $V_s \propto d^2$ in Stokes' regime ŌĆö doubling the diameter increases fall velocity **four-fold**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Newton's Settling Velocity (Turbulent Regime)

### Step 1 ŌĆö Force balance with turbulent drag

For high Reynolds numbers, the drag is purely pressure/form drag:

$$F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w V_s^2}{2}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Equate to submerged weight

$$(\rho_s - \rho_w) g \frac{\pi d^3}{6} = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_w V_s^2}{2}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify

$$V_s^2 = \frac{4 g d (S-1)}{3 C_D}$$

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 g d (S-1)}{3 C_D}}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Insert $C_D \approx 0.4$ (constant for spheres in turbulent flow)

$$V_s = \sqrt{\frac{4 g d (S-1)}{3 \times 0.4}} = \sqrt{\frac{10 g d (S-1)}{3}}$$

$$V_s = 1.82 \sqrt{g d (S-1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** $V_s \propto \sqrt{d}$ in Newton's regime ŌĆö doubling the diameter increases fall velocity by only **1.41 times**. This is because larger grains are more efficiently slowed by turbulent eddies.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Shields' Critical Shear Stress

### Step 1 ŌĆö Particle on a flat bed, force/moment analysis

A spherical particle of diameter $d$ on a flat riverbed experiences:
- Drag force $F_D = C_D \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \tau_{cr}$ (horizontal, applied at center)
- Lift force $F_L = C_L \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \tau_{cr}$ (vertical, applied at center)
- Submerged weight $W_s = (\rho_s - \rho_w) g \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$ (at center)
- Normal reaction $N$ and friction $fN$ at the contact point.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Take moments about the pivot (point of contact)

Moments causing motion (overturning):
$$M_o = F_D \cdot \frac{d}{2} + F_L \cdot \frac{d}{6}$$

Moments resisting motion (stabilizing):
$$M_r = (W_s - F_L) \cdot \frac{d}{6}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Equate moments at incipient motion

$$F_D \cdot \frac{d}{2} + F_L \cdot \frac{d}{6} = (W_s - F_L) \cdot \frac{d}{6}$$

Multiply by $6/d$:

$$3 F_D + F_L = W_s - F_L \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3 F_D + 2 F_L = W_s$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute $F_D$ and $F_L$ in terms of $\tau_{cr}$

$$3 \left( C_D \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \tau_{cr} \right) + 2 \left( C_L \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \tau_{cr} \right) = (\rho_s - \rho_w) g \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for $\tau_{cr}$

$$\frac{\pi d^2 \tau_{cr}}{4} (3 C_D + 2 C_L) = \frac{\pi d^3}{6} (\rho_s - \rho_w) g$$

$$\tau_{cr} = \frac{2 d (\rho_s - \rho_w) g}{3 (3 C_D + 2 C_L)}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Define Shields parameter

$$\tau_{cr}^* = \frac{\tau_{cr}}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g d} = \frac{2}{3 (3 C_D + 2 C_L)}$$

The right side is a function of $Re_* = u_* d/\nu$, since $C_D$ and $C_L$ depend on the boundary Reynolds number. This functional relationship is the **Shields Curve**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asymptotic Result:** For $Re_* > 70$ (fully rough regime, coarse non-cohesive sediment):
> $$\tau_{cr}^* \approx 0.047 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_{cr} = 0.047 (\rho_s - \rho_w) g d$$
> The 0.06 value used in design is a safety factor accounting for non-uniformity, turbulence, and unsteady flow.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: White's Equation (Semi-Empirical)

White (1940) collected experimental data on incipient motion of uniform sand and gravel, and plotted critical tractive force against median grain size. He fitted:

$$\tau_{cr} = a \cdot d_{50}$$

With $a$ ranging from $0.085$ to $0.21$ depending on the specific value chosen by the designer (commonly **$a = 0.18$** for design):

$$\boxed{\tau_{cr} = 0.18 \, d_{50} \quad \text{(d in mm, } \tau_{cr} \text{ in N/m}^2\text{)}}$$

For metric (m):
$$\tau_{cr} = 180 \, d_{50} \quad (d_{50} \text{ in m, } \tau_{cr} \text{ in N/m}^2)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** Shields gives $\tau_{cr} = 0.047 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.65 \times d = 760 d$ (d in m). White gives $180 d$. White's is more conservative for design.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A river has uniform bed sand with $d = 0.5$ mm, $S = 2.65$, $T = 20^\circ$C ($\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m┬▓/s). Compute the settling velocity using (a) Stokes, (b) Newton, and (c) Rubey's equations. Comment on accuracy.

### (a) Stokes' Law

$$V_s = \frac{(2.65-1) \times 9.81 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{18 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$V_s = \frac{1.65 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{4.047 \times 10^{-6}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$V_s = 0.0225 \text{ m/s} = 22.5 \text{ mm/s}$$

Check $Re_p = \frac{V_s d}{\nu} = \frac{0.0225 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-6}} = 11.25$

> ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ $Re_p = 11.25 > 0.1$ ŌåÆ **Stokes is INVALID**. Result is unreliable.

### (b) Newton's Law

$$V_s = 1.82 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.65}$$

$$V_s = 1.82 \sqrt{8.094 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.82 \times 0.08997 = 0.1637 \text{ m/s}$$

Check $Re_p = \frac{0.1637 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-6}} = 81.85$

> ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ $Re_p = 81.85 < 1000$ ŌåÆ **Newton is at the limit**. Slightly overestimates.

### (c) Rubey's Equation

Compute dimensionless term:
$$\frac{36 \nu^2}{g d^3 (S-1)} = \frac{36 \times 10^{-12}}{9.81 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^3 \times 1.65}$$

$$= \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-11}}{9.81 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.65} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-11}}{2.024 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.0178$$

$$V_s = \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.65} \cdot \left[ \sqrt{\frac{2}{3} + 0.0178} - \sqrt{0.0178} \right]$$

$$= 0.08997 \cdot \left[ \sqrt{0.6845} - 0.1335 \right] = 0.08997 \cdot [0.8273 - 0.1335]$$

$$= 0.08997 \times 0.6938 = 0.0624 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rubey's gives $V_s \approx 62.4$ mm/s**, which is the correct answer for this intermediate-size particle. The iterative method (with proper $C_D$ vs. $Re_p$ curve) would yield a value in the range 60ŌĆō70 mm/s.
