# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define settling velocity. State Stokes' law for settling velocity of a fine sediment particle and write the condition of its validity.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Settling velocity ($V_s$ or $\omega$):** It is the constant terminal velocity attained by a sediment particle falling in an infinite, still fluid when the effective submerged weight is balanced by the drag force.

> **Stokes' Law:** $V_s = \dfrac{g d^2 (S-1)}{18 \nu}$ where $d$ = particle diameter, $S$ = specific gravity, $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity.

> **Validity:** $Re_p = \dfrac{V_s d}{\nu} < 0.1$ (laminar flow around the particle, applies to clays and fine silts with $d < 0.1$ mm approximately).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the Shields parameter? Briefly describe the Shields curve.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Shields Parameter:** Dimensionless critical shear stress defined as:
> $$\tau_{cr}^* = \frac{\tau_{cr}}{(\rho_s - \rho_w) g d}$$
> It represents the ratio of the hydraulic force on a bed particle to its submerged weight.

> **Shields Curve:** A plot of $\tau_{cr}^*$ against the **Boundary Reynolds Number** $Re_* = \dfrac{u_* d}{\nu}$ ($u_*$ = shear velocity). The curve has three regions: (i) laminar ($Re_* < 2$, $\tau_{cr}^*$ rises steeply), (ii) transition ($2 < Re_* < 70$, $\tau_{cr}^* \approx 0.03$ minimum), (iii) fully rough ($Re_* > 70$, $\tau_{cr}^*$ rises logarithmically, asymptoting to 0.047). It is the fundamental design chart for predicting incipient motion.

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö On Settling Velocity & Regimes of Flow

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Derive the expression for the settling velocity of a fine sediment particle under Stokes' regime and a coarse sediment particle under Newton's regime. State the valid range of Reynolds number for each.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A river carries uniform sediment of $d = 1.5$ mm, $S = 2.65$, water temperature $25^\circ$C ($\nu = 0.9 \times 10^{-6}$ m┬▓/s). Compute the settling velocity by:
(i) Stokes' law
(ii) Newton's law
(iii) Rubey's equation
Comment on the validity of each result.

---

#### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation**

> *Already covered in SECTION 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes. Students should reproduce the step-by-step force balance:*
> - Equate submerged weight to drag: $(\rho_s - \rho_w) g \frac{\pi d^3}{6} = C_D \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \frac{\rho_w V_s^2}{2}$
> - Stokes regime ($C_D = 24/Re_p$): $V_s = \frac{g d^2 (S-1)}{18 \nu}$, valid for $Re_p < 0.1$.
> - Newton regime ($C_D = 0.4$): $V_s = 1.82 \sqrt{g d (S-1)}$, valid for $Re_p > 1000$.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $d = 1.5$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $S = 2.65$, $\nu = 0.9 \times 10^{-6}$ m┬▓/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓.

**(i) Stokes' Law:**

$$V_s = \frac{g d^2 (S-1)}{18 \nu} = \frac{9.81 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 1.65}{18 \times 0.9 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$V_s = \frac{9.81 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.65}{1.62 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{3.642 \times 10^{-5}}{1.62 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.249 \text{ m/s}$$

Check: $Re_p = \frac{V_s d}{\nu} = \frac{2.249 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9 \times 10^{-6}} = 3748$

> ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ $Re_p = 3748 \gg 0.1$ ŌåÆ **Stokes' Law is INVALID**. The result is grossly overestimated.

**(ii) Newton's Law:**

$$V_s = 1.82 \sqrt{g d (S-1)} = 1.82 \sqrt{9.81 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.65}$$

$$V_s = 1.82 \sqrt{0.02428} = 1.82 \times 0.1558 = 0.2836 \text{ m/s}$$

Check: $Re_p = \frac{0.2836 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9 \times 10^{-6}} = 472.6$

> ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ $Re_p = 472.6$ is in the **transition regime** (between 0.1 and 1000). Newton's Law gives an approximate result.

**(iii) Rubey's Equation:**

Compute dimensionless term:
$$\frac{36 \nu^2}{g d^3 (S-1)} = \frac{36 \times (0.9 \times 10^{-6})^2}{9.81 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^3 \times 1.65}$$

$$= \frac{36 \times 8.1 \times 10^{-13}}{9.81 \times 3.375 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.65} = \frac{2.916 \times 10^{-11}}{5.462 \times 10^{-8}} = 5.34 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$V_s = \sqrt{0.02428} \left[ \sqrt{\frac{2}{3} + 5.34 \times 10^{-4}} - \sqrt{5.34 \times 10^{-4}} \right]$$

$$= 0.1558 \times [0.8172 - 0.0231] = 0.1558 \times 0.7941 = 0.1237 \text{ m/s}$$

**Comments on Validity:**

| Method | $V_s$ (m/s) | $Re_p$ | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stokes | $2.249$ | $3748$ | ŌØī Invalid ŌĆö Re way above $0.1$ |
| Newton | $0.2836$ | $473$ | ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Approximate ŌĆö Re in transition |
| Rubey | $0.1237$ | $\sim 206$ | Ō£ģ Most reliable empirical method |

> **Rubey's value of $0.124$ m/s is the recommended answer.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö On Shields' Analysis, Critical Tractive Force & Scour

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Derive the Shields' critical shear stress parameter by force/moment analysis of a bed particle. State the asymptotic value for coarse, non-cohesive sediment.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A bridge pier of width $B = 2$ m is located in a river with $y_1 = 4$ m approach depth. The bed material has $d_{50} = 0.5$ mm, $S = 2.65$. Compute the local scour depth using the CSU HEC-18 equation. Compare with White's critical tractive force method. Comment on the design implications.

---

#### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation**

> *Reproduce the step-by-step moment-balance derivation from SECTION 3.3. The key result is:*
> $$\tau_{cr}^* = \frac{2}{3(3C_D + 2C_L)} \approx 0.047 \text{ for fully rough regime}$$
> $$\Rightarrow \tau_{cr} = 0.047 (\rho_s - \rho_w) g d$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $B = 2$ m, $y_1 = 4$ m, $d_{50} = 0.5$ mm, $S = 2.65$.

**(i) CSU HEC-18 Scour Equation:**

$$\frac{d_s}{y_1} = 1.7 \left( \frac{B}{y_1} \right)^{0.78}$$

$$\frac{d_s}{4} = 1.7 \left( \frac{2}{4} \right)^{0.78} = 1.7 \times (0.5)^{0.78} = 1.7 \times 0.5822 = 0.9897$$

$$d_s = 0.9897 \times 4 = 3.96 \text{ m}$$

> So, **local scour depth $\approx 4.0$ m**.

**(ii) White's Critical Tractive Force:**

$$\tau_{cr} = 0.18 \times d_{50} = 0.18 \times 0.5 = 0.09 \text{ N/m}^2$$

The bed shear stress from the approach flow $\tau_b = \rho_w g R_h S_0$. If $\tau_b > \tau_{cr}$, scour will occur.

**Shields' Design Critical Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_{cr} = 0.06 (\rho_s - \rho_w) g d = 0.06 \times 1000 \times 1.65 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\tau_{cr} = 0.486 \text{ N/m}^2$$

This is much higher than White's $0.09$ N/m┬▓, but it represents the **threshold for motion of the bed material itself**, not the localized scour around the pier.

**Design Implications:**

| Aspect | Value | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| CSU scour depth | $3.96$ m | Foundation must be placed below $\sim 8$ m (factor of safety $\approx 2$) |
| White's $\tau_{cr}$ | $0.09$ N/m┬▓ | Very fine sand ŌĆö easily moved by low flows |
| Shields' $\tau_{cr}$ | $0.486$ N/m┬▓ | Bed material threshold |

> **Conclusion:** The pier must be founded **at least 8 m below mean bed level** to account for the $\sim 4$ m scour depth. The fine sand ($0.5$ mm) makes the bed **highly erodible**, so riprap protection or a deeper foundation may be necessary.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Reynolds number check** after applying Stokes' or Newton's law ŌĆö the examiner will deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks if $Re_p$ is not verified.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** in White's equation (must convert $d$ to mm; many students use m and get a wrong answer).
> 3. **Confusing Shields' parameter with critical shear stress** ŌĆö the parameter is dimensionless; the shear stress has units of N/m┬▓.
> 4. **Forgetting the safety factor** of 0.06 (instead of 0.047) in design ŌĆö examiners expect the conservative value.
> 5. **Mixing up** clear-water and live-bed scour equations in the HEC-18 formula.
> 6. **Not mentioning flow regime** when computing $V_s$ ŌĆö always state whether the result is Stokes, Newton, or transition.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This!)**

- **Sediment classification** uses Wentworth scale; $S \approx 2.65$ for quartz.
- **Stokes' law** valid for $Re_p < 0.1$ (very fine particles, $d < 0.1$ mm).
- **Newton's law** valid for $Re_p > 1000$ (gravel, $d > 2$ mm).
- **Rubey's equation** is the go-to empirical formula for **all** particle sizes in KTU problems.
- **Shields parameter** $\tau_{cr}^* = \tau_{cr} / [(\rho_s - \rho_w) g d]$ is dimensionless and a function of $Re_*$.
- **Asymptotic Shields value** = 0.047 (theoretical), 0.06 (design).
- **White's equation** = $\tau_{cr} = 0.18 d$ (mm) ŌĆö a direct, conservative design tool.
- **Flow regimes** determined by $k_s^+ = u_* k_s / \nu$:
   - Smooth: $< 4$, Transitional: $4$ŌĆō$70$, Fully rough: $> 70$.
- **CSU HEC-18 pier scour**: $d_s/y_1 = 1.7 (B/y_1)^{0.78}$.
- **Boundary Reynolds number** $Re_* = u_* d/\nu$ is the independent variable of the Shields chart.
- **Always verify** the regime (laminar/transition/turbulent) by computing $Re_p$ or $Re_*$ before applying a formula.
- **Angle of repose** governs bank stability; **porosity** affects bed density.
- **Live-bed scour** vs. **clear-water scour** ŌĆö distinguish clearly in viva questions.
- **Friction factor** in fully rough regime: $\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = 2 \log_{10} (12 R_h / k_s)$.
- **Shape factor** and **roundness** affect $C_D$ ŌĆö non-spherical particles fall slower.
- **KTU exam tip**: Draw the Shields curve, label the three regions, and mark the operating point ŌĆö full marks guaranteed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bed Form Mechanics, Design of Stable Channels - Garret's Method, Bed Load Transport and its Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET747 - RIVER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bed Form Mechanics, Design of Stable Channels - Garret's Method, Bed Load Transport and its Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bed Form Mechanics ŌĆö The Foundational Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Bed forms* are the geometric configurations that the loose alluvial boundary of a river or channel assumes under the action of flowing water, governed by the interplay between tractive shear stress, flow velocity, and grain size. They represent a self-organized equilibrium between sediment transport capacity and flow energy dissipation.

The mechanism is essentially a **hydrodynamic instability**: when water flows over an erodible bed, small perturbations grow into organized geometric patterns (ripples ŌåÆ dunes ŌåÆ plane bed ŌåÆ antidunes), each representing a distinct mode of energy transfer between the fluid and the sediment.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine blowing across the surface of a sand dune at the beach. With a gentle breeze, you see small **ripples** form. If you blow harder, the ripples merge into larger **dunes** that slowly migrate downwind. Blow extremely hard, and the sand surface becomes glassy flat, or even throws up **antidunes** where crests and troughs are in phase with surface water waves. This is exactly what happens beneath a river ŌĆö the "wind" is the river's shear stress, the "sand" is the bed sediment, and the "blow strength" is the **Froude number** ($Fr$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Bed Forms Matter in KTU/Engineering Practice:**
> - They govern **flow resistance (Manning's n)** ŌĆö dunes can triple the roughness compared to a plane bed.
> - They determine **bed load transport rate** ŌĆö different bed forms yield vastly different sediment flux.
> - They control **channel stability** ŌĆö antidunes signal imminent scour or flooding.

---

## 1.2 Design of Stable Channels ŌĆö Garret's Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *stable channel* (also called a *threshold channel* or *regime channel*) is one in which the bed and banks neither erode nor silt up over a long period, i.e., the boundary shear stress is just at the critical threshold required to move the bed material. **Garret's Method** is an empirical graphical/analytical procedure for determining the permissible tractive force on non-cohesive, non-ripple-forming bed material of various sizes.

Garret's method is essentially a **modernized, expanded version of Lane's tractive force approach**, providing a family of curves (Garret's chart) relating the **critical tractive force** $\tau_c$ to the **median grain size** $d$ of bed material, valid for a wide range of particle sizes from fine sand to coarse gravel.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of trying to push a heavy box across a floor. The force required depends on how heavy the box is. Similarly, a river's "push" (tractive force) must be sufficient to overcome a sediment grain's weight. Garret's chart is essentially a **"pushing-force calculator"** that tells you the exact push needed to just barely start moving each size of grain. Design the river so its push is *just below* this threshold, and the channel remains stable indefinitely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Stability Condition (Garret's Criterion):**
> $$\boxed{\tau_o \leq \tau_c \cdot K}$$
> where $\tau_o$ = actual tractive force on the bed, $\tau_c$ = critical tractive force from Garret's chart, and $K$ = correction factor for side slopes, bend curvature, etc.

---

## 1.3 Bed Load Transport and its Estimation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Bed load* is the fraction of the total sediment load that moves by **rolling, sliding, or saltation** along the channel bed, in continuous or nearly continuous contact with the bed. It is distinguished from *suspended load*, which travels within the water column supported by turbulence.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Picture a busy hiking trail covered in pebbles. Hikers' feet dislodge pebbles, which roll and bounce down the trail (rolling/saltating) but never become airborne above the path. This is **bed load**. Now imagine a dust storm ŌĆö the dust is held aloft by turbulent eddies ŌĆö that is **suspended load**. In a river, the "feet" are the water's shear stress, the "trail" is the bed, and the "bouncing pebbles" are the bed load particles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Bed Load Proportions (KTU Standard Values in Bold):**
> - **Coarse gravel rivers (mountain streams):** bed load may constitute **40ŌĆō100%** of total load.
> - **Sand-bed rivers (plains):** bed load is typically **5ŌĆō25%** of total load.
> - **Silt/clay rivers (lowland):** bed load often **< 5%** of total load.
> The dimensionless **Shields parameter** $\theta$ and **Froude number** $Fr$ govern the transition.

---

## 1.4 Real-World Examples & Engineering Significance

| River/Project | Bed Form Observed | Design Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Mississippi River (USA)** | Large dunes (1ŌĆō3 m height) | Manning's n rises to **0.020ŌĆō0.030**; flood levels rise |
| **Colorado River (USA)** | Ripples, antidunes in rapids | Navigability and dam-scour predictions |
| **Brahmaputra (India)** | Dunes + mega-ripples | Severe bank erosion during monsoon |
| **Periyar, Kerala** | Plane bed to dunes | Stability of barrages and check dams |
| **Tarbela Dam, Pakistan** | Antidunes during flushing | Reservoir flushing operations |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bed Form Mechanics ŌĆö Regime Classification

The bed configuration is classified using the **Froude number** $Fr$, defined as:

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot D_m}}$$

where $V$ = mean velocity (m/s), $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s┬▓**), $D_m$ = hydraulic mean depth (m).

### Lower Flow Regime ($Fr < 1$ ŌĆö Subcritical)

- **Fr < 0.2:** No sediment motion or very small **ripples** (height $H < 0.1$ m).
- **0.2 < Fr < 0.4:** **Ripples** with straight crests, wavelength $\lambda \approx 1000 d_{50}$.
- **0.4 < Fr < 0.8:** **Dunes** ŌĆö large asymmetric bedforms, $H$ up to 1/3 of flow depth, $\lambda \approx 6D$.
- **0.8 < Fr < 1.0:** **Washed-out dunes** transitioning to plane bed.

### Upper Flow Regime ($Fr > 1$ ŌĆö Supercritical)

- **1.0 < Fr < 1.5:** **Plane bed** (no distinct bedforms), maximum transport.
- **1.5 < Fr < 2.5:** **Standing waves / antidunes** ŌĆö bedforms in phase with water surface.
- **Fr > 2.5:** **Breaking antidunes and chutes** ŌĆö violent energy dissipation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simons & Richardson (1961) Classification** ŌĆö universally cited in KTU 2024 syllabus, mapping Froude number to bed form, resistance, and sediment transport.

---

## 2.2 Tractive Force Theory (Foundation for Garret's Method)

The **boundary shear stress** (tractive force per unit wetted area) on the channel bed is:

$$\tau_o = \gamma \cdot R \cdot S$$

where:
- $\gamma$ = specific weight of water = **9810 N/m┬│**
- $R$ = hydraulic radius (m)
- $S$ = bed slope (dimensionless)
- $\tau_o$ has units of **N/m┬▓** or **Pa**

For a **wide channel** ($R \approx D$, where $D$ = depth of flow), this simplifies to:

$$\tau_o = \gamma \cdot D \cdot S$$

### Critical Tractive Force (Shields' Approach)

The **Shields parameter** $\theta_c$ is a dimensionless critical tractive force:

$$\theta_c = \frac{\tau_c}{(\rho_s - \rho) \cdot g \cdot d}$$

where $\rho_s$ = density of sediment (**2650 kg/m┬│** for quartz sand), $\rho$ = density of water (**1000 kg/m┬│**), $d$ = median grain diameter. Solving for $\tau_c$:

$$\tau_c = \theta_c \cdot (\rho_s - \rho) \cdot g \cdot d$$

For **quartz sand in water**, $\rho_s - \rho = 1650$ kg/m┬│, simplifying to:

$$\tau_c = 0.061 \cdot (1650) \cdot 9.81 \cdot d \approx 0.99 \cdot d \;\; \text{(for } d \text{ in mm, } \tau_c \text{ in N/m┬▓)}$$

Lane (1955) proposed a simpler empirical relationship for non-cohesive uniform material:

$$\tau_c = 0.75 \cdot d \;\; \text{(d in mm, } \tau_c \text{ in N/m┬▓)}$$

This **Lane's straight-line relation** is the precursor to Garret's curves.

---

## 2.3 Garret's Method ŌĆö Detailed Theory

Garret's method is a refinement of Lane's tractive force method. The **Garret's chart** plots:

- **X-axis:** Median grain size $d$ (mm), logarithmic, range **0.1 mm to 100 mm**.
- **Y-axis:** Critical tractive force $\tau_c$ (N/m┬▓ or kg/m┬▓).

The chart is divided into zones:

| Zone | Grain Size Range | Empirical Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| Fine sand | 0.1 ŌĆō 0.5 mm | $\tau_c$ rises rapidly (cohesive effects vanish) |
| Medium sand | 0.5 ŌĆō 5 mm | $\tau_c \approx 0.75 d$ (Lane's line) |
| Coarse sand | 5 ŌĆō 50 mm | $\tau_c$ follows $d^{0.6}$ approximately |
| Gravel/cobble | 50 ŌĆō 100 mm | $\tau_c$ approaches asymptotic limit |

**Key equation from Garret's chart (analytical approximation):**

$$\tau_c = 0.16 \cdot d^{0.7} \;\; \text{(for d in mm, } \tau_c \text{ in N/m┬▓, approximate)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Design Procedure (Garret's Method):**
> 1. Determine $Q$ (discharge), $S$ (bed slope), $n$ (Manning's roughness), and $d_{50}$ (median grain size).
> 2. Use **Manning's equation** to find normal depth $D$ and velocity $V$:
> $$V = \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}, \quad Q = A \cdot V$$
> 3. Compute actual tractive force: $\tau_o = \gamma D S$
> 4. Read critical tractive force $\tau_c$ from Garret's chart at given $d_{50}$.
> 5. **Stability Check:** If $\tau_o \leq \tau_c$, channel is stable. If $\tau_o > \tau_c$, increase $D$, reduce $S$, or use larger material.
> 6. Apply correction factor for **side slope** $K_1$ and **curvature** $K_2$.

### Correction Factors

| Condition | Factor | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Side slope correction | $K_1 = \cos\theta \sqrt{1 - \frac{\tan^2\theta}{\tan^2\phi}}$ | 0.8 ŌĆō 1.0 |
| Bend correction | $K_2 = 1.05$ to $1.10$ for mild bends | 1.0 ŌĆō 1.5 |
| Silt factor (Lane) | $f = 1.0$ (clean) to $1.5$ (silty) | 1.0 ŌĆō 1.5 |

---

## 2.4 Bed Load Transport ŌĆö Mechanics

Bed load particles are mobilized when **local instantaneous tractive force** $\tau(t)$ exceeds the **critical value** $\tau_c$. The probability of motion is given by an **Einstein-type probability function**:

$$p = 1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_{-B_* \psi - 1/\eta_0}^{B_* \psi - 1/\eta_0} e^{-t^2} dt$$

where $\psi = \tau_o / \tau_c$ is the transport intensity parameter, $B_*$ and $\eta_0$ are constants.

The **dimensionless bed load transport parameter** $\Phi$ is defined as:

$$\Phi = \frac{q_b}{\sqrt{(s-1) g d^3}}$$

where $q_b$ = bed load transport rate per unit width (m┬▓/s), $s = \rho_s/\rho$ = relative density (**2.65** for quartz).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Froude number | $Fr = V / \sqrt{gD}$ | $V$: m/s, $D$: m | Classifies bed form regime |
| 2 | Boundary shear stress | $\tau_o = \gamma R S$ | $\gamma$: N/m┬│, $R$: m | Wide channel: $R \approx D$ |
| 3 | Lane's critical tractive force | $\tau_c = 0.75 d$ | $d$ in mm, $\tau_c$ in N/m┬▓ | Uniform non-cohesive sand |
| 4 | Shields critical tractive force | $\tau_c = \theta_c (\rho_s - \rho) g d$ | $\theta_c \approx 0.047$ (Shields) | Hydrodynamically smooth to rough |
| 5 | Garret's analytical form | $\tau_c \approx 0.16 d^{0.7}$ | $d$ in mm, $\tau_c$ in N/m┬▓ | Approximation of Garret's chart |
| 6 | Manning's equation | $V = \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | $n$: dimensionless | SI units |
| 7 | Shield's parameter | $\theta = \tau_o / [(\rho_s-\rho) g d]$ | dimensionless | $\theta \geq 0.047$ ŌćÆ motion |
| 8 | **Schoklitsch (1930)** | $q_b = (2.5/d^{3/4})(q - q_c) S^{1/2}$ | $q$: m┬▓/s, $d$: mm | Empirical, easy to apply |
| 9 | **Meyer-Peter & M├╝ller (1948)** | $q_b = 8 \sqrt{g S^3 d^3} \left[ (k/k')((\tau_o - \tau_c)/\tau_c) \right]^{3/2}$ | $d$: m, $k,k'$: Strickler coefs | Most widely used, $k'/k = 1$ approx |
| 10 | **Einstein (1950)** | $\Phi = f(\psi)$ where $\psi = (s-1)d / (R S)$ | Dimensionless | Probabilistic, complex |
| 11 | **Bagnold (1966)** | $q_b = (e_b \tau_o V) / [\tan\alpha - \tan\beta]$ | Stream power approach | $\alpha$: friction angle, $\beta$: slope |
| 12 | **Engelund-Hansen (1967)** | $q_b = 0.05 \gamma_s V^2 \sqrt{\tau_o/(\gamma_s - \gamma)} \cdot [D S / (s-1) d]^{3/2}$ | Total load, but used for bed | Hyperbolic, semi-empirical |
| 13 | **Yang (1973)** | $\log C_t = 5.435 - 0.286 \log(\omega d/\nu) - 0.457 \log (u_*/\omega)$ | $\omega$: fall velocity | Unit stream power method |
| 14 | **Ackers-White (1973)** | $G_{gr} = c (F_{gr} - A)^{m}$ | $G_{gr}, F_{gr}$: dimensionless | Valid 0.04 mm to 7 mm |
| 15 | Critical discharge (Schoklitsch) | $q_c = 0.26 (s-1)^{5/3} d^{3/2} / S^{7/6}$ | m┬▓/s | Threshold condition |

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas above are KTU 2024 syllabusŌĆōprescribed and appear regularly in B.Tech university exams.** For table cells, absolute value bars have been written as $\vert x \vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Bed Form / Stable Channel Knowledge |
|---|---|
| Irrigation design | Lining-free canals, Kuttanad below-sea-level farming |
| Bridge engineering | Predicting scour at piers ŌĆö antidunes cause 3├Ś local scour |
| Reservoir engineering | Sediment flushing via controlled floods (Tarbela, Bhakra) |
| River training | Groynes, bandallas depend on understanding bedform migration |
| Inland navigation | Maintaining navigable depth in alluvial rivers |
| Environmental flow | Designing minimum flows to maintain sediment continuity |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Boundary Shear Stress from Force Balance

Consider a control volume of water of length $L$ along the channel, with cross-section $A$ and wetted perimeter $P$. The gravitational force component along the flow direction is:

$$F_g = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot L \cdot S$$

This is balanced by the shear force on the wetted boundary:

$$F_\tau = \tau_o \cdot P \cdot L$$

Setting $F_g = F_\tau$ at uniform flow equilibrium:

$$\rho g A L S = \tau_o P L$$

Using the hydraulic radius definition $R = A/P$ and $\gamma = \rho g$:

$$\boxed{\tau_o = \gamma R S}$$

For a wide rectangular channel where $R \approx D$ (depth):

$$\tau_o = \gamma D S = 9810 \cdot D \cdot S \;\; \text{(N/m┬▓)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Illustration:** $D = 2$ m, $S = 1/2000 = 0.0005$:
> $\tau_o = 9810 \times 2 \times 0.0005 = 9.81$ N/m┬▓
> Compared with Lane's $\tau_c = 0.75 \times 0.3 = 0.225$ N/m┬▓ (for $d = 0.3$ mm)
> Ratio $\tau_o/\tau_c = 43.6 \Rightarrow$ highly unstable bed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shields' Critical Tractive Force (Dimensional Analysis)

Let $\tau_c$ depend on grain size $d$, fluid density $\rho$, sediment density $\rho_s$, gravity $g$, and viscosity $\mu$:

$$\tau_c = f(d, \rho, \rho_s, g, \mu)$$

With 6 variables and 3 fundamental dimensions (M, L, T), Buckingham $\pi$ theorem gives 3 dimensionless groups:

$$\theta = \frac{\tau_c}{(\rho_s - \rho) g d}, \quad Re_* = \frac{u_* d}{\nu}$$

where $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_c/\rho}$ is the **shear velocity** and $\nu$ is the **kinematic viscosity** (**1.0 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ m┬▓/s** for water at 20┬░C). Shields' result is the empirical curve:

$$\theta_c = f(Re_*) \approx 0.047 \;\; \text{(fully rough regime, } Re_* > 70\text{)}$$

Solving for $\tau_c$:

$$\tau_c = 0.047 (\rho_s - \rho) g d = 0.047 \times 1650 \times 9.81 \times d$$

$$\boxed{\tau_c = 760.6 \cdot d \;\; \text{(d in m, } \tau_c \text{ in N/m┬▓)}}$$

Equivalent in **mm units**: $\tau_c = 0.76 d$ for $d$ in mm ŌĆö very close to Lane's $0.75 d$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Meyer-Peter & M├╝ller (1948) Bed Load Equation

Meyer-Peter & M├╝ller conducted **flume experiments** with uniform sediment (0.4 to 28.6 mm) and proposed the dimensionless transport rate:

$$\Phi = 8 (\theta - \theta_c)^{3/2}$$

where $\Phi = q_b / \sqrt{(s-1) g d^3}$ and $\theta = \tau_o / [(\rho_s - \rho) g d]$.

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$\frac{q_b}{\sqrt{(s-1) g d^3}} = 8 \left( \frac{\tau_o - \tau_c}{(\rho_s - \rho) g d} \right)^{3/2}$$

$$q_b = 8 \sqrt{(s-1) g d^3} \left( \frac{\tau_o - \tau_c}{(\rho_s - \rho) g d} \right)^{3/2}$$

In terms of slope $S$ (using $\tau_o = \gamma D S \approx \rho g D S$):

$$q_b = 8 \sqrt{(s-1) g d^3} \left( \frac{(\tau_o/\tau_c) - 1}{1} \right)^{3/2} \cdot \left(\frac{\tau_c}{(\rho_s - \rho) g d}\right)^{3/2}$$

For rough turbulent flow with $k = k'$ (Meyer-Peter's grain roughness = total roughness), the standard form becomes:

$$\boxed{q_b = 8 \sqrt{g S^3 d^3} \left( \frac{\tau_o - \tau_c}{\tau_c} \right)^{3/2}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):**
Given: $D = 1.5$ m, $S = 1/1500$, $d = 2$ mm = 0.002 m, $V = 1.2$ m/s.

Step 1: Compute $\tau_o$:
$$\tau_o = \gamma D S = 9810 \times 1.5 \times \frac{1}{1500} = 9.81 \text{ N/m}^2$$

Step 2: Compute $\tau_c$ (Lane's):
$$\tau_c = 0.75 \times 2 = 1.5 \text{ N/m}^2$$

Step 3: Substitute into Meyer-Peter formula:
$$q_b = 8 \sqrt{9.81 \times (1/1500)^3 \times (0.002)^3} \times \left(\frac{9.81 - 1.5}{1.5}\right)^{3/2}$$

Compute term by term:
- $S^3 = (1/1500)^3 = 2.96 \times 10^{-10}$
- $d^3 = (0.002)^3 = 8 \times 10^{-9}$
- $g S^3 d^3 = 9.81 \times 2.96 \times 10^{-10} \times 8 \times 10^{-9} = 2.32 \times 10^{-17}$
- $\sqrt{2.32 \times 10^{-17}} = 4.82 \times 10^{-9}$
- $(9.81 - 1.5)/1.5 = 5.54$
- $5.54^{1.5} = 13.04$
- $q_b = 8 \times 4.82 \times 10^{-9} \times 13.04 = 5.03 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

Step 4: Convert to more useful units:
$$q_b = 5.03 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 0.503 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s} \text{ per meter width}$$

**Daily bed load per meter width** (kg/day):
$$M_b = q_b \cdot \rho_s \cdot 86400 = 5.03 \times 10^{-7} \times 2650 \times 86400 \approx 115 \text{ kg/day/m}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Schoklitsch Bed Load Formula

Schoklitsch (1930) used the **discharge-based approach**:

$$q_b = \frac{2.5}{d^{3/4}} (q - q_c) S^{1/2}$$

where $q = Q/B$ (discharge per unit width), $q_c$ = critical discharge for threshold:

$$q_c = \frac{0.26 (s-1)^{5/3} d^{3/2}}{S^{7/6}}$$

**Derivation logic:** Schoklitsch reasoned that bed load is proportional to the **excess discharge** $(q - q_c)$ available for transport, and inversely related to grain size (larger grains need more force per unit weight).

**Quick Example:** $q = 2$ m┬▓/s, $d = 5$ mm, $S = 1/500$:
$$q_c = 0.26 \times 1.65^{5/3} \times 5^{1.5} \times 500^{7/6} \approx 0.16 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$
$$q_b = (2.5/5^{0.75}) \times (2 - 0.16) \times (1/500)^{0.5} = 0.331 \times 1.84 \times 0.0447 \approx 0.0272 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

This is a large bed load transport ŌĆö the channel would degrade rapidly.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Bagnold's Stream Power Approach

Bagnold (1966) defined **stream power** $\omega = \tau_o V$ (W/m┬▓) and the **efficiency** $e_b$ of bed load transport:

$$q_b = \frac{e_b \cdot \tau_o \cdot V}{(\tan\alpha - \tan\beta)}$$

where $\alpha$ = angle of internal friction of sediment (**~32┬░** for sand), $\beta$ = bed slope angle. Bagnold found $e_b \approx 0.13$ for bed load.

**Step-by-step:** $\tau_o = \gamma D S$, $V$ = mean velocity:
$$q_b = \frac{0.13 \times \gamma D S \times V}{(\tan 32┬░ - S)} = \frac{0.13 \times 9810 \times D \times S \times V}{0.6249 - S}$$

For $D = 1$ m, $S = 0.001$, $V = 1$ m/s:
$$q_b = \frac{0.13 \times 9810 \times 1 \times 0.001 \times 1}{0.6249 - 0.001} = \frac{1.275}{0.6239} = 2.04 \text{ (N/m┬Ęs, energy units)}$$

Convert to volumetric: $q_b \approx 2.04/(\rho_s g) = 2.04/(2650 \times 9.81) \approx 7.85 \times 10^{-5}$ m┬▓/s.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Garret's Method ŌĆö Stability Check Procedure

**Given:** $Q = 50$ m┬│/s, $S = 1/2000$, $d_{50} = 0.3$ mm, $n = 0.025$, rectangular section, width $B = 20$ m.

**Step 1:** Manning's equation for wide channel: $Q = (1/n) D^{5/3} S^{1/2} B$
$$50 = \frac{1}{0.025} D^{5/3} (1/2000)^{1/2} \times 20$$
$$50 = 40 \times D^{5/3} \times 0.02236$$
$$D^{5/3} = \frac{50}{40 \times 0.02236} = 55.9$$
$$D = 55.9^{3/5} = 55.9^{0.6} \approx 10.95 \text{ m}$$

(Reasonable for the given $Q$ and gentle slope.)

**Step 2:** Velocity: $V = Q/(BD) = 50/(20 \times 10.95) = 0.228$ m/s.

**Step 3:** Froude number: $Fr = V/\sqrt{gD} = 0.228/\sqrt{9.81 \times 10.95} = 0.022$ ŌĆö extremely subcritical, lower regime.

**Step 4:** Actual tractive force: $\tau_o = 9810 \times 10.95 \times 0.0005 = 53.7$ N/m┬▓.

**Step 5:** Garret's chart $\tau_c$ at $d_{50} = 0.3$ mm (in fine sand zone): $\tau_c \approx 1.6$ N/m┬▓ (approximately, from standard Garret's chart).

**Step 6:** Stability ratio: $\tau_o/\tau_c = 53.7/1.6 = 33.6 \gg 1$ ŌćÆ **highly unstable**. The bed will erode strongly; antidunes/chutes may form.

**Conclusion:** Channel needs redesign ŌĆö flatten slope, add bed lining, or use coarser bed material.
