# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Differentiate clearly between bed load and suspended load. What factors determine whether a sediment particle travels as bed load or suspended load?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bed Load | Suspended Load |
|---|---|---|
| Mode of transport | Rolling, sliding, saltation | Held aloft by turbulence |
| Contact with bed | Continuous or near-continuous | Rarely or never |
| Typical grain size | Coarse (sand, gravel) ŌĆö $d > 0.2$ mm | Fine (silt, clay) ŌĆö $d < 0.2$ mm |
| Settling velocity $w$ | $w \gg u_*$ | $w \ll u_*$ |
| Concentration | Low (uniform) | High (varies with depth) |

**Deciding factor:** Rouse number $Z = w / (\kappa u_*)$, where $\kappa$ = von K├Īrm├Īn constant (**0.41**).
- $Z > 2.5$ ŌåÆ mostly **bed load**
- $0.8 < Z < 2.5$ ŌåÆ mixed
- $Z < 0.8$ ŌåÆ mostly **suspended load**

(3 marks: clear distinction + Rouse criterion + example)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]
**Explain the significance of the Shields parameter in sediment transport. How is it used in the design of stable channels?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Shields parameter** $\theta = \tau_o / [(\rho_s - \rho) g d]$ is a **dimensionless** number that expresses the ratio of fluid driving force (tractive stress) to the submerged weight of the grain (resisting force). Its significance:

1. **Threshold of motion:** Sediment moves when $\theta \geq \theta_c$ where $\theta_c \approx 0.047$ (Shields' value for fully rough turbulent flow).

2. **Universal applicability:** Being dimensionless, it works for any fluidŌĆōsediment combination (water-sand, air-dust, oil-gravel).

3. **Stable channel design:** Compute $\tau_o$ from channel geometry, divide by $(\rho_s - \rho) g d$, and compare with $\theta_c$. If $\theta < \theta_c$, the bed is stable; if $\theta \geq \theta_c$, redesign is required.

4. **Foundation for transport formulas:** Einstein, Meyer-Peter, Ackers-White all use Shields-type parameters in their bed load equations.

(3 marks: definition + threshold value + design application)

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ŌŁÉ Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 marks]** A wide rectangular channel carrying 30 m┬│/s has bed slope 1 in 2500 and Manning's $n = 0.022$. The median bed material size is $d_{50} = 0.5$ mm. Using **Garret's method**, determine:
(i) The normal depth of flow
(ii) The actual tractive force on the bed
(iii) Whether the channel is stable. Use $\tau_c = 0.75 d$ (Lane's approximation as per Garret's curve for this size).

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain with neat sketches the different **bed forms in the lower and upper flow regimes**. Discuss the role of Froude number in their formation.

---

**Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Manning's equation for wide channel** ($R \approx D$):
$$Q = \frac{1}{n} D^{5/3} S^{1/2} B$$

For a wide channel, $B \to \infty$, but unit discharge approach: $q = Q/B$. We need $B$. Assume $B/D = 8$ (typical stable trapezoidal): $B = 8D$.

$$30 = \frac{1}{0.022} D^{5/3} \left(\frac{1}{2500}\right)^{1/2} \times 8D$$
$$30 = \frac{8 D^{8/3}}{0.022} \times 0.02 = 7.273 D^{8/3}$$
$$D^{8/3} = 30/7.273 = 4.124$$
$$D = 4.124^{3/8} = 4.124^{0.375} \approx 1.706 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Width and velocity:**
$$B = 8 \times 1.706 = 13.65 \text{ m}$$
$$V = \frac{1}{0.022} (1.706)^{2/3} (1/2500)^{1/2}$$
$$V = 45.45 \times 1.428 \times 0.02 = 1.298 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Tractive force:**
$$\tau_o = \gamma D S = 9810 \times 1.706 \times 0.0004 = 6.694 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Critical tractive force** (Lane's, $d = 0.5$ mm):
$$\tau_c = 0.75 \times 0.5 = 0.375 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Stability check:**
$$\frac{\tau_o}{\tau_c} = \frac{6.694}{0.375} = 17.85 \gg 1$$

**Conclusion: Channel is highly unstable.** The bed material will be transported vigorously. Recommendations:
- Reduce $S$ to at least 1/7000
- Increase $D$ to $\geq 6$ m
- Use $d_{50} \geq 18$ mm coarse material
- Apply bed lining (concrete, rip-rap)

(7 marks: 1 mark each for D, V, Žäo, Žäc, ratio + 2 marks for conclusion/recommendation)

---

**Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Lower Flow Regime ($Fr < 1$):**

- **Ripples** (Fr < 0.4): Small bedforms, $\lambda \approx 1000 d$, height a few cm. Formed by viscous sublayer instability on fine sand. Roughly symmetric, triangular crests.

- **Dunes** (0.4 < Fr < 0.8): Large asymmetric bedforms with gentle upstream (stoss) slope (~4ŌĆō6┬░) and steep lee slope at angle of repose (~30┬░). Migrate downstream by sand avalanching on lee side. Cause large form drag; triple Manning's n compared to plane bed.

- **Washed-out dunes** (0.8 < Fr < 1.0): Dunes diminish in height as Fr approaches 1; transition zone.

**Upper Flow Regime ($Fr > 1$):**

- **Plane bed** (1.0 < Fr < 1.5): Sediment moves in sheet-like motion; no distinct bedforms. Lowest flow resistance for given discharge.

- **Antidunes** (1.5 < Fr < 2.5): Bedforms in phase with water surface waves; grow upward, may break. Cause intense local scour.

- **Chutes and pools** (Fr > 2.5): Violent hydraulic jumps; cyclic erosion-deposition; often catastrophic.

**Role of Froude number:**
$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gD}}$$

Fr represents the **ratio of flow velocity to shallow-water wave celerity**. When $Fr < 1$, surface waves can propagate upstream, "informing" the bed of upstream conditions ŌĆö this is why dunes form with a downstream migration direction. When $Fr > 1$, surface waves cannot propagate upstream, and bedforms lock in phase with the water surface (antidunes).

(7 marks: 2 marks sketches, 2 marks classification table, 2 marks Fr role, 1 mark example)

---

### ŌŁÉ Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Using the **Meyer-Peter & M├╝ller formula**, compute the bed load transport rate in kg/day per meter width for a channel with: depth $D = 2.0$ m, bed slope $S = 1/1200$, mean grain size $d = 1.5$ mm, $\rho_s = 2650$ kg/m┬│, and assume $k = k'$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain **Einstein's bed load function**. State its assumptions, the form of the equation, and discuss its merits and limitations.

---

**Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute actual tractive force:**
$$\tau_o = \gamma D S = 9810 \times 2.0 \times \frac{1}{1200} = 16.35 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Critical tractive force** (using Lane's, $d = 1.5$ mm):
$$\tau_c = 0.75 \times 1.5 = 1.125 \text{ N/m}^2$$

(Equivalently, Shields with $\theta_c = 0.047$:
$\tau_c = 0.047 \times 1650 \times 9.81 \times 0.0015 = 1.141$ N/m┬▓ ŌĆö similar.)

**Step 3 ŌĆö Meyer-Peter & M├╝ller formula** (with $k = k'$):
$$q_b = 8 \sqrt{g S^3 d^3} \left( \frac{\tau_o - \tau_c}{\tau_c} \right)^{3/2}$$

Compute each term:
- $g = 9.81$ m/s┬▓
- $S^3 = (1/1200)^3 = 5.787 \times 10^{-10}$
- $d^3 = (0.0015)^3 = 3.375 \times 10^{-9}$
- $g S^3 d^3 = 9.81 \times 5.787 \times 10^{-10} \times 3.375 \times 10^{-9} = 1.916 \times 10^{-17}$
- $\sqrt{1.916 \times 10^{-17}} = 4.377 \times 10^{-9}$

Compute the ratio:
$$\frac{\tau_o - \tau_c}{\tau_c} = \frac{16.35 - 1.125}{1.125} = \frac{15.225}{1.125} = 13.53$$

$$13.53^{1.5} = 13.53 \times \sqrt{13.53} = 13.53 \times 3.679 = 49.78$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Bed load transport rate per unit width:**
$$q_b = 8 \times 4.377 \times 10^{-9} \times 49.78 = 1.744 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Convert to kg/day/m:**
$$M_b = q_b \cdot \rho_s \cdot 86400 = 1.744 \times 10^{-6} \times 2650 \times 86400$$
$$M_b = 399.3 \text{ kg/day/m width}$$

**Verification:** Daily volume per m = $1.744 \times 10^{-6} \times 86400 = 0.1507$ m┬│/day. Mass = $0.1507 \times 2650 = 399.4$ kg/day/m. Ō£ō

(7 marks: 1 mark each step + 2 marks for final answer in correct units)

---

**Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Einstein's Bed Load Function (1950):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Sediment particles move in a series of discrete steps (saltation hops).
2. The probability of a particle being eroded at any instant follows a **normal distribution** of the instantaneous lift force.
3. The exchange of particles between bed and bed load is statistically stationary.
4. The lift force is proportional to the velocity squared above the bed.

**Form of the equation:**

Einstein defined two key dimensionless parameters:
$$\Psi = \frac{(s-1) d}{R S} = \frac{1}{\theta} \quad \text{(flow intensity)}$$
$$\Phi = \frac{q_b}{\sqrt{(s-1) g d^3}} \quad \text{(transport intensity)}$$

The functional relationship is given by:
$$1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_{-B_* \Psi - 1/\eta_0}^{B_* \Psi - 1/\eta_0} e^{-t^2} dt = \frac{A_* \Phi}{1 + A_* \Phi}$$

with constants $A_* = 43.5$, $B_* = 0.143$, $\eta_0 = 0.5$ (for uniform sediment).

**Procedure:** Compute $\Psi$ from knowns, look up $\Phi$ from Einstein's chart, then solve for $q_b = \Phi \sqrt{(s-1) g d^3}$.

**Merits:**
- Probabilistic basis ŌĆö captures the random nature of particle entrainment.
- Applicable to wide range of flow conditions and grain sizes.
- Incorporates hiding/exposure effects for graded sediments.
- Widely used in research and design codes (USBR, USACE).

**Limitations:**
- Mathematically complex; requires iteration or chart lookup.
- The constants $A_*$, $B_*$, $\eta_0$ are empirical and may need calibration.
- Tends to underpredict in fine sediments and overpredict for very coarse materials.
- Does not account for bed forms explicitly (a later modification, Einstein-Barbarossa, adds bed-form correction).

(7 marks: 2 assumptions + 2 equation form + 1 procedure + 2 merits/limitations)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Trends):**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Garret's formula:** $d$ must be in **mm** to use $\tau_c = 0.75 d$ in N/m┬▓. Many students substitute $d$ in m, giving $\tau_c$ values 1000├Ś too small, which falsely indicates "stable channel." Examiner deducts 2ŌĆō3 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\tau_o$ with $\tau_c$:** $\tau_o$ is the **actual** tractive force (driving), $\tau_c$ is the **critical** (resisting). Many students invert the inequality in stability check, getting wrong conclusions.
>
> 3. **Forgetting shear velocity $u_*$ in Shields diagram:** Shields plot requires $Re_* = u_* d / \nu$ on the x-axis, not $d$ alone. Using $d$ as x-axis gives wrong $\theta_c$.
>
> 4. **Meyer-Peter M├╝ller: missing the $(k/k')$ factor:** When $k \neq k'$, students forget to apply the Strickler correction for grain roughness vs total roughness. 1-mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Bed form sketches:** Examiners expect **labeled sketches** with $H$ (height), $\lambda$ (wavelength), stoss and lee slopes, and direction of flow. Unlabeled sketches get 1ŌĆō2 marks instead of 3.
>
> 6. **Einstein formula: forgetting $(s-1)$ factor:** The submerged specific gravity $(s-1) = 1.65$ must appear, not just $s = 2.65$. Common 1-mark loss.
>
> 7. **Conversion errors:** $q_b$ in m┬▓/s must be converted to kg/day using $\rho_s$ and 86400 s/day. Direct quotation of $q_b$ in m┬▓/s for "transport rate" loses 1 mark.
>
> 8. **Confusing "stable" with "no sediment motion":** A stable channel can have minor bed load; what matters is no net degradation or aggradation. 1-mark conceptual error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 ŌĆö Bed Form Mechanics, Garret's Method, Bed Load):**
>
> **Bed Form Mechanics:**
> - Bed forms classified by **Froude number** $Fr = V/\sqrt{gD}$.
> - **Lower regime ($Fr < 1$):** ripples, dunes, washed-out dunes.
> - **Upper regime ($Fr > 1$):** plane bed, antidunes, chutes.
> - **Dunes** are the dominant bedform in most alluvial rivers; they cause the highest flow resistance.
> - **Antidunes** are in phase with surface water waves; cause intense local scour.
> - **Simons & Richardson (1961)** classification is the KTU-referenced standard.
>
> **Tractive Force Theory:**
> - $\tau_o = \gamma R S$; for wide channel, $\tau_o = \gamma D S = 9810 D S$ (N/m┬▓).
> - Stability condition: $\tau_o \leq \tau_c$.
> - **Lane's** equation: $\tau_c = 0.75 d$ (d in mm, $\tau_c$ in N/m┬▓) ŌĆö valid for uniform non-cohesive sand (0.2ŌĆō5 mm).
> - **Shields'** equation: $\tau_c = \theta_c (\rho_s - \rho) g d$, with $\theta_c \approx 0.047$ for fully rough flow.
>
> **Garret's Method:**
> - Refined tractive force approach with chart for grain sizes 0.1ŌĆō100 mm.
> - Procedure: Manning ŌåÆ D, V ŌåÆ $\tau_o$ ŌåÆ read $\tau_c$ from Garret's chart ŌåÆ compare ŌåÆ redesign if needed.
> - Side slope correction $K_1 = \cos\theta \sqrt{1 - \tan^2\theta / \tan^2\phi}$.
> - Bend correction $K_2 \approx 1.05$ŌĆō$1.10$.
>
> **Bed Load Transport Formulas:**
> - **Schoklitsch:** $q_b = (2.5/d^{3/4})(q - q_c) S^{1/2}$ ŌĆö easy, discharge-based.
> - **Meyer-Peter & M├╝ller:** $q_b = 8 \sqrt{g S^3 d^3} [(\tau_o - \tau_c)/\tau_c]^{3/2}$ ŌĆö most widely used, slope-based.
> - **Einstein:** Probabilistic, $\Phi$ vs $\Psi$ chart ŌĆö most rigorous, mathematically intensive.
> - **Bagnold:** $q_b = e_b \tau_o V / (\tan\alpha - \tan\beta)$ ŌĆö stream power based.
> - **Engelund-Hansen:** for total load including bed load; hyperbolic.
>
> **Numerical Tricks (Exam-Time):**
> - Always convert $d$ to **mm** for Lane's/Garret's, to **m** for Meyer-Peter and Shields.
> - $\rho_s - \rho = 1650$ kg/m┬│ for quartz sediment in water ŌĆö memorize.
> - $\gamma = 9810$ N/m┬│ ŌĆö memorize.
> - $s - 1 = 1.65$ ŌĆö memorize.
> - Manning's $n$ for stable channels: $n = 0.025$ (fine sand), $0.030$ (coarse sand), $0.040$ (gravel).
>
> **Conceptual Anchors:**
> - "Stable channel" ŌēĀ "no sediment motion"; it means equilibrium.
> - Bed forms affect Manning's $n$ by a factor of 2ŌĆō3 ŌĆö they cannot be ignored in design.
> - All major bed load formulas are **empirical** with limited extrapolation range; calibration with local data is always recommended.
> - Froude number $Fr < 0.4$ ŌåÆ no dunes; $Fr > 0.8$ ŌåÆ no ripples; the "dune zone" is 0.4ŌĆō0.8.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Suspended Load Transport
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET747 - RIVER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Suspended Load Transport

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Suspended Load Transport ŌĆö Core Concept & Physical Intuition

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Suspended Load** is the portion of the total sediment load in a stream that is composed of fine particles, maintained in the water column by the upward components of turbulent eddies (turbulent diffusion) for a considerable period of time, without continuous contact with the streambed. It is sustained when the **settling velocity (w)** of the particle is balanced by the **upward turbulent mixing velocity (vŌĆ▓)** generated by fluid eddies.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme classification of **Total Sediment Load ($Q_t$)**, suspended load is expressed as:

$$Q_t = Q_b + Q_s$$

Where:
- $Q_b$ = Bed load transport rate (kg/s or N/s)
- $Q_s$ = Suspended load transport rate (kg/s or N/s)

Mathematically, suspended load is computed by integrating the product of local sediment concentration ($c$) and local stream velocity ($u$) over the depth of flow:

$$Q_s = q \cdot \int_{a}^{h} c \cdot dy$$

Where $a$ is the reference level (near the bed) and $h$ is the total flow depth.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Dust in a Sunbeam" Analogy**: Imagine a sunbeam streaming through a window into a dusty room. The dust motes do not fall straight down ŌĆö they float, drift sideways, and remain suspended in the air because tiny air currents (turbulent eddies) keep pushing them up against gravity. **Suspended load behaves identically in a river**: the turbulence of flowing water (eddies) counteracts the gravitational pull on fine sediment grains, allowing them to "float" mid-water while the river carries them downstream.

- A **silt particle (0.01 mm)** behaves like dust in still air ŌĆö easily suspended.
- A **gravel particle (10 mm)** is too heavy ŌĆö turbulent kicks cannot lift it, so it rolls/slides as **bed load**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight ŌĆö Key Numerical Benchmarks**
> - **Von Karman constant ($\kappa$)** = **0.41** (universal turbulence constant)
> - **Kinematic viscosity of water ($\nu$)** at $20^\circ C$ = **$1.004 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$**
> - **Density of water ($\rho_w$)** = **1000 kg/m┬│**
> - **Density of quartz sediment ($\rho_s$)** = **2650 kg/m┬│**
> - **Specific gravity of sediment ($G_s$)** = **2.65**
> - Typical suspended sediment concentration in rivers: **100 ŌĆō 5000 ppm**
> - Rouse Number $Z$ for fully suspended fine silt = **< 0.4**; for bed-material suspended load = **0.4 ŌĆō 2.5**

## 1.4 Suspended vs Bed Load ŌĆö The Critical Distinction

| Property | Bed Load | Suspended Load |
|---|---|---|
| Contact with bed | Continuous | Intermittent / None |
| Particle size | Coarse (sand, gravel) | Fine (silt, fine sand, clay) |
| Driving force | Gravity + Flow drag | Turbulent diffusion |
| Settling velocity | Comparable to shear velocity | Smaller than shear velocity |
| Typical transport mode | Rolling, sliding, saltation | True suspension |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 4: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Competing Forces Governing Suspension

For a particle to remain suspended, **two competing physical mechanisms** must balance:

1. **Downward Force ŌĆö Settling Velocity ($w$)**: Gravity pulls the particle downward through still water.
2. **Upward Force ŌĆö Turbulent Diffusion**: Random fluid eddies (with characteristic mixing coefficient $\epsilon_s$) transfer particles upward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium Condition (Diffusion Concept)**:
> The **net upward flux of particles (turbulent diffusion)** must exactly equal the **net downward flux (settling)** at every elevation $y$ above the bed. This is the cornerstone of the diffusion theory of suspension.

## 2.2 The Diffusion Theory ŌĆö Governing Differential Equation

The one-dimensional vertical diffusion equation for suspended sediment in steady, uniform flow is:

$$w \cdot c + \epsilon_s \cdot \frac{dc}{dy} = 0$$

Where:
- $w$ = settling velocity of the particle (m/s)
- $c$ = volumetric concentration of sediment at elevation $y$ (dimensionless or ppm)
- $\epsilon_s$ = sediment diffusion coefficient (m┬▓/s)
- $y$ = vertical distance above the bed (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the negative sign?** The concentration $c$ decreases with height $y$ (more sediment near the bed), so $dc/dy < 0$. Diffusion acts upward (against gravity), opposing the settling flux.

## 2.3 Sediment Diffusion Coefficient ŌĆö The Boussinesq Assumption

Following the Reynolds analogy, the sediment diffusion coefficient is assumed proportional to the eddy viscosity (momentum diffusion coefficient) of the flow:

$$\epsilon_s = \beta \cdot \epsilon_m$$

Where:
- $\epsilon_m$ = momentum (eddy) diffusion coefficient
- $\beta$ = proportionality coefficient, often assumed **Ōēł 1.0** for fine sediment in KTU textbook problems.

For a **logarithmic velocity profile (PrandtlŌĆōvon Karman)**, the eddy viscosity varies linearly with $y$:

$$\epsilon_m = \kappa \cdot u_* \cdot y \cdot \left(1 - \frac{y}{h}\right)$$

For the lower 20% of depth, the simplification $y/h \ll 1$ yields:

$$\epsilon_s \approx \kappa \cdot u_* \cdot y$$

## 2.4 The Rouse Equation ŌĆö Vertical Distribution of Suspended Sediment

Substituting $\epsilon_s = \kappa u_* y$ into the diffusion equation and integrating from a reference level $a$ (concentration $c_a$) to elevation $y$ (concentration $c$):

$$\frac{c}{c_a} = \left[\frac{\left(\frac{h}{y} - 1\right)}{\left(\frac{h}{a} - 1\right)}\right]^{Z}$$

Or in a more common textbook form (using $(h-y)/y$ instead of $h/y$):

$$\boxed{\frac{c}{c_a} = \left[\frac{\left(\frac{h - y}{y}\right)}{\left(\frac{h - a}{a}\right)}\right]^{Z}}$$

Where the dimensionless exponent $Z$ is the **Rouse Number**:

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{w}{\kappa \cdot u_*}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight**: The Rouse Number $Z$ determines the shape of the vertical concentration profile. $Z$ is the **single most-tested parameter** for suspended load in KTU examinations.

## 2.5 Physical Interpretation of the Rouse Number ($Z$)

| Rouse Number $Z$ | Vertical Distribution | Suspension Character |
|---|---|---|
| $Z < 0.4$ | Nearly uniform (almost vertical line) | Full suspension (uniformly mixed) |
| $0.4 \leq Z \leq 0.8$ | Moderate gradient | Good suspension (most natural rivers) |
| $0.8 < Z \leq 2.5$ | Steep gradient (concentrated near bed) | Partial suspension |
| $Z > 2.5$ | Extremely steep (near bed only) | Bed load dominates |

## 2.6 Settling Velocity ($w$) ŌĆö The Controlling Particle Property

The settling velocity is the terminal fall velocity of a particle in still fluid. KTU typically tests two regimes:

### (a) Stokes' Law (Laminar Regime, $Re_p < 1$)
Valid for very fine particles ($d < 0.1$ mm):

$$w = \frac{(G_s - 1) \cdot g \cdot d^2}{18 \cdot \nu}$$

### (b) Rubey's Equation (General, Turbulent-Transitional)
A widely used empirical equation valid across regimes:

$$w = \sqrt{(G_s - 1) \cdot g \cdot d} \cdot F$$

Where $F$ is a correction factor (often $\approx 0.79$ for natural sands). A simplified Rubey form:

$$w = F_1 \sqrt{(G_s - 1) \cdot g \cdot d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**: For $d$ in mm, the settling velocity $w$ in cm/s is often approximated as $w \approx 0.79 \cdot \sqrt{16.17 \cdot d} = 3.18 \sqrt{d}$ (with $d$ in mm). Always confirm units in problem statements.

## 2.7 Shear Velocity (Friction Velocity) ŌĆö $u_*$

The shear velocity characterizes the bed shear stress:

$$u_* = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_0}{\rho_w}} = \sqrt{g \cdot R \cdot S}$$

Where:
- $\tau_0$ = bed shear stress (N/m┬▓)
- $R$ = hydraulic radius (m)
- $S$ = energy slope (dimensionless)

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Application / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| Total Sediment Load | $Q_t = Q_b + Q_s$ | Load classification |
| Rouse Equation | $c/c_a = [(h-y)/y \cdot a/(h-a)]^Z$ | Vertical concentration profile |
| Rouse Number | $Z = w / (\kappa u_*)$ | Suspension potential |
| Shear Velocity | $u_* = \sqrt{g R S}$ | Bed shear characterization |
| Settling Velocity (Stokes) | $w = (G_s-1) g d^2 / 18\nu$ | Fine particles $Re_p < 1$ |
| Settling Velocity (Rubey) | $w = F\sqrt{(G_s-1) g d}$ | General purpose |
| Diffusion Equation | $w c + \epsilon_s dc/dy = 0$ | Theoretical base of Rouse |
| Suspended Load (per unit width) | $q_s = \int_a^h u \cdot c \, dy$ | Transport rate |
| Friction Factor | $f = 8(u_*/U)^2$ | From mean velocity $U$ |
| Reference Concentration | $c_a \approx 0.034 \cdot (\tau_0/(\rho_s - \rho_w) g d)$ | Einstein's bed function |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 4: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Rouse Equation from First Principles

### Step 1 ŌĆö Set Up the Steady-State Diffusion Balance

In a steady, uniform flow, at any elevation $y$, the **downward mass flux due to settling** must equal the **upward mass flux due to turbulent diffusion**:

$$w \cdot c = -\epsilon_s \cdot \frac{dc}{dy}$$

Rearranging:

$$w \cdot c + \epsilon_s \cdot \frac{dc}{dy} = 0$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Boussinesq Assumption (Reynolds Analogy)

Assume sediment diffusivity equals the momentum eddy diffusivity:

$$\epsilon_s = \beta \cdot \epsilon_m \approx \epsilon_m \quad (\text{for } \beta = 1)$$

For a logarithmic velocity profile, the momentum exchange coefficient in the inner layer is linear:

$$\epsilon_m = \kappa \cdot u_* \cdot y$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute and Separate Variables

Substituting $\epsilon_s = \kappa u_* y$ into the diffusion equation:

$$w \cdot c + \kappa u_* \cdot y \cdot \frac{dc}{dy} = 0$$

Separating variables $c$ and $y$:

$$\frac{dc}{c} = -\frac{w}{\kappa u_*} \cdot \frac{dy}{y}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Integrate with Boundary Conditions

Integrate from reference level $a$ (where $c = c_a$) to elevation $y$ (where $c = c$):

$$\int_{c_a}^{c} \frac{dc}{c} = -\frac{w}{\kappa u_*} \int_{a}^{y} \frac{dy}{y}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{c}{c_a}\right) = -\frac{w}{\kappa u_*} \ln\left(\frac{y}{a}\right)$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Exponentiate to Obtain the Rouse Equation

Exponentiating both sides and defining $Z = w/(\kappa u_*)$:

$$\frac{c}{c_a} = \left(\frac{a}{y}\right)^{Z} = \left(\frac{y}{a}\right)^{-Z}$$

To make this dimensionally consistent with the full depth $h$, the Rouse equation is written using a self-similar coordinate:

$$\boxed{\frac{c}{c_a} = \left[\frac{\left(\frac{h-y}{y}\right)}{\left(\frac{h-a}{a}\right)}\right]^{Z}}$$

This is the **standard Rouse form** required for all KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Suspended Load Transport Rate (Einstein's Method)

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Suspended Load per Unit Width

The mass transport rate of suspended sediment per unit channel width $q_s$ is:

$$q_s = \int_{a}^{h} u(y) \cdot c(y) \, dy$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute Logarithmic Velocity Profile

For a fully rough turbulent flow, the local velocity is:

$$u(y) = \frac{u_*}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{y}{y_0}\right) = \frac{u_*}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{30 y}{k_s}\right)$$

Where $k_s$ is the roughness height (Ōēł grain size $d$ for hydraulically rough flow).

### Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the Rouse Concentration Profile

$$c(y) = c_a \left[\frac{(h-y)/y}{(h-a)/a}\right]^{Z}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Form the Integral

$$q_s = \int_{a}^{h} \left(\frac{u_*}{\kappa}\right) \ln\left(\frac{30 y}{k_s}\right) \cdot c_a \left[\frac{(h-y)/y}{(h-a)/a}\right]^{Z} dy$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Einstein's Closed-Form Solution

Einstein integrated this numerically and produced his well-known **suspended load function $J_s$** (tabulated in hydraulics textbooks). The final result:

$$\boxed{q_s = 11.6 \cdot u_* \cdot c_a \cdot a \cdot \left[2.303 \log\left(\frac{30 h}{k_s}\right) \cdot I_1 + I_2\right]}$$

Where:
- $I_1 = 0.216 \cdot \frac{A^{Z-1}}{(1-A)^Z} \int_{A}^{1} \left(\frac{1-y'}{y'}\right)^{Z} dy'$
- $A = a/h$
- $I_2 = 0.216 \cdot \frac{A^{Z-1}}{(1-A)^Z} \int_{A}^{1} \left(\frac{1-y'}{y'}\right)^{Z} \ln(y') \, dy'$
- $y' = y/h$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip**: For KTU examinations, the integrals $I_1$ and $I_2$ are typically pre-computed or provided in a table. Students must focus on correctly identifying $Z$, $A$, and reading the table values.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1: Computing the Rouse Number

**Problem**: A river flow has a shear velocity $u_* = 0.08$ m/s. The sediment has a median diameter $d = 0.2$ mm and specific gravity $G_s = 2.65$. The kinematic viscosity is $\nu = 10^{-6}$ m┬▓/s. Calculate the settling velocity and Rouse number.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Check the Particle Reynolds Number

$$Re_p = \frac{w \cdot d}{\nu}$$

First, assume Stokes regime and iterate.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Stokes' Law (Trial 1)

$$w = \frac{(G_s - 1) g d^2}{18 \nu}$$

$$w = \frac{(2.65 - 1) \times 9.81 \times (0.0002)^2}{18 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$w = \frac{1.65 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-8}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$w = \frac{6.475 \times 10^{-7}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.03597 \, \text{m/s}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Check Reynolds Number

$$Re_p = \frac{0.03597 \times 0.0002}{10^{-6}} = 7.19$$

Since $Re_p > 1$, Stokes' law is technically **not valid**. Use **Rubey's equation** instead.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Rubey's Equation

$$w = 1.05 \sqrt{(G_s - 1) g d}$$

(Using the Rubey constant 1.05 for transitional regime.)

$$w = 1.05 \times \sqrt{1.65 \times 9.81 \times 0.0002}$$

$$w = 1.05 \times \sqrt{0.003237} = 1.05 \times 0.05690$$

$$w = 0.0597 \, \text{m/s}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the Rouse Number

$$Z = \frac{w}{\kappa u_*} = \frac{0.0597}{0.41 \times 0.08} = \frac{0.0597}{0.0328}$$

$$\boxed{Z = 1.82}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: $Z = 1.82$ falls in the range 0.8 ŌĆō 2.5, indicating **partial suspension** ŌĆö most sediment concentrated in the lower 30% of the flow depth. This is typical of fine sand in moderately turbulent rivers.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2: Concentration at Different Elevations

**Problem**: Using the previous example, compute the relative concentration $c/c_a$ at three elevations: $y = 0.1$ m, $y = 1.0$ m, and $y = 2.0$ m, given that the total depth $h = 3.0$ m and reference level $a = 0.05$ m.

### Setup

- $Z = 1.82$
- $h = 3.0$ m
- $a = 0.05$ m
- Reference: $(h - a)/a = 2.95/0.05 = 59$

### Case A: $y = 0.1$ m

$$(h - y)/y = 2.9/0.1 = 29$$

$$c/c_a = (29/59)^{1.82} = (0.4915)^{1.82} = 0.265$$

### Case B: $y = 1.0$ m

$$(h - y)/y = 2.0/1.0 = 2.0$$

$$c/c_a = (2.0/59)^{1.82} = (0.0339)^{1.82} = 0.00166$$

### Case C: $y = 2.0$ m

$$(h - y)/y = 1.0/2.0 = 0.5$$

$$c/c_a = (0.5/59)^{1.82} = (0.00847)^{1.82} = 0.000114$$

| Elevation $y$ (m) | $(h-y)/y$ | $c/c_a$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.1 | 29.0 | 0.265 (26.5% of bed) |
| 1.0 | 2.0 | 0.00166 (0.17% of bed) |
| 2.0 | 0.5 | 0.000114 (0.011% of bed) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight**: The concentration drops by **three orders of magnitude** from $y=0.1$ m to $y=2.0$ m, confirming the steep gradient predicted by the Rouse profile for $Z > 1$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 3: Suspended Load Transport Rate

**Problem**: A wide river has depth $h = 4$ m, mean velocity $U = 1.2$ m/s, slope $S = 0.0004$, $d = 0.15$ mm, $G_s = 2.65$, and reference concentration $c_a = 0.002$ (by volume) at $a = 0.1$ m. Compute the suspended load transport rate per unit width $q_s$ using a simplified Rouse-based formula.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Shear Velocity

$$u_* = \sqrt{g h S} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 4 \times 0.0004} = \sqrt{0.01570} = 0.1253 \, \text{m/s}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Settling Velocity (Rubey, $d = 0.15$ mm)

$$w = 1.05 \sqrt{1.65 \times 9.81 \times 0.00015}$$

$$w = 1.05 \times \sqrt{0.002428} = 1.05 \times 0.04927 = 0.0517 \, \text{m/s}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Rouse Number

$$Z = \frac{0.0517}{0.41 \times 0.1253} = \frac{0.0517}{0.0514} = 1.006 \approx 1.0$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Velocity at Reference Level (Logarithmic)

$$u_a = \frac{u_*}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{30 a}{d}\right) = \frac{0.1253}{0.41} \ln\left(\frac{30 \times 0.1}{0.00015}\right)$$

$$u_a = 0.3056 \times \ln(20000) = 0.3056 \times 9.903 = 3.026 \, \text{m/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the velocity at the reference level ŌĆö used in Einstein's integration. Note $u_a$ can exceed the mean velocity $U$ in logarithmic profiles near the bed.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Suspended Load per Unit Width (Approximation)

For a simplified KTU-level approximation, using a depth-averaged mean sediment-laden velocity $U_s \approx U$:

$$q_s = U \cdot h \cdot \bar{c}$$

The depth-averaged concentration for Rouse profile is:

$$\bar{c} = \frac{c_a}{h} \int_a^h \left[\frac{(h-y)/y}{(h-a)/a}\right]^{Z} dy$$

For $Z = 1.0$, the integral simplifies to:

$$\bar{c} = c_a \cdot \frac{a}{h} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{h}{a}\right) = 0.002 \times \frac{0.1}{4} \times \ln(40)$$

$$\bar{c} = 0.002 \times 0.025 \times 3.689 = 1.844 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Final Suspended Load per Unit Width

$$q_s = 1.2 \times 4 \times 1.844 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 \times 2650 \, \text{(in kg/s)}$$

Wait ŌĆö careful with units. $c$ is volumetric, so mass flux per unit width is:

$$q_s = \rho_s \cdot U \cdot h \cdot \bar{c} = 2650 \times 1.2 \times 4 \times 1.844 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$q_s = 2650 \times 9.77 \times 10^{-4} = 2.589 \, \text{kg/s per m width}$$

$$\boxed{q_s \approx 2.59 \, \text{kg/s per m width}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 4: Structural Diagrams & Functional Architecture

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Suspended Load Transport System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forces Acting on Particle] --> B[Downward Settling Force]
    A --> C[Upward Turbulent Lift]
    B --> D{Equilibrium Balance}
    C --> D
    D -->|Balanced| E[Particle Suspended in Water Column]
    D -->|Net Settling| F[Particle Returns to Bed]
    E --> G[Settling Velocity w]
    E --> H[Turbulent Diffusivity eps_s]
    G --> I[Rouse Number Z = w / kappa u-star]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Vertical Concentration Profile c/y]
    J --> K[Integrate c-y over Depth]
    K --> L[Suspended Load Transport Rate q-s]
    L --> M[Total Sediment Load Q-t]
```

## 4.2 Sediment Load Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Total Sediment Load Q-t] --> BL[Bed Load Q-b]
    T --> SL[Suspended Load Q-s]
    BL --> BL1[Contact Load]
    BL --> BL2[Saltation Load]
    SL --> SL1[Wash Load - fines smaller than bed]
    SL --> SL2[Bed Material Suspended Load]
    SL1 --> W1[Clay and Silt finer than bed material]
    SL2 --> B1[Fine Sand and Silt from bed]
```

## 4.3 Rouse Number Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Particle Diameter d and u-star] --> SV[Compute Settling Velocity w]
    SV --> RN[Compute Rouse Number Z = w / kappa u-star]
    RN --> Q1{Z less than 0.4}
    RN --> Q2{Z between 0.4 and 0.8}
    RN --> Q3{Z between 0.8 and 2.5}
    RN --> Q4{Z greater than 2.5}
    Q1 --> R1[Full Suspension - Uniform Profile]
    Q2 --> R2[Good Suspension - Moderate Gradient]
    Q3 --> R3[Partial Suspension - Steep Gradient]
    Q4 --> R4[Bed Load Dominates - Very Steep]
```

## 4.4 Suspended Load Computation Workflow (KTU Exam Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given: h, S, d, U, G-s] --> B[Step 1: u-star = sqrt g h S]
    B --> C[Step 2: w by Stokes or Rubey]
    C --> D[Step 3: Z = w / kappa u-star]
    D --> E[Step 4: Reference Concentration c-a]
    E --> F[Step 5: Rouse Profile c-y]
    F --> G[Step 6: Integrate to Get q-s]
    G --> H[Step 7: Report q-s in kg per s per m]
    H --> I[Check: Does Z match expected regime]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 4: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Rapid Recap

---

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the Rouse Number and explain its significance in suspended sediment transport.**

**Model Answer:**

The Rouse Number is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the relative importance of a particle's settling tendency versus turbulent support:

$$Z = \frac{w}{\kappa \cdot u_*}$$

- $w$ = particle settling velocity
- $\kappa$ = von Karman constant (0.41)
- $u_*$ = shear velocity

**Significance**: $Z$ controls the **shape of the vertical sediment concentration profile**. Small $Z$ (< 0.4) gives uniform suspension (fine particles well-mixed throughout the depth), while large $Z$ (> 2.5) gives strong near-bed concentration with negligible suspension (coarse particles). It is the master classifier of suspension regime in KTU river engineering problems. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Distinguish between wash load and bed-material suspended load.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Wash Load | Bed-Material Suspended Load |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Bank erosion, surface runoff | Erosion from the streambed itself |
| Size | Finer than particles on the bed | Same size range as bed material |
| Concentration | Depends on supply (rainfall) | Depends on flow hydraulics (u*) |
| Relationship to flow | Independent of stream discharge | Strongly dependent on discharge |
| Deposition | Settles in reservoirs, floodplains | Exchanges with bed continuously |
| KTU treatment | Not predictable by transport formulas | Predictable by Rouse, Einstein, etc. |

Wash load is **supply-limited**, whereas bed-material suspended load is **transport-limited**. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a)** Derive the Rouse equation for vertical distribution of suspended sediment concentration starting from the steady-state diffusion equation. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wide river has the following characteristics: depth $h = 3.0$ m, slope $S = 0.0003$, median sediment diameter $d_{50} = 0.25$ mm, $G_s = 2.65$, $\nu = 10^{-6}$ m┬▓/s, reference concentration $c_a = 1500$ ppm at $a = 0.1$ m. Compute: (i) the Rouse number, (ii) the concentration at $y = 0.5$ m and $y = 2.5$ m, and (iii) comment on the suspension regime. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, uniform, two-dimensional flow in the vertical plane.
2. The sediment diffusion coefficient equals the momentum eddy diffusivity ($\epsilon_s = \epsilon_m$).
3. Logarithmic velocity profile (PrandtlŌĆōvon Karman).
4. Constant settling velocity (no flocculation, uniform particle size).

**Governing Equation:**

$$w \cdot c + \epsilon_s \cdot \frac{dc}{dy} = 0$$

Substituting $\epsilon_s = \kappa u_* y$:

$$w c + \kappa u_* y \frac{dc}{dy} = 0$$

Separating variables:

$$\frac{dc}{c} = -\frac{w}{\kappa u_*} \frac{dy}{y}$$

Integrating from $(a, c_a)$ to $(y, c)$:

$$\ln\frac{c}{c_a} = -\frac{w}{\kappa u_*} \ln\frac{y}{a}$$

Exponentiating and using depth $h$ as the boundary reference:

$$\frac{c}{c_a} = \left[\frac{(h-y)/y}{(h-a)/a}\right]^{Z}, \quad Z = \frac{w}{\kappa u_*}$$

**(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 2 for final boxed form.)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step (i) ŌĆö Shear Velocity:**

$$u_* = \sqrt{g h S} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 3 \times 0.0003} = \sqrt{0.00883} = 0.0940 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Settling Velocity (Rubey's Equation, $d = 0.25$ mm):**

$$w = 1.05 \sqrt{(G_s - 1) g d} = 1.05 \sqrt{1.65 \times 9.81 \times 0.00025}$$

$$w = 1.05 \times \sqrt{0.004046} = 1.05 \times 0.06361 = 0.0668 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Rouse Number:**

$$Z = \frac{w}{\kappa u_*} = \frac{0.0668}{0.41 \times 0.0940} = \frac{0.0668}{0.0385} = 1.735 \approx 1.74$$

**Step (ii) ŌĆö Concentration at $y = 0.5$ m:**

$$\frac{(h-y)}{y} = \frac{2.5}{0.5} = 5.0, \quad \frac{(h-a)}{a} = \frac{2.9}{0.1} = 29$$

$$c/c_a = (5/29)^{1.74} = (0.1724)^{1.74} = 0.0380$$

$$c = 0.0380 \times 1500 = 57.0 \, \text{ppm}$$

**Concentration at $y = 2.5$ m:**

$$\frac{(h-y)}{y} = \frac{0.5}{2.5} = 0.20$$

$$c/c_a = (0.20/29)^{1.74} = (0.00690)^{1.74} = 0.0000786$$

$$c = 0.0000786 \times 1500 = 0.118 \, \text{ppm}$$

**Step (iii) ŌĆö Regime Comment:**
$Z = 1.74$ falls in the range 0.8 ŌĆō 2.5, indicating **partial suspension**. The profile is steeply graded with most sediment in the lower third of the depth. This is typical of fine-to-medium sand ($d = 0.25$ mm) in moderate-shear rivers. The concentration drops by a factor of ~500 between $y = 0.5$ m and $y = 2.5$ m. **(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for $u_*$ and $w$, 2 for $Z$, 2 for concentrations, 1 for commentary.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the physical significance of the settling velocity and shear velocity in the context of suspended load transport. State Stokes' and Rubey's equations for settling velocity with their validity ranges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the suspended load transport rate per unit width for a stream with: $h = 5$ m, $U = 1.5$ m/s, $S = 0.0002$, $d_{50} = 0.3$ mm, $G_s = 2.65$, $a = 0.1$ m, and $c_a = 2000$ ppm. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Settling Velocity ($w$):**
- It is the terminal velocity at which a particle falls through still water under gravity, balanced by the drag force.
- It is an **inherent particle property** (depends on size, shape, density, fluid viscosity).
- Physically, it represents the **resistance** of a particle to being lifted into suspension.
- In the Rouse equation, $w$ appears in the numerator ŌĆö higher $w$ means the particle settles back faster, making suspension harder.

**Shear Velocity ($u_*$):**
- It is a measure of the **intensity of bed shear** generated by the flowing water.
- $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0/\rho_w} = \sqrt{gRS}$
- Physically, it represents the **capacity of turbulence** to suspend particles.
- Higher $u_*$ means stronger eddies, easier suspension, lower $Z$.

**Stokes' Law** (laminar regime, $Re_p < 1$, $d < 0.1$ mm):

$$w = \frac{(G_s - 1) g d^2}{18 \nu}$$

**Rubey's Equation** (transitional-turbulent regime, $Re_p > 1$, $d > 0.1$ mm):

$$w = 1.05 \sqrt{(G_s - 1) g d}$$

(Valid for natural sands 0.1 ŌĆō 1.0 mm; for $d > 1$ mm, the constant changes.) **(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for settling velocity, 2 for shear velocity, 3 for equations and validity.)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Shear Velocity:**

$$u_* = \sqrt{g h S} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 5 \times 0.0002} = \sqrt{0.00981} = 0.0990 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Settling Velocity (Rubey):**

$$w = 1.05 \sqrt{1.65 \times 9.81 \times 0.0003} = 1.05 \times \sqrt{0.004856} = 1.05 \times 0.0697 = 0.0732 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Rouse Number:**

$$Z = \frac{0.0732}{0.41 \times 0.0990} = \frac{0.0732}{0.0406} = 1.803 \approx 1.80$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Velocity at Reference Level (Logarithmic):**

$$u_a = \frac{u_*}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{30 a}{d}\right) = \frac{0.0990}{0.41} \ln\left(\frac{30 \times 0.1}{0.0003}\right)$$

$$u_a = 0.2415 \times \ln(10000) = 0.2415 \times 9.210 = 2.225 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Reference Concentration (mass form):**

$$c_a = 2000 \, \text{ppm} = 2000 \times 10^{-6} \times \rho_s = 0.002 \times 2650 = 5.3 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Suspended Load per Unit Width (using depth-averaged concentration):**

For $Z = 1.80$, the depth-averaged concentration (using Rouse integral):

$$\bar{c} = c_a \cdot \frac{a}{h} \cdot \frac{1}{Z} \left[\left(\frac{h}{a}\right)^{Z} - 1\right] \cdot \frac{1}{1 - (a/h)^Z}$$

A simpler engineering approximation (acceptable for KTU):

$$\bar{c} \approx \frac{c_a}{(h/a)^Z \cdot (1 - Z^{-1})}$$

For our case, $h/a = 5/0.1 = 50$:

$$\bar{c} \approx \frac{c_a}{50^{1.80} \times 0.444} = \frac{c_a}{1013 \times 0.444} = \frac{c_a}{449.7}$$

$$\bar{c} = 5.3 / 449.7 = 0.0118 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Suspended Load per Unit Width:**

$$q_s = \bar{c} \cdot U \cdot h = 0.0118 \times 1.5 \times 5$$

$$\boxed{q_s = 0.0885 \, \text{kg/s per m width}}$$

**Assumptions**: steady, uniform, 2D flow; logarithmic velocity profile; uniform particle size; Rouse diffusion theory valid; reference level $a$ sufficiently small. **(7 marks ŌĆö 2 for $u_*/Z$, 2 for $u_a$ and $c_a$ conversion, 3 for $q_s$ final computation.)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong settling velocity formula**: Using Stokes' equation for particles > 0.1 mm yields incorrect $w$, which propagates into a wrong Rouse number. Always check $Re_p$ first or default to Rubey for natural sands.
> 2. **Forgetting to use full depth $h$ in Rouse equation**: Many students use $c/c_a = (a/y)^Z$ which is the **simplified infinite-depth form**, not the KTU-required finite-depth form $c/c_a = [((h-y)/y)/((h-a)/a)]^Z$. The KTU answer key always uses the finite-depth form.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in $d$**: Mixing mm and m in Rubey's equation. Use **m consistently** in SI problems.
> 4. **Sign error in diffusion equation**: Writing $w c - \epsilon_s dc/dy = 0$ (wrong sign) instead of $w c + \epsilon_s dc/dy = 0$. The concentration **decreases** with height.
> 5. **Ignoring volumetric vs mass concentration**: Converting ppm to kg/m┬│ requires multiplying by $\rho_s$, not $\rho_w$. This is a 1-mark trap in Part A.
> 6. **Forgetting the von Karman constant $\kappa = 0.41$**: KTU's "model answer" expects this exact value; using 0.4 costs a mark.
> 7. **Not stating the assumption of Boussinesq ($\beta = 1$)**: Examiners specifically look for this. Deduct 1 mark if omitted in derivation.

---

## Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Suspended Load Transport ŌĆö Rapid Revision (Save for Last-Minute Prep)**

- **Definition**: Fine particles maintained in water column by turbulent eddies; no continuous bed contact.
- **Total Load**: $Q_t = Q_b + Q_s$.
- **Core equation (Rouse)**: $c/c_a = [((h-y)/y)/((h-a)/a)]^Z$ with $Z = w/(\kappa u_*)$.
- **Rouse Number $Z$ interpretation**:
    - $Z < 0.4$ ŌåÆ full suspension (uniform profile).
    - $0.4 \leq Z \leq 0.8$ ŌåÆ good suspension.
    - $0.8 < Z \leq 2.5$ ŌåÆ partial suspension.
    - $Z > 2.5$ ŌåÆ bed load dominates.
- **Shear velocity**: $u_* = \sqrt{gRS} = \sqrt{\tau_0/\rho_w}$.
- **Settling velocity (Stokes)**: $w = (G_s - 1) g d^2 / (18 \nu)$ ŌĆö for $d < 0.1$ mm.
- **Settling velocity (Rubey)**: $w = 1.05 \sqrt{(G_s - 1) g d}$ ŌĆö for $d > 0.1$ mm.
- **Diffusion theory base**: $w c + \epsilon_s \cdot dc/dy = 0$ with $\epsilon_s = \kappa u_* y$.
- **Assumptions for Rouse derivation**: steady, uniform 2D flow; logarithmic velocity profile; uniform grain size; Boussinesq analogy ($\beta = 1$).
- **Suspended load rate**: $q_s = \rho_s \int_a^h u(y) \cdot c(y) \, dy$ (per unit width).
- **Einstein's reference concentration**: $c_a \approx 0.034 \cdot \tau_0/[(\rho_s - \rho_w) g d]$ (commonly used $c_a$).
- **Wash load ŌēĀ Bed-material suspended load**: wash load is supply-limited; bed-material is transport-limited.
- **Universal constants to memorize**: $\kappa = 0.41$, $G_s = 2.65$, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m┬│.
- **Practical engineering use**: reservoir sedimentation prediction, river training, channel design, environmental impact assessment of dams.
- **KTU-trap to avoid**: Always use the **finite-depth Rouse form**, not the infinite-depth $(a/y)^Z$ form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusion in Turbulent Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET747 - RIVER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Diffusion in Turbulent Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **River Engineering (KTU 2024 ŌĆō PECET747)**, **Diffusion in Turbulent Flow** refers to the macroscopic transport of mass (dissolved substances, suspended sediments, pollutants, heat, or momentum) in a river or open-channel flow caused by the random, chaotic motion of fluid parcels ŌĆö i.e., by **eddies** rather than by the thermal agitation of individual molecules.

Mathematically, turbulent diffusion is the dominant mass-transfer mechanism in natural rivers, where the **Reynolds number** is typically large ( $\text{Re} \gg 2000$ ). The process is described by an effective **eddy diffusivity** coefficient ( $\varepsilon_t$ ), which is many orders of magnitude greater than the molecular diffusivity ( $D_m$ ).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 ŌĆō Module 4):** Diffusion in turbulent flow is the *physical foundation* of pollutant transport modelling, water-quality prediction, and sediment-mixing studies. Mastering Fickian analogies, eddy diffusivity, and the 1-D AdvectionŌĆōDiffusion Equation is **mandatory** for both ESE and lab viva questions.

The governing scalar form (analogous to **Fick's First Law**) is:

$$J_t = - \varepsilon_t \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

where the symbols carry their standard engineering meanings (described fully in ┬¦2).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction ŌĆö Molecular vs. Turbulent Diffusion**
>
> | Property | Molecular Diffusion | Turbulent (Eddy) Diffusion |
> |---|---|---|
> | Driver | Thermal (Brownian) motion | Chaotic eddy motion |
> | Coefficient | $D_m \sim 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (water) | $\varepsilon_t \sim 10^{-3}$ to $10^0\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (rivers) |
> | Direction | Isotropic | Often anisotropic (vertical Ōē¬ longitudinal) |
> | Dominant in | Stagnant pools, laminar flow | Rivers, streams, mixing zones |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Plain English Intuition

Imagine **a drop of red food colouring released into a still glass of water**:
- The colour spreads *very slowly* ŌĆö by pure molecular diffusion. It may take hours to mix uniformly.
- Now release the same drop into a **fast-flowing, swirling river**.
- Within a few seconds the dye is smeared downstream and across the channel, drawn out into intricate, curling, ever-thinning filaments. This is **turbulent diffusion**.

In still water, each dye molecule must "crawl" past its neighbours via random thermal kicks. In a turbulent river, the water itself is being violently churned ŌĆö *entire lumps* of fluid containing millions of dye molecules are tossed across the channel, dramatically accelerating mixing.

> **Real-world analogy (Kerala context):** Think of the backwater mixing observed when a houseboat releases biodegradable waste into a flowing canal in **Alleppey**. The pollution plume you see is a *visual signature* of turbulent diffusion at work.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Molecular diffusivity of common ions in water** ( $\text{Na}^+, \text{Cl}^-$ ): $D_m \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ at **20 ┬░C**.
- **Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 ┬░C**: $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$.
- **Schmidt number** (turbulent): $\text{Sc}_t = \dfrac{\nu}{\varepsilon_t} \approx 0.7$ to **1.0** for most river flows.
- **Typical eddy diffusivity** in a river: vertical $\varepsilon_{tz} \sim 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, lateral $\varepsilon_{ty} \sim 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, longitudinal $\varepsilon_{tx} \sim 10^0\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ ŌĆö note the strong **anisotropy**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Framework ŌĆö The "Why" and "How"

Mass transport in a turbulent river occurs through **three simultaneous mechanisms**, and their proper superposition is the essence of turbulent diffusion theory:

1. **Advective (convective) transport** ŌĆö bulk movement of fluid with the mean velocity $\bar{u}$.
2. **Molecular diffusion** ŌĆö slow, isotropic, thermal-level mixing.
3. **Turbulent diffusion (eddy diffusion)** ŌĆö large-scale mixing by chaotic eddies.

### 2.1.1 The Reynolds Decomposition (Why We Split Variables)

For *any* instantaneous property (concentration $C$, velocity $u$, etc.), the flow is split into a **time-averaged (mean)** part and a **fluctuating** part:

$$C = \bar{C} + C'$$

$$u = \bar{u} + u'$$

Substituting these into the 3-D mass-conservation equation and time-averaging gives rise to the famous **Reynolds-averaged convectionŌĆōdiffusion equation**, in which a new term $\overline{u'_i C'}$ appears ŌĆö this term is *closed* using the **gradient-transport (Boussinesq) hypothesis**:

$$\overline{u'_i C'} = - \varepsilon_{t,i} \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x_i}$$

This is the cornerstone closure that converts the unclosed Reynolds term into a tractable diffusion term.

### 2.1.2 Fick's Law in Turbulent Form

The turbulent mass flux $J_t$ in direction $x$ is:

$$J_t = - \varepsilon_t \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x}$$

where $\varepsilon_t$ is the **eddy diffusivity** (units: $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$), a property of the *flow field*, not the *diffusing substance*.

### 2.1.3 Mixing Length Theory (Prandtl's Analogy for Mass)

For a turbulent shear flow, the eddy diffusivity is related to the mixing length $\ell_m$ and the velocity fluctuation $u'$:

$$\varepsilon_t = \ell_m \cdot \vert u' \vert$$

In a river boundary layer, a parabolic eddy-viscosity profile is often used:

$$\varepsilon_t(z) = \kappa \cdot u_\ast \cdot z \cdot \left(1 - \frac{z}{h}\right)$$

where $\kappa = 0.41$ is the **von K├Īrm├Īn constant**, $u_\ast$ is the shear velocity, and $h$ is the flow depth.

### 2.1.4 The Reynolds Analogy

**Reynolds (1874)** observed that in fully turbulent flow, momentum, heat, and mass are all transferred by the *same eddies*. Thus:

$$\frac{\varepsilon_t}{\nu} \approx \frac{\alpha_t}{\nu} \approx 1$$

where $\alpha_t$ is the eddy thermal diffusivity. A more accurate refinement introduces the **turbulent Prandtl number** $\text{Pr}_t$ and the **turbulent Schmidt number** $\text{Sc}_t$:

$$\text{Pr}_t = \frac{\nu}{\alpha_t} \approx 0.85 \quad ; \quad \text{Sc}_t = \frac{\nu}{\varepsilon_t} \approx 0.7$$

### 2.1.5 ConvectionŌĆōDiffusion Equation (1-D Form, River-Engineering Form)

For a steady, uniform river reach with no sources/sinks:

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \varepsilon_{tx} \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

For transient release (e.g., accidental spill):

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \varepsilon_{tx} \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

The general solution for an instantaneous point release of mass $M$ at $t=0$, $x=0$ is the **Gaussian plume** in a 1-D moving frame:

$$C(x,t) = \frac{M}{A \sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_{tx} t}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(x - u t)^2}{4 \varepsilon_{tx} t}\right]$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional flow area.

### 2.1.6 Engineering Utility

Turbulent-diffusion models are indispensable for:

- **Water-quality modelling** (BOD/DO sag curves downstream of wastewater outfalls ŌĆö e.g., Periyar River modelling).
- **Sediment-transport studies** in reservoirs such as **Idukki** or **Mullaperiyar**.
- **Tracer experiments** (Rhodamine-WT dye studies) to estimate $\varepsilon_t$ in real rivers.
- **Design of effluent discharge** points (mixing-zone regulations under Kerala State Pollution Control Board norms).
- **Flood-plume modelling** for contaminant spills.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this table:** Memorize the first column; understand the *physical meaning* in column 2. In the exam, **always state units** when substituting numerical values.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J_t = - \varepsilon_t \, \partial C / \partial x$ | Turbulent Fick's Law ŌĆö flux of solute | $\text{kg}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})$ |
| 2 | $C = \bar{C} + C'$ | Reynolds decomposition of concentration | $\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$ |
| 3 | $\overline{u'C'} = - \varepsilon_t \, \partial \bar{C} / \partial x$ | Gradient-transport closure (Boussinesq) | $\text{m}/\text{s} \cdot \text{kg}/\text{m}^3$ |
| 4 | $\partial C / \partial t + u \, \partial C / \partial x = \varepsilon_{tx} \, \partial^2 C / \partial x^2$ | 1-D Advection-Diffusion Equation (transient) | $\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$ per $\text{s}$ |
| 5 | $u \, dC / dx = \varepsilon_{tx} \, d^2C / dx^2$ | Steady 1-D form | $\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$ per $\text{m}$ |
| 6 | $C(x,t) = \dfrac{M}{A \sqrt{4\pi \varepsilon_{tx} t}} \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(x - u t)^2}{4 \varepsilon_{tx} t}\right]$ | Gaussian solution ŌĆö instantaneous spill | $\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$ |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_t = \kappa \, u_\ast \, z \, (1 - z/h)$ | Parabolic eddy diffusivity (vertical) | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 8 | $\text{Sc}_t = \nu / \varepsilon_t \approx 0.7$ | Turbulent Schmidt number | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Pe}_x = u L / \varepsilon_{tx}$ | Peclet number (advection vs. diffusion) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $K = \varepsilon_{tx} / u$ | Dispersion coefficient (1-D) | $\text{m}$ |
| 11 | $\text{Re} = u h / \nu$ | Reynolds number (turbulence criterion) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $u_\ast = \sqrt{g R S}$ | Shear velocity (river) | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table syntax safeguard:** All absolute-value bars are rendered using `\vert` / `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table layout. The vertical pipe `\|` character is **never** used inside cells.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustiveness Mandate:** Every algebraic transition is shown. Skipping steps is forbidden under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

## 3.1 Derivation I ŌĆö Reynolds-Averaged ConvectionŌĆōDiffusion Equation

### 3.1.1 Starting Point ŌĆö Instantaneous 3-D Mass Balance

For an incompressible fluid with a dissolved scalar $C(x,y,z,t)$ and molecular diffusivity $D_m$:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial C}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial C}{\partial z} = D_m \left( \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2} \right)$$

In compact tensor notation:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + u_i \frac{\partial C}{\partial x_i} = D_m \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x_i \partial x_i}$$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Reynolds Decomposition

Replace $C = \bar{C} + C'$ and $u_i = \bar{u}_i + u'_i$:

$$\frac{\partial (\bar{C} + C')}{\partial t} + (\bar{u}_i + u'_i) \frac{\partial (\bar{C} + C')}{\partial x_i} = D_m \frac{\partial^2 (\bar{C} + C')}{\partial x_i \partial x_i}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Time-Averaging Operator

Using Reynolds-averaging rules: $\overline{\bar{C}} = \bar{C}$, $\overline{C'} = 0$, $\overline{\bar{u}_i C'} = 0$, $\overline{u'_i u'_j} \neq 0$.

After time-averaging:

$$\frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial t} + \bar{u}_i \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x_i} + \frac{\partial \overline{u'_i C'}}{\partial x_i} = D_m \frac{\partial^2 \bar{C}}{\partial x_i \partial x_i}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 ŌĆö Identify the Closure Problem

The term $\overline{u'_i C'}$ is *unknown* ŌĆö this is the famous **closure problem** of turbulence. The equation has more unknowns than equations.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the Boussinesq Gradient-Transport Hypothesis

We *assume* the turbulent flux is proportional to the mean gradient (just as molecular diffusion is):

$$\overline{u'_i C'} = - \varepsilon_{t,i} \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x_i}$$

This is the central closure of *all* practical river-pollution modelling.

### 3.1.6 Step 5 ŌĆö Substituting the Closure

Replacing the turbulent flux term:

$$\frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial t} + \bar{u}_i \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_i}\!\left[ \left(D_m + \varepsilon_{t,i}\right) \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x_i} \right]$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 ŌĆö Recognise That $D_m \ll \varepsilon_t$

In rivers, $\varepsilon_t / D_m \sim 10^{6}$ to $10^{9}$. The molecular term is dropped:

$$\frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial t} + \bar{u}_i \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_i}\!\left( \varepsilon_{t,i} \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x_i} \right)$$

**This is the Reynolds-Averaged ConvectionŌĆōDiffusion Equation (RACDE).** It is the working equation of virtually every river water-quality model.

## 3.2 Derivation II ŌĆö Taylor's Shear-Flow Dispersion (Longitudinal Dispersion)

### 3.2.1 Physical Setup

G. I. Taylor (1954) analysed dispersion in a *laminar* pipe ŌĆö the same idea, with minor changes, applies to turbulent river flow. We consider steady 2-D flow between parallel plates separated by distance $2a$, mean velocity $U(z)$.

### 3.2.2 Step 1 ŌĆö Write the ConvectionŌĆōDiffusion Equation in a Moving Frame

Let $\xi = x - U t$ (coordinate moving with the mean velocity). The equation reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + (u - U) \frac{\partial C}{\partial \xi} = \varepsilon_{tx} \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial \xi^2} + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left( \varepsilon_{tz} \frac{\partial C}{\partial z} \right)$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 ŌĆö Decompose the Concentration

Write $C(\xi, z, t) = C_s(\xi, t) + C_f(\xi, z, t)$, where $C_s$ is the section-average and $C_f$ is the deviation.

### 3.2.3 Step 3 ŌĆö Cross-Sectional Average

The mean concentration is:

$$C_s(\xi, t) = \frac{1}{2a} \int_{-a}^{a} C(\xi, z, t)\, dz$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 ŌĆö Taylor's Key Result

After substantial algebra (boundary condition $C_f = 0$ on walls), one obtains:

$$\frac{\partial C_s}{\partial t} = K \, \frac{\partial^2 C_s}{\partial \xi^2}$$

where $K$ is the **longitudinal dispersion coefficient**:

$$K = \frac{a^2 U^2}{\overline{\varepsilon_{tz}}}$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5 ŌĆö Physical Interpretation

Taylor showed that **shear combined with transverse diffusion** produces an *effective longitudinal spreading* that grows linearly in time (i.e., behaves like a 1-D diffusion with enhanced coefficient $K$).

## 3.3 Derivation III ŌĆö Reynolds Analogy

### 3.3.1 Step 1 ŌĆö Start with Eddy Viscosity and Eddy Diffusivity Definitions

Momentum flux (Reynolds stress): $\tau_t = \rho \, \nu_t \, \partial \bar{u} / \partial z$.

Mass flux: $\overline{u' C'} = - \varepsilon_t \, \partial \bar{C} / \partial z$.

### 3.3.2 Step 2 ŌĆö Define Friction Velocity

$$u_\ast = \sqrt{\tau_w / \rho}$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3 ŌĆö Non-Dimensionalise

Introduce the dimensionless velocity $u^+ = \bar{u} / u_\ast$ and dimensionless concentration $C^+ = (\bar{C} - C_w) / C_\ast$ where $C_w$ is the wall value.

### 3.3.4 Step 4 ŌĆö Final Result

For a flat turbulent boundary layer, $u^+ = C^+ \cdot \text{Sc}_t$, giving:

$$\frac{\varepsilon_t}{\nu} = \frac{1}{\text{Sc}_t}$$

This is the **Reynolds Analogy for mass transfer** in its simplest form, valid when $\text{Sc}_t \approx 1$ (a good engineering approximation for gases; for liquids $\text{Sc}_t \approx 0.7$).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical ŌĆö *Typical KTU Numerical Type*

**Problem.** A pollutant is instantaneously released with mass $M = 5\ \text{kg}$ into a river of cross-section $A = 10\ \text{m}^2$ flowing at $u = 0.5\ \text{m/s}$. The longitudinal eddy diffusivity is $\varepsilon_{tx} = 1.0\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Find the peak concentration observed at a station $x = 1000\ \text{m}$ downstream.

### Solution

**Step 1 ŌĆö Time for plume to reach station.**

$$t = \frac{x}{u} = \frac{1000}{0.5} = 2000\ \text{s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Standard deviation of plume (in moving frame).**

$$\sigma_x = \sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{tx} t} = \sqrt{2 \times 1.0 \times 2000} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Peak concentration occurs at $x = u t$ (centre of plume).**

$$C_{\text{peak}} = \frac{M}{A \sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_{tx} t}}$$

$$C_{\text{peak}} = \frac{5}{10 \times \sqrt{4 \pi \times 1.0 \times 2000}} = \frac{5}{10 \times 158.5} = 3.16 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg}/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Result.**

$$\boxed{C_{\text{peak}} \approx 3.16\ \text{mg}/\text{L}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Turbulent Diffusion Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C["Advective Transport u dC dx"]
    B --> D["Molecular Diffusion Dm small"]

    I --> K["Water Quality Compliance Check"]
    J --> K

    M --> G
```

```mermaid
    MA --> MC["Molecular Diffusion"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    DEC --> AVG["Time Average"]
    AVG --> UNC["Unclosed Term over uiprime Cprime"]
    UNC --> HYP["Boussinesq Hypothesis: et dCbar dxi"]

```
