<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (2 ├Ś 3 marks = 6 marks)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**Differentiate between molecular diffusion and turbulent (eddy) diffusion in a river flow. Why is turbulent diffusion dominant in most natural rivers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Molecular Diffusion | Turbulent Diffusion |
|---|---|---|
| Driving mechanism | Thermal (Brownian) motion of molecules | Chaotic motion of fluid eddies |
| Coefficient magnitude | $D_m \sim 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | $\varepsilon_t \sim 10^{-3}$ to $1\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| Nature | Isotropic | Generally anisotropic |
| Dominance | Negligible in turbulent rivers | Dominant ($\varepsilon_t / D_m \sim 10^6$ to $10^9$) |

In natural rivers, $\text{Re} \gg 2000$ ŌĆö the flow is fully turbulent. The eddy size (mm to m scale) is many orders of magnitude larger than molecular mean-free-paths. Hence eddies transport mass far more efficiently than molecular agitation, making turbulent diffusion the controlling mechanism for pollutant mixing. **(3 marks ŌĆö full credit only if all three comparison points + the dominance ratio justification are given.)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Define the turbulent Schmidt number ($\text{Sc}_t$) and state its typical range for open-channel flow.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The turbulent Schmidt number is the ratio of eddy viscosity (momentum diffusivity) to eddy mass diffusivity:

$$\text{Sc}_t = \frac{\nu_t}{\varepsilon_t} = \frac{\nu}{\varepsilon_t}\quad \text{(high-Re limit)}$$

It is a dimensionless measure of the relative effectiveness of eddies in transporting momentum versus mass. For typical open-channel / river flows, $\text{Sc}_t \approx 0.7$ to $1.0$, with a common engineering assumption of $\text{Sc}_t = 0.7$ (Reynolds analogy). The fact that $\text{Sc}_t < 1$ implies eddies are *slightly more efficient* at transporting mass than momentum. **(3 marks ŌĆö must include definition, formula, and numerical range.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 14 marks each)

### **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Starting from the instantaneous 3-D mass balance, derive the **Reynolds-Averaged ConvectionŌĆōDiffusion Equation (RACDE)** for a passive scalar in turbulent incompressible flow. Clearly state each step, including the closure hypothesis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A river reach 5 km long has a mean velocity $u = 0.4\ \text{m/s}$ and depth $h = 2\ \text{m}$. The bed slope is $S = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ and $n$ (Manning) $\approx 0.03$. An instantaneous spill of $M = 20\ \text{kg}$ of a conservative tracer occurs at $t=0$ at $x=0$. Estimate the peak concentration at the downstream end of the reach and the time of its arrival. Use a typical value of $\varepsilon_{tx} = 0.5\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ and assume the cross-sectional area $A = 20\ \text{m}^2$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution A(a) ŌĆö Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the instantaneous mass balance:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + u_i \frac{\partial C}{\partial x_i} = D_m \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x_i \partial x_i}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply Reynolds decomposition $C = \bar{C} + C'$, $u_i = \bar{u}_i + u'_i$:

$$\frac{\partial (\bar{C} + C')}{\partial t} + (\bar{u}_i + u'_i) \frac{\partial (\bar{C} + C')}{\partial x_i} = D_m \frac{\partial^2 (\bar{C} + C')}{\partial x_i \partial x_i}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the time-average operator, using $\overline{C'} = 0$ and $\overline{\bar{u}_i C'} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial t} + \bar{u}_i \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x_i} + \frac{\partial \overline{u'_i C'}}{\partial x_i} = D_m \frac{\partial^2 \bar{C}}{\partial x_i \partial x_i}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Identify the unclosed term $\overline{u'_i C'}$ ŌĆö *closure problem*.

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Apply the Boussinesq gradient-transport hypothesis:

$$\overline{u'_i C'} = - \varepsilon_{t,i} \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x_i}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Substitute and drop $D_m$ (negligible in rivers):

$$\frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial t} + \bar{u}_i \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_i}\!\left( \varepsilon_{t,i} \frac{\partial \bar{C}}{\partial x_i} \right)$$

**This is the RACDE. (Total: 7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution A(b) ŌĆö Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Travel time to downstream end ($x = 5000\ \text{m}$):

$$t = \frac{x}{u} = \frac{5000}{0.4} = 12\,500\ \text{s} \approx 3.47\ \text{h}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Standard deviation of plume at that instant:

$$\sigma_x = \sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{tx} t} = \sqrt{2 \times 0.5 \times 12\,500} = \sqrt{12\,500} \approx 111.8\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Peak concentration (centre of Gaussian at $x = u t$):

$$C_{\text{peak}} = \frac{M}{A \sqrt{4 \pi \varepsilon_{tx} t}}$$

$$C_{\text{peak}} = \frac{20}{20 \times \sqrt{4 \pi \times 0.5 \times 12\,500}} = \frac{20}{20 \times 280.2} = 3.57 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg}/\text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Convert to mg/L:

$$C_{\text{peak}} = 3.57\ \text{mg}/\text{L}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Slugging/shear velocity estimate (for completeness, not asked, but useful):

$$u_\ast = \sqrt{g h S} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 2 \times 4 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.028\ \text{m}/\text{s}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Comment on validity ŌĆö since $u_\ast h / \varepsilon_{tz} \gg 1$ the flow is fully turbulent, and the Gaussian model is valid. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the **Reynolds analogy** between momentum, heat, and mass transfer in turbulent flow. Derive the relation $\varepsilon_t / \nu \approx 1 / \text{Sc}_t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using a parabolic eddy-diffusivity profile $\varepsilon_{tz}(z) = \kappa u_\ast z (1 - z/h)$, derive the **vertical concentration profile** $C(z)$ in a wide river with a uniform surface source (or uniform surface concentration $C_s$) and zero-flux at the bed. Show that $C(z)$ follows a **logarithmic** distribution. State clearly the boundary conditions. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution B(a) ŌĆö Reynolds Analogy (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Concept statement.
The Reynolds analogy rests on the principle that in fully turbulent flow, the *same eddies* transport momentum, heat, and mass. Therefore the eddy diffusivities of momentum ($\nu_t$), heat ($\alpha_t$), and mass ($\varepsilon_t$) are all of comparable magnitude.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the friction (shear) velocity $u_\ast = \sqrt{\tau_w / \rho}$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Express the turbulent fluxes:

- Momentum: $\tau_t / \rho = \nu_t \, d\bar{u}/dz$
- Mass: $\overline{u' C'} = - \varepsilon_t \, d\bar{C}/dz$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Define inner (wall) variables and non-dimensionalise. With $u^+ = \bar{u}/u_\ast$ and $C^+ = (C_w - \bar{C}) / C_\ast$:

$$\frac{d u^+}{d z^+} = \frac{d C^+}{d z^+}$$

Hence $u^+ = C^+ + \text{const}$. Differentiating and rearranging:

$$\frac{\varepsilon_t}{\nu} = \frac{1}{\text{Sc}_t}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Comment ŌĆö for gases $\text{Sc}_t \approx 0.85$, for liquids $\text{Sc}_t \approx 0.7$, often taken as unity in first-order engineering work. **(Total: 7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution B(b) ŌĆö Vertical Concentration Profile (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the governing 1-D vertical diffusion equation (steady, no advection in $z$):

$$\frac{d}{dz}\!\left( \varepsilon_{tz}(z) \frac{dC}{dz} \right) = 0$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State boundary conditions:
- At free surface $z = h$: $C(0)$ specified (or constant flux); we take $C = C_s$ at $z = h$.
- At bed $z = 0$: zero flux $\varepsilon_{tz} \, dC/dz = 0$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** First integration (constant flux):

$$\varepsilon_{tz}(z) \frac{dC}{dz} = \text{const} = q_0$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** With the parabolic profile $\varepsilon_{tz}(z) = \kappa u_\ast z (1 - z/h)$:

$$\frac{dC}{dz} = \frac{q_0}{\kappa u_\ast \, z (1 - z/h)}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** For the lower half of the flow where $z \ll h$, $1 - z/h \approx 1$:

$$\frac{dC}{dz} \approx \frac{q_0}{\kappa u_\ast z}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Integrate:

$$C(z) = \frac{q_0}{\kappa u_\ast} \ln(z) + \text{const}$$

**This is the logarithmic concentration profile ŌĆö analogous to the log-velocity law of PrandtlŌĆōvon K├Īrm├Īn. (Total: 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic**
>
> 1. **Mixing up $D_m$ and $\varepsilon_t$:** Many students substitute $D_m \approx 10^{-9}$ instead of $\varepsilon_t \approx 1$ in the Gaussian equation. Result: peak concentrations are off by **6ŌĆō9 orders of magnitude**. *Always confirm which diffusivity is in play.*
> 2. **Forgetting units of $t$ in $\sqrt{4\pi \varepsilon_{tx} t}$:** A common 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Not stating the closure hypothesis explicitly:** RACDE derivations are *incomplete* without writing the Boussinesq equation on the answer sheet. Examiners deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing $\text{Sc}_t$ with molecular $\text{Sc}$:** Molecular $\text{Sc} = \nu / D_m \sim 600$ (water). Turbulent $\text{Sc}_t = \nu_t / \varepsilon_t \sim 0.7$. They are completely different!
> 5. **Skipping boundary conditions:** In the log-profile derivation, omitting B.C.s (surface and bed) costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Sign errors in Gaussian exponent:** $(x - u t)^2$ is correct, $(u t - x)^2$ also valid (squared). But writing $-(x - u t)$ without the square leads to nonsense.
> 7. **Not using $\kappa = 0.41$ explicitly:** Examiners expect the von K├Īrm├Īn constant in log-law derivations.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist ŌĆö print and revise the night before the exam:**

- **Definition:** Turbulent diffusion = mass transport by chaotic eddies ŌĆö dominant in rivers since $\text{Re} \gg 2000$.
- **Master formula:** $J_t = - \varepsilon_t \, dC/dx$ (Fick's law, turbulent form).
- **Eddy diffusivity magnitudes** (typical river): $\varepsilon_{tz} \sim 10^{-3}$, $\varepsilon_{ty} \sim 10^{-2}$, $\varepsilon_{tx} \sim 1.0\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$.
- **Reynolds decomposition:** $C = \bar{C} + C'$; turbulent flux term $\overline{u' C'}$ is *closed* by Boussinesq hypothesis: $\overline{u' C'} = - \varepsilon_t \, d\bar{C}/dx$.
- **RACDE:** $\partial \bar{C}/\partial t + \bar{u}_i \, \partial \bar{C}/\partial x_i = \partial / \partial x_i (\varepsilon_{t,i} \, \partial \bar{C}/\partial x_i)$.
- **Gaussian plume (1-D moving frame):**

$$C(x,t) = \frac{M}{A\sqrt{4\pi \varepsilon_{tx} t}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(x - u t)^2}{4 \varepsilon_{tx} t}\right]$$

- **Taylor's shear-flow dispersion:** $K = a^2 U^2 / \overline{\varepsilon_{tz}}$.
- **Reynolds analogy:** $\varepsilon_t / \nu \approx 1 / \text{Sc}_t$, with $\text{Sc}_t \approx 0.7$ in rivers.
- **Parabolic $\varepsilon_t$ profile:** $\varepsilon_{tz}(z) = \kappa u_\ast z (1 - z/h)$, $\kappa = 0.41$.
- **Log-concentration profile (lower flow):** $C(z) \propto \ln z$ ŌĆö analogous to log-velocity law.
- **Peclet number** $\text{Pe} = u L / \varepsilon_{tx}$ ŌĆö large $\text{Pe}$ ŌćÆ advection dominates.
- **Engineering applications:** pollutant spill modelling, BOD/DO sag, sediment dispersion, dye-tracer calibration, mixing-zone design.
- **Exam mantra:** *"Diffusion in turbulent flow is eddy-driven, $\varepsilon_t \gg D_m$, modelled by RACDE, and solved analytically as a Gaussian plume."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Equation for Suspension of Sediment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET747 - RIVER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Differential Equation for Suspension of Sediment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **River Engineering**, the **Differential Equation for Suspension of Sediment** (commonly known as the **Diffusion Theory of Sediment Suspension** or the **Rouse Equation**) describes the vertical distribution of suspended sediment concentration in an open channel under steady, uniform, turbulent flow conditions.

The suspended load is transported in a river by a balance between two opposing physical mechanisms:
1. **Downward flux** caused by the gravitational settling of sediment particles at their **fall velocity (settling velocity)** $w_s$.
2. **Upward flux** caused by the **turbulent diffusion (eddy diffusion)** of sediment particles, governed by the **eddy diffusion coefficient** $\varepsilon_s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Sediment suspension occurs when the upward turbulent eddies are strong enough to counteract the gravitational settling of sediment particles. The concentration $C(y)$ decreases exponentially from the riverbed to the water surface.

At **dynamic equilibrium**, the net vertical flux of suspended sediment is zero, which leads to the classical differential equation:

$$\frac{dC}{dy} = -\frac{w_s \cdot C}{\varepsilon_s}$$

where:
- $C$ = sediment concentration at height $y$ above the bed (in kg/m┬│ or ppm by weight)
- $y$ = vertical distance from the riverbed (in m)
- $w_s$ = **settling velocity** of the sediment particle (in m/s)
- $\varepsilon_s$ = **eddy diffusivity (mass diffusion coefficient)** for sediment (in m┬▓/s)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: "The Dusty Room"**
>
> Imagine a room filled with smoke (sediment particles) being slowly pulled downward by gravity. A ceiling fan (turbulent eddies) keeps pushing the smoke particles upward. At equilibrium, a steady, vertical "layered" cloud of smoke exists: very dense near the floor and very thin near the ceiling.
>
> The **heavier the dust particle** (higher $w_s$) ŌåÆ the faster it sinks ŌåÆ it stays closer to the floor.
> The **stronger the fan turbulence** (higher $\varepsilon_s$ or higher $u_*$) ŌåÆ the more it lifts ŌåÆ the more uniform the distribution.

In a river:
- **Bed load** rolls/slides along the bed (heavy particles, low turbulence).
- **Suspended load** is the "smoke cloud" carried by turbulent eddies in the flow.
- **Wash load** consists of extremely fine particles (negligible $w_s$) that stay almost uniformly distributed throughout the depth.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants Used

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\kappa$ | **von K├Īrm├Īn constant** | **0.41** (dimensionless) |
| $u_*$ | **Shear velocity** | $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0/\rho}$ (m/s) |
| $\rho$ | **Density of water** | **1000 kg/m┬│** |
| $\rho_s$ | **Density of sediment** | typically **2650 kg/m┬│** |
| $g$ | **Acceleration due to gravity** | **9.81 m/s┬▓** |
| $\nu$ | **Kinematic viscosity of water** | $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m┬▓/s at 20┬░C |

## 1.4 The Rouse Number (Crucial KTU Parameter)

The non-dimensional exponent governing the shape of the concentration profile is the **Rouse Number**, denoted $Z$:

$$Z = \frac{w_s}{\kappa \cdot u_*}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Rouse Number $Z$ is the **single most important parameter** in sediment suspension problems. It dictates whether a particle of given $w_s$ will be carried in suspension, partly in suspension, or remain as bed load.

| Rouse Number $Z$ | Suspension Behavior |
|---|---|
| $Z < 0.0625$ | **Uniform suspension** (very fine particles like wash load) |
| $0.0625 \le Z \le 0.25$ | **Most particles in suspension** |
| $0.25 < Z \le 1.0$ | **Significant fraction in suspension** |
| $1.0 < Z \le 3.0$ | **Limited suspension** (only fine fraction suspended) |
| $Z > 3.0$ | **Practically no suspension** (moves as bed load) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Assumptions

The diffusion theory (Rouse, 1937) rests upon the following idealized conditions, which must be stated in any KTU board answer:

1. **Steady, uniform flow** in a wide, rectangular channel.
2. **Two-dimensional flow** (no transverse variation; parameters vary only with $y$).
3. **Turbulent diffusion is the sole mixing mechanism** (molecular diffusion neglected).
4. **Sediment particles are uniform in size, shape, and density** (no interaction between particles).
5. **The mass diffusion coefficient $\varepsilon_s$ equals the momentum exchange coefficient $\varepsilon_m$** (this is the **Reynolds analogy** for sediment).
6. **The logarithmic velocity distribution (Prandtl-von K├Īrm├Īn)** holds throughout the depth.
7. **Sediment concentration is dilute** (no hindered settling effects).
8. **No net deposition or erosion** at the bed (equilibrium suspension).

## 2.2 The Prandtl Mixing Length Theory Connection

From **Prandtl's mixing length theory**, the momentum exchange (eddy viscosity) in open-channel flow varies linearly with distance from the bed up to the water surface:

$$\varepsilon_m = \kappa \cdot u_* \cdot y \cdot \left(1 - \frac{y}{h}\right)$$

- At the bed ($y = 0$): $\varepsilon_m = 0$ (no turbulent mixing at the rigid boundary).
- At the surface ($y = h$): $\varepsilon_m = 0$ (no turbulent mixing at the free surface).
- Maximum value occurs at **$y = h/2$** (mid-depth).

## 2.3 The Rouse Equation (Final Integrated Form)

After integration of the differential equation with proper boundary conditions (covered in Section 3), the classic **Rouse Vertical Distribution Equation** is obtained:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C}{C_a} = \left[\frac{\left(\dfrac{y}{h}\right) \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{a}{h}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{a}{h}\right) \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)}\right]^{\,Z}\;}$$

or equivalently:

$$C = C_a \left[\frac{h - y}{y} \cdot \frac{a}{h - a}\right]^{\,Z}, \quad \text{where} \quad Z = \frac{w_s}{\kappa \cdot u_*}$$

Here, $C_a$ is the **reference concentration** at the **reference level $a$** measured from the bed (typically $a = 0.05h$ or $a = 2d_{50}$ to $a = 5d_{50}$).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles **all high-frequency formulas** for the topic "Differential Equation for Suspension of Sediment" as per KTU 2024 Module 4 expectations.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w_s \cdot C + \varepsilon_s \dfrac{dC}{dy} = 0$ | Governing differential equation (flux balance) | ŌĆö |
| 2 | $C/C_a = \left[\dfrac{(y/h)(1-a/h)}{(a/h)(1-y/h)}\right]^{Z}$ | Rouse concentration distribution | ŌĆö |
| 3 | $Z = \dfrac{w_s}{\kappa \cdot u_*}$ | **Rouse Number** (suspension index) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $u_* = \sqrt{g \cdot R \cdot S}$ | Shear velocity from energy slope | m/s |
| 5 | $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_0 / \rho}$ | Shear velocity from bed shear stress | m/s |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_m = \kappa \cdot u_* \cdot y \cdot (1 - y/h)$ | Momentum exchange coefficient (parabolic) | m┬▓/s |
| 7 | $w_s = \dfrac{(\rho_s - \rho) \cdot g \cdot d^2}{18 \cdot \mu}$ (Stokes' law, $Re_p < 1$) | Settling velocity (laminar) | m/s |
| 8 | $w_s = 1.1 \sqrt{(\rho_s/\rho - 1) \cdot g \cdot d}$ (Turton & Levenspiel) | Settling velocity (turbulent) | m/s |
| 9 | $C_a \approx \dfrac{0.018 \cdot S \cdot (u_*^2 / g \cdot d_{50})}{(C/C_a)}$ | Reference concentration (van Rijn, 1984) | kg/m┬│ |
| 10 | $\tau_0 = \gamma \cdot R \cdot S$ | Bed shear stress | N/m┬▓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Rule for KTU Answers:** Always clearly distinguish the **reference level $a$** (where $C = C_a$ is known) from the **arbitrary elevation $y$** (where $C$ is to be found). A common valuation pitfall is interchanging these two variables.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reservoir Sedimentation Studies:** Predicting the vertical distribution of silt in reservoirs to estimate trap efficiency and useful life.
- **Design of Intakes and Outlets:** Sizing the inlet elevation of water supply intakes to avoid drawing in turbid water.
- **Navigational Channel Design:** Predicting where shoaling will occur due to sediment deposition.
- **Bridge Scour and Pier Design:** Estimating the suspended sediment transport rate contributing to scour.
- **Estuarine and Coastal Engineering:** Predicting sediment behavior in tidal flows where $w_s$ competes with turbulent eddies.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Modeling pollutant-attached fine sediment transport in rivers.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Rouse Equation

This derivation is the **core analytical content** of the KTU Module 4 syllabus. Every step below is expected to be reproducible in the university examination for full marks.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Define the Two Opposing Sediment Fluxes

Consider a small horizontal control volume in the river of unit area, located at a height $y$ from the bed.

**Downward flux** of sediment due to gravity (settling of particles):
$$F_{down} = w_s \cdot C(y)$$

**Upward flux** of sediment due to turbulent diffusion (analogous to Fick's law applied to mass):
$$F_{up} = -\varepsilon_s \cdot \frac{dC}{dy}$$

> The negative sign appears because sediment diffuses from **higher concentration (near the bed)** to **lower concentration (near the surface)**, i.e., in the direction of decreasing $C$. With $y$ increasing upward, $\dfrac{dC}{dy} < 0$, making $F_{up} > 0$ (upward).

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Equilibrium (Continuity) Condition

Under **dynamic equilibrium**, the net vertical flux must be zero, i.e., the sediment that settles down is exactly replaced by the sediment that diffuses upward. Therefore:

$$F_{down} + F_{up} = 0$$

Substituting the flux expressions:

$$w_s \cdot C - \varepsilon_s \cdot \frac{dC}{dy} = 0$$

Rearranging to obtain the **governing differential equation of sediment suspension**:

$$\frac{dC}{dy} = -\frac{w_s \cdot C}{\varepsilon_s}$$

This is the formal answer to "State the differential equation for the suspension of sediment."

### Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Reynolds Analogy (Rouse Assumption)

A critical assumption by Rouse (1937) is that the **eddy diffusivity for sediment mass equals the eddy diffusivity for momentum**:

$$\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_m$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, $\varepsilon_s$ is often taken as $\beta \cdot \varepsilon_m$, where $\beta \approx 0.6$ to $1.0$ depending on sediment size. KTU problems typically assume $\beta = 1$.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute the Prandtl-von K├Īrm├Īn Distribution for $\varepsilon_m$

From the logarithmic velocity profile in open-channel flow, the momentum exchange coefficient follows the **parabolic distribution**:

$$\varepsilon_m(y) = \kappa \cdot u_* \cdot y \cdot \left(1 - \frac{y}{h}\right)$$

Substituting this into the governing equation:

$$w_s \cdot C = \kappa \cdot u_* \cdot y \cdot \left(1 - \frac{y}{h}\right) \cdot \frac{dC}{dy}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Separate the Variables

Dividing both sides by $C$ and by the eddy viscosity term:

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -\frac{w_s}{\kappa \cdot u_* \cdot y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)} \, dy$$

Define the Rouse number for convenience:

$$Z = \frac{w_s}{\kappa \cdot u_*}$$

The equation becomes:

$$\frac{dC}{C} = -\frac{Z \, dy}{y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the Boundary Conditions

Integrate from the **reference level $y = a$** (where concentration is $C = C_a$) to an **arbitrary level $y$** (where concentration is $C$):

$$\int_{C_a}^{C} \frac{dC}{C} = -Z \int_{a}^{y} \frac{dy}{y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)}$$

The left side evaluates simply:

$$\ln\left(\frac{C}{C_a}\right) = -Z \int_{a}^{y} \frac{dy}{y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)}$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Evaluate the Integral Using Partial Fractions

Decompose the integrand using partial fractions. We want constants $A$ and $B$ such that:

$$\frac{1}{y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)} = \frac{A}{y} + \frac{B}{1 - \dfrac{y}{h}}$$

Multiplying both sides by $y \cdot (1 - y/h)$:

$$1 = A \cdot \left(1 - \frac{y}{h}\right) + B \cdot y$$

Setting $y = 0$: $\quad 1 = A \cdot 1 \;\Rightarrow\; A = 1$

Setting $y = h$: $\quad 1 = B \cdot h \;\Rightarrow\; B = 1/h$

Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)} = \frac{1}{y} + \frac{1}{h \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)}$$

### Step 8 ŌĆö Perform the Integration

Now evaluate the integral:

$$\int \frac{dy}{y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)} = \int \frac{dy}{y} + \int \frac{dy}{h \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)}$$

The first integral: $\displaystyle\int \frac{dy}{y} = \ln y$

The second integral: substitute $u = 1 - y/h$, so $du = -dy/h$:

$$\int \frac{dy}{h \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)} = -\int \frac{du}{u} = -\ln\left(1 - \frac{y}{h}\right)$$

Combining:

$$\int \frac{dy}{y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)} = \ln y - \ln\left(1 - \frac{y}{h}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{y}{1 - \dfrac{y}{h}}\right)$$

### Step 9 ŌĆö Apply the Definite Integration Limits

Substituting the limits $a$ to $y$:

$$\int_{a}^{y} \frac{dy}{y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)} = \ln\left(\frac{y}{1 - \dfrac{y}{h}}\right) - \ln\left(\frac{a}{1 - \dfrac{a}{h}}\right) = \ln\left[\frac{y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{a}{h}\right)}{a \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)}\right]$$

### Step 10 ŌĆö Combine to Obtain the Rouse Equation

Returning to the integrated flux equation:

$$\ln\left(\frac{C}{C_a}\right) = -Z \cdot \ln\left[\frac{y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{a}{h}\right)}{a \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)}\right] = \ln\left[\frac{y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{a}{h}\right)}{a \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)}\right]^{-Z}$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\frac{C}{C_a} = \left[\frac{y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{a}{h}\right)}{a \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)}\right]^{-Z} = \left[\frac{a \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)}{y \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{a}{h}\right)}\right]^{Z}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rouse Equation (Canonical Form):**
>
> $$\boxed{\;\frac{C}{C_a} = \left[\frac{\left(\dfrac{a}{h}\right) \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{h}\right)}{\left(1 - \dfrac{a}{h}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{y}{h}\right)}\right]^{Z}\;} \quad \text{where} \quad Z = \frac{w_s}{\kappa \cdot u_*}$$
>
> This is the **most important equation** in Module 4 of PECET747. The concentration decays rapidly with height when $Z$ is large (heavy particles) and is nearly uniform when $Z$ is small (very fine particles).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A wide river has a depth of $h = 4.0$ m. The shear velocity is $u_* = 0.08$ m/s. The reference level is $a = 0.2$ m, with a reference concentration $C_a = 5000$ ppm. A sediment particle has settling velocity $w_s = 0.012$ m/s. Using $\kappa = 0.41$, find the suspended sediment concentration at depths $y = 0.4$ m, $1.0$ m, and $2.0$ m from the bed.

**Solution:**

**Step A:** Calculate the Rouse number.

$$Z = \frac{w_s}{\kappa \cdot u_*} = \frac{0.012}{0.41 \times 0.08} = \frac{0.012}{0.0328} = 0.366$$

**Step B:** Compute the constant reference bracket term.

$$\left(\frac{a}{h}\right) = \frac{0.2}{4.0} = 0.05 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(1 - \frac{a}{h}\right) = 0.95$$

So the denominator constant is $\left(\dfrac{a/h}{1 - a/h}\right) = \dfrac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.0526$.

**Step C:** Apply the Rouse equation for each depth.

$$\frac{C}{C_a} = \left[\frac{(a/h)(1 - y/h)}{y/h \cdot (1 - a/h)}\right]^{0.366} = \left[\frac{0.05 \cdot (1 - y/4)}{0.95 \cdot (y/4)}\right]^{0.366}$$

**At $y = 0.4$ m** (very near bed):
- $y/h = 0.1$, $1 - y/h = 0.9$
- Bracket = $(0.05 \times 0.9) / (0.95 \times 0.1) = 0.045 / 0.095 = 0.4737$
- $C/C_a = (0.4737)^{0.366} = 0.732$
- $C = 5000 \times 0.732 = 3660$ ppm

**At $y = 1.0$ m**:
- $y/h = 0.25$, $1 - y/h = 0.75$
- Bracket = $(0.05 \times 0.75) / (0.95 \times 0.25) = 0.0375 / 0.2375 = 0.1579$
- $C/C_a = (0.1579)^{0.366} = 0.503$
- $C = 5000 \times 0.503 = 2515$ ppm

**At $y = 2.0$ m** (mid-depth):
- $y/h = 0.5$, $1 - y/h = 0.5$
- Bracket = $(0.05 \times 0.5) / (0.95 \times 0.5) = 0.025 / 0.475 = 0.0526$
- $C/C_a = (0.0526)^{0.366} = 0.310$
- $C = 5000 \times 0.310 = 1550$ ppm

**Interpretation:** Concentration falls from 5000 ppm at the reference level to about 1550 ppm at mid-depth ŌĆö a 69% drop in only half the depth, because $Z = 0.366$ corresponds to "significant suspension" regime.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Behavior at Limiting Cases

| Limit | Mathematical Limit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $Z \to 0$ | $C/C_a \to 1$ for all $y$ | **Wash load** ŌĆö uniform concentration (no settling) |
| $Z \to \infty$ | $C/C_a \to 0$ for $y > a$ | All sediment stays at bed; **bed load only** |
| $y = a$ | $C/C_a = 1$ (definition) | Reference level ŌĆö concentration $C_a$ |
| $y = h$ | $C/C_a = 0$ (theoretically) | Concentration vanishes at water surface |
| $y = h/2$ | Maximum of $\varepsilon_m$ | Maximum turbulent mixing capacity |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Suspended Sediment Diffusion Mechanism

The following Mermaid diagram provides a **block-level functional architecture** of the diffusion theory of sediment suspension, mapping all interacting components of the physical system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[River Flow with Turbulent Eddies] --> B[Turbulent Diffusion Process]
    C[Settling Force due to Gravity] --> D[Sediment Particle Behavior]
    B --> E[Upward Sediment Flux F_up]
    D --> F[Downward Sediment Flux F_down]
    E --> G[Flux Balance at Equilibrium]
    F --> G
    G --> H[F_down + F_up = 0]
    H --> I[Governing Differential Equation]
    I --> J[w_s C + epsilon_s dC/dy = 0]
    J --> K[Substitute Parabolic epsilon_m]
    K --> L[Separate Variables and Integrate]
    L --> M[Apply Boundary Conditions C_a at y=a]
    M --> N[Rouse Equation Final Form]
    N --> O{Compute Rouse Number Z}
    O --> P[Z less than 0.0625 Uniform Wash Load]
    O --> Q[Z between 0.25 and 1 Significant Suspension]
    O --> R[Z greater than 3 No Suspension Bed Load]
    P --> S[Design Intake at Any Depth]
    Q --> T[Predict Reservoir Sedimentation]
    R --> U[Design Scour Protection at Bed]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Vertical Concentration Profile Decay

The following diagram conceptually illustrates the **vertical concentration distribution** governed by the Rouse equation for varying Rouse numbers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Reference Level a near Bed] --> M1[Concentration C_a Maximum]
    L1 --> M2[Mid Depth y = h/2 Concentration Falls]
    M1 --> M3[Surface y = h Concentration Approaches Zero]
    M2 --> M3
    M1 --> N1[Curve for Z small Near Vertical Uniform]
    M1 --> N2[Curve for Z moderate Exponential Decay]
    M1 --> N3[Curve for Z large Steep Drop near Bed]
    N1 --> O1[Application Wash Load Transport]
    N2 --> O2[Application Standard Suspended Load]
    N3 --> O3[Application Bed Material Transport]
```
