## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
*What is a Helley-Smith sampler? Briefly describe its working principle and mention its hydraulic efficiency for sand particles.*

**Model Answer:**

The Helley-Smith sampler (US BL-84) is a **pressure-difference type bed-load sampler** developed by Helley and Smith in 1971. It has a square nozzle of **7.62 cm ├Ś 7.62 cm (3 inch ├Ś 3 inch)** followed by an expanding diffuser with expansion ratio **1.375:1**. A mesh sample bag (0.25 mm) is fitted at the rear.

**Working principle:** The sampler is placed flush on the streambed. Bed-load particles (rolling, sliding, saltating sand grains) enter the nozzle by gravity and bed shear. The diffuser reduces the velocity, causing sediment to drop into the sample bag. The slight negative pressure inside (due to diffuser expansion) actively *sucks* bed load in.

**Hydraulic efficiency:** Ōēł **100% (unity)** for sand with **d Ōēł 0.5 mm in flows of ~1 m/s**. Efficiency drops for finer particles (d < 0.2 mm) and coarser particles (d > 16 mm) which may choke the nozzle. Emmett's (1980) calibration curves are used for correction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
*Explain the "isokinetic" condition in suspended-load sampling. Why is the transit rate of a US D-74 sampler kept at 0.4 times the depth-averaged velocity?*

**Model Answer:**

**Isokinetic condition:** The velocity at the inlet of the suspended-load sampler must equal the velocity of the stream at the same point. Only then does the sampler extract a *representative* water-sediment mixture without disturbing the local flow field. If the inlet velocity is less, fine particles are *sucked in disproportionately* (oversampling of fines); if it is greater, fines are *deflected* (undersampling).

**Why transit rate = 0.4 ┼½:** A US D-74 is moved from the surface to the bed and back at a constant speed. If the sampler moves as fast as the water (v_t = ┼½), the relative velocity between water and inlet is **zero**, and the sampler does not draw additional water. However, this causes the inlet velocity to be **higher** than the local stream velocity (because the water in the bottle is *moving with the sampler*). To compensate, the transit rate is reduced to **0.4 ┼½**, which makes the inlet velocity **equal** to the local stream velocity ŌĆö achieving isokinetic sampling. The factor 0.4 was established empirically by the USGS from flume tests.

---

### 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **construction, working, and efficiency characteristics** of the **Helley-Smith bed load sampler**. With a neat diagram, explain the role of the expanding diffuser.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Helley-Smith sampler (nozzle width **b = 7.62 cm**) is held on the bed for **t = 90 s**. The dry mass of sand collected is **W_b = 6.75 kg**. Median particle size **d_50 = 0.50 mm**. Compute (i) the unit bed-load transport rate $q_b$, (ii) the volumetric transport rate per unit width $q_{bv}$, and (iii) the daily bed-load transport per unit width in tonnes.

---

#### **Model Answer to Question A:**

**(a) Construction, Working, and Efficiency of the Helley-Smith Sampler [7 Marks]:**

**Construction:**

- **Body:** Cast aluminium or bronze; weight Ōēł 4 kg.
- **Nozzle:** Square opening, **7.62 cm ├Ś 7.62 cm** at the upstream end. Sides are parallel (no contraction).
- **Diffuser:** Internal expanding duct, **expansion ratio 1.375:1** over a length of 30 cm. The walls diverge at a small angle (~6┬░).
- **Sample bag:** Standard monofilament mesh, **0.25 mm aperture** (US BL-84) or **0.0625 mm** for fine sediment work.
- **Tail piece:** A short exit vent prevents air lock and releases excess water.
- **Handle / rod:** A wading rod is used for shallows; a cable for deeper water.

**Working:**

1. The nozzle is placed **flush** with the bed (no gap, no tilt).
2. Bed load enters the nozzle under the **combined action of gravity and bed shear**.
3. Inside the diffuser, the **cross-section expands**, so the velocity drops.
4. The reduction in velocity causes sediment to **drop out of flow** and settle into the sample bag.
5. Water passes through the mesh and exits via the tail vent.
6. After a measured time *t* (typically 30 s to 5 min), the sampler is lifted; the bag is removed; the sediment is dried and weighed.

**Role of the Diffuser:**

The diffuser creates a **slight negative pressure** at the nozzle entrance. The expansion ratio 1.375:1 is empirically optimized: too little expansion ŌåÆ insufficient suction ŌåÆ low efficiency for fine particles; too much expansion ŌåÆ flow separation and back-eddies ŌåÆ sediment lost from the bag. The 1.375 ratio was found by Helley and Smith to give a **hydraulic efficiency of ~150%** (over-sampling) for fine particles and **~100%** for 0.5 mm sand. The result is corrected using Emmett's (1980) calibration tables.

**Efficiency Characteristics:**

| Particle Size d | Efficiency ╬Ę |
|---|---|
| > 0.5 mm | Ōēł 100% |
| 0.25 ŌĆō 0.50 mm | 90 ŌĆō 100% |
| 0.10 ŌĆō 0.25 mm | 60 ŌĆō 90% (correction needed) |
| < 0.10 mm | < 60% (not reliable; use suspended sampler) |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** b = 7.62 cm = 0.0762 m; t = 90 s; W_b = 6.75 kg; d_50 = 0.50 mm; Žü_s = 2650 kg/m┬│.

**(i) Unit bed-load transport rate:**

$$q_b = \frac{W_b}{b \cdot t} = \frac{6.75}{0.0762 \times 90} = \frac{6.75}{6.858} = 0.9843 \text{ kg/(m┬Ęs)}$$

**(ii) Volumetric transport rate:**

$$q_{bv} = \frac{q_b}{\rho_s} = \frac{0.9843}{2650} = 3.715 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{(m┬Ęs)}$$

**(iii) Daily transport per unit width:**

$$q_{b,day} = q_b \times 86400 \text{ s/day} = 0.9843 \times 86400 = 85.04 \times 10^{3} \text{ kg/(m┬Ęday)}$$

$$= 85.04 \text{ tonnes/(m┬Ęday)}$$

> **Answer:** $q_b = 0.984$ kg/(m┬Ęs); $q_{bv} = 3.72 \times 10^{-4}$ m┬│/(m┬Ęs); $q_{b,day} = 85.04$ tonnes/(m┬Ęday).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **US D-74 depth-integrating suspended sediment sampler** with a sketch. Explain the **transit method** of operation and the concept of **equal transit rate (ETR)**. Why is the inlet coefficient of US D-74 designed to be **1.0**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A US D-74 sample (bottle volume **V = 0.5 L**) collected from a vertical of depth **h = 1.8 m** at transit rate **v_t = 0.5 m/s** yields dry sediment mass **W_s = 0.85 g**. The depth-averaged velocity is $\bar{u}$ = 1.20 m/s. The stream discharge at that vertical is **q = 2.4 m┬▓/s per unit width**. Compute (i) the depth-averaged suspended sediment concentration $\bar{C}$, (ii) the suspended sediment discharge per unit width $q_s$, and (iii) verify whether the transit rate satisfies the ETR criterion (k = 0.4).

---

#### **Model Answer to Question B:**

**(a) US D-74 Description [7 Marks]:**

The US D-74 is a **depth-integrating suspended sediment sampler** developed by the U.S. Geological Survey. It is the world standard for suspended-load measurement in rivers.

**Construction:**

- **Body:** Cast bronze, streamlined, weight Ōēł 27 kg.
- **Intake nozzle:** Standard diameter **4.7625 mm (3/16 inch)**; optional **6.35 mm** and **9.525 mm** for high-sediment flows.
- **Internal cavity:** Houses a **0.5 L polyethylene bottle** (US D-74) or glass bottle (US D-74A).
- **Air vent at top:** The bottle mouth is open to atmosphere via a small vent, ensuring the bottle fills **from the bottom up** as air escapes from the top.
- **Tail fin:** Keeps the sampler oriented into the flow.
- **Hollow stem:** Connects to a suspension cable or wading rod.

**Transit Method of Operation:**

1. With the bottle **empty and capped vent open**, the sampler is **lowered at constant speed** from the water surface to within a few cm of the bed.
2. It is **immediately raised** at the same speed back to the surface.
3. The constant speed is the **transit rate** $v_t$.
4. The **cumulative volume** drawn is the product of intake area, $v_t$, and total time, i.e., the sampler *integrates* the suspended concentration over the entire depth.

**Equal Transit Rate (ETR) Criterion:**

The transit rate is chosen so that the inlet velocity equals the local stream velocity. Empirically:

$$v_t = k \cdot \bar{u}, \quad k = 0.4 \text{ (standard)}$$

**Why inlet coefficient = 1.0:**

The inlet coefficient $C_i$ is defined as:

$$C_i = \frac{V_{inlet}}{V_{stream}}$$

A value of **1.0** means the inlet velocity **exactly matches** the stream velocity ŌĆö i.e., isokinetic. This is achieved by careful nozzle design (sharp-edged orifice, no contraction) and by using the ETR criterion. Isokinetic sampling prevents:
- **Oversampling of fines** (if $C_i < 1$)
- **Undersampling of fines** (if $C_i > 1$)
- **Bias in coarse particles** (negligible ŌĆö they enter by inertia regardless)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** V = 0.5 L = 5 ├Ś 10Ōü╗Ōü┤ m┬│; h = 1.8 m; v_t = 0.5 m/s; W_s = 0.85 g = 8.5 ├Ś 10Ōü╗Ōü┤ kg; $\bar{u}$ = 1.20 m/s; q = 2.4 m┬▓/s per unit width.

**(i) Depth-averaged concentration:**

$$\bar{C} = \frac{W_s}{V} = \frac{8.5 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.70 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(ii) Suspended sediment discharge per unit width:**

$$q_s = \bar{C} \cdot q = 1.70 \times 2.4 = 4.08 \text{ kg/(m┬Ęs)}$$

Convert to tonnes per day:

$$q_{s,day} = 4.08 \times 86400 = 352,512 \text{ kg/(m┬Ęday)} = 352.5 \text{ tonnes/(m┬Ęday)}$$

**(iii) Verify ETR criterion:**

The ETR transit rate should be:

$$v_{t,ETR} = 0.4 \times \bar{u} = 0.4 \times 1.20 = 0.48 \text{ m/s}$$

The actual transit rate used is $v_t$ = 0.50 m/s.

Percent deviation:

$$\Delta v = \frac{0.50 - 0.48}{0.48} \times 100 = +4.17\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The transit rate **exceeds** the ETR value by ~4.2%, which is within the **acceptable field tolerance of ┬▒10%** recommended by the USGS. The sampling is therefore considered **acceptable**, but for publication-grade work, the recommended transit rate of 0.48 m/s should be used.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** ŌĆö most students lose 1ŌĆō2 marks for mixing cm and m. The nozzle width **b = 7.62 cm MUST be converted to 0.0762 m** before substituting.
> 2. **Confusing W_b (total mass) with q_b (rate)** ŌĆö q_b is mass per unit width per unit TIME, not just total mass.
> 3. **Omitting the efficiency correction** ŌĆö for d_50 < 0.5 mm, the Helley-Smith efficiency drops. State the assumption ╬Ę = 100% explicitly OR apply Emmett's correction.
> 4. **Using the wrong transit rate factor** ŌĆö k = 0.4 is for ETR; some texts (especially older ones) use 0.5. State your choice.
> 5. **Not drawing the sketch** ŌĆö for "with a neat diagram" sub-questions, **2 marks are reserved for the diagram**. Even a rough sketch earns 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing transit rate (v_t) with stream velocity (u)** ŌĆö they are different quantities. v_t = 0.4 ┼½, NOT equal to ┼½.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Sediment Samplers (Module 4)**

### Bed-Load Samplers
- Ō£ģ **Helley-Smith (US BL-84):** Square 7.62 cm nozzle, 1.375 diffuser, mesh bag. Efficiency Ōēł 100% for 0.5 mm sand. The KTU favourite.
- Ō£ģ **Arnhem Sampler:** Dutch design, 50 cm square nozzle, settling chamber. Lower efficiency.
- Ō£ģ **Pit / Trap Sampler:** Long-term continuous measurement, no live reading.
- Ō£ģ **Bed load transport rate:** $q_b = W_b / (b \cdot t)$, units **kg/(m┬Ęs)**.
- Ō£ģ Apply **Emmett's (1980) efficiency correction** for d < 0.5 mm.

### Suspended-Load Samplers
- Ō£ģ **US D-74:** Depth-integrating, round 4.7625 mm nozzle, 0.5 L bottle, bottom-up filling. World standard.
- Ō£ģ **USDH-48:** Combined velocity + sediment sampler for deep rivers.
- Ō£ģ **US P-61:** Point-integrating, sealed bottle, for **vertical profile** C(y) studies.
- Ō£ģ **Isokinetic principle:** Inlet velocity = stream velocity. Avoids bias.
- Ō£ģ **Transit rate:** $v_t = 0.4 \bar{u}$ (ETR method).
- Ō£ģ **Concentration:** $\bar{C} = W_s / V$ (kg/m┬│ or mg/L). Conversion: **1 kg/m┬│ = 10ŌüČ mg/L** (since 1 m┬│ = 10ŌüČ mL = 10ŌüČ ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│ L = 10┬│ L, so 1 kg/m┬│ = 1000 g/1000 L = 1 g/L = 1000 mg/L).
- Ō£ģ **Sediment discharge:** $Q_s = \bar{C} \cdot Q$ (kg/s).

### Cross-Cutting Concepts
- Ō£ģ **Rouse profile:** $C_y/C_a = [(y/y_a)(h-y_a)/(h-y)]^Z$, $Z = \omega/(\kappa u_*)$.
- Ō£ģ **Shear velocity:** $u_* = \sqrt{gRS}$ (uniform flow).
- Ō£ģ **Fall velocity (Rubey):** $\omega = \sqrt{(4/3)(C_D/C_D')(G_s-1)gd}$.
- Ō£ģ **KTU favourite constants:** $G_s = 2.65$, $\rho_s = 2650$ kg/m┬│, $\kappa = 0.41$ (clear) or 0.30 (sediment-laden), k = 0.4 (ETR).
- Ō£ģ **Nozzle design rule:** nozzle size > 2.5 ├Ś d_84 to prevent choking.
- Ō£ģ **Bed-load zone:** bottom 0 to 0.05 h; **Suspended-load zone:** 0.05 h to surface.

### Final Formula Master List (Memorize for KTU)
1. $q_b = W_b / (b \cdot t)$
2. $Q_b = q_b \cdot B$
3. $v_t = 0.4 \bar{u}$
4. $\bar{C} = W_s / V$
5. $Q_s = \bar{C} \cdot Q$
6. $u_* = \sqrt{gRS}$
7. $Z = \omega / (\kappa u_*)$
8. $C_y / C_a = [(y/y_a)(h-y_a)/(h-y)]^Z$
9. $q_{s,day} = q_s \times 86400$ (for unit conversions)
10. $q_{b,corrected} = q_{b,uncorrected} / \eta$

> ­¤Ä» **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** In any 14-mark question on samplers, ALWAYS start with a **labelled sketch** (2 marks), then write the **principle** (2 marks), **construction** (2ŌĆō3 marks), **working** (2ŌĆō3 marks), **numerical** (3ŌĆō4 marks). This is the standard KTU evaluation breakdown.
